events.name: Praetorian Guard Auction of the Throne (193)
events.summary: In 193, the praetorian guard auctioned off the office of emperor to the highest bidder, then killed the lucky winner 66 days later when he couldn't live up to his promises. Brownworth cites this as the starkest example of how the army had discovered the dangerous secret of empire.

2.summary: In 193, the praetorian guard auctioned off the office of emperor to the highest bidder, then killed the lucky winner 66 days later when he couldn't live up to his promises. Brownworth cites this as the starkest example of how the army had discovered the dangerous secret of empire.

events.wikipedia_extract: Marcus Didius Julianus was Roman emperor from March to June 193, during the Year of the Five Emperors.

events.name: Crisis of the Third Century
events.summary: The 50-year period between 235 and 285 that Brownworth calls the low point of Roman history, with emperors lasting an average of two years each. A 50-year-old in 285 would have known 27 legitimate emperors, numerous pretenders, and at least one breakaway empire.

2.summary: The 50-year period between 235 and 285 that Brownworth calls the low point of Roman history, with emperors lasting an average of two years each. A 50-year-old in 285 would have known 27 legitimate emperors, numerous pretenders, and at least one breakaway empire.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Crisis of the Third Century, also known as the Military Anarchy or the Imperial Crisis, was a period in Roman history during which the Roman Empire nearly collapsed under the combined pressure of repeated foreign invasions, civil wars and economic disintegration. At the height of the crisis, the Roman state split into three distinct and competing polities. The period is usually dated between the death of Severus Alexander (235) and accession of Diocletian (284).

events.name: Roman–Persian War (283)
events.summary: Carus's eastern campaign that successfully sacked the Persian capital but ended abruptly on the banks of the Tigris when Carus died — officially by lightning, but Brownworth implies assassination by Diocletian. The army then began a long withdrawal through Asia Minor under Numerian.

2.summary: Carus's eastern campaign that successfully sacked the Persian capital but ended abruptly on the banks of the Tigris when Carus died — officially by lightning, but Brownworth implies assassination by Diocletian. The army then began a long withdrawal through Asia Minor under Numerian.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Roman–Persian wars, also called the Roman–Iranian wars, took place between the Greco-Roman world and the Iranian world, beginning with the Roman Republic and the Parthian Empire in 54 BC and ending with the Byzantine and the Sasanian empires in 628 AD. While the conflict between the two civilizations did involve direct military engagements, a significant role was played by a plethora of vassal kingdoms and allied nomadic tribes, which served as buffer states or proxies for either side. Despite nearly seven centuries of hostility, the Roman–Persian wars had an entirely inconclusive outcome, as both the Byzantines and the Sasanians were attacked by the Rashidun Caliphate as part of the early Muslim conquests. The Rashidun offensives resulted in the collapse of the Sasanian Empire and largely confined the Byzantine Empire to Anatolia and southeastern Europe for the ensuing Arab–Byzantine wars.

events.name: Death of Numerian
events.summary: Numerian, traveling in a closed carriage due to an eye infection, was found dead and partially decomposed when the army reached Nicomedia. Diocletian immediately accused Aper, publicly executed him on the spot, and was acclaimed emperor by the troops.

2.summary: Numerian, traveling in a closed carriage due to an eye infection, was found dead and partially decomposed when the army reached Nicomedia. Diocletian immediately accused Aper, publicly executed him on the spot, and was acclaimed emperor by the troops.

events.wikipedia_extract: Numerian was Roman emperor from 283 to 284 with his older brother Carinus. They were sons of Carus, a general raised to the office of praetorian prefect under Emperor Probus in 282.

events.name: Diocletian's Divine Association (Jovian identity)
events.summary: Diocletian announced that he was the son of Jupiter, requiring all citizens to sacrifice to him and making assassination tantamount to sacrilege. He assigned Maximian to the junior deity Hercules to reinforce his own supremacy, and this policy brought him into direct conflict with Christians who refused to worship him.

2.summary: Diocletian announced that he was the son of Jupiter, requiring all citizens to sacrifice to him and making assassination tantamount to sacrilege. He assigned Maximian to the junior deity Hercules to reinforce his own supremacy, and this policy brought him into direct conflict with Christians who refused to worship him.

events.wikipedia_extract: Diocletian, nicknamed Jovius, was Roman emperor from 284 until his abdication in 305. He was born Diocles to a family of low status in the Roman province of Dalmatia. As with other Illyrian soldiers of the period, Diocles rose through the ranks of the military early in his career, serving under Aurelian and Probus, and eventually becoming a cavalry commander for the army of Emperor Carus. After the deaths of Carus and his son Numerian on a campaign in Persia, Diocles was proclaimed emperor by the troops, taking the name "Diocletianus". The title was also claimed by Carus's surviving son, Carinus, but Diocletian defeated him in the Battle of the Margus.

events.name: Battle of the River Margus
events.summary: The decisive confrontation between Diocletian and the western emperor Carinus near modern-day Belgrade. Carinus held the superior army and was on the verge of victory when one of his own officers, whose wife he had seduced, killed him, allowing Diocletian to become undisputed master of the Roman world.

2.summary: The decisive confrontation between Diocletian and the western emperor Carinus near modern-day Belgrade. Carinus held the superior army and was on the verge of victory when one of his own officers, whose wife he had seduced, killed him, allowing Diocletian to become undisputed master of the Roman world.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of the Margus or Battle of Margum was fought in July 285 for control of the Roman Empire between the armies of Diocletian and Carinus in the valley of the Margus River in Moesia, probably near the settlement of Margum. The battle proved to be the tipping point that led to the eventual resolution of the Crisis of the Third Century and the return of stability to the Empire.

events.name: Division of the Roman Empire
events.summary: Brownworth describes Diocletian splitting the Roman Empire into eastern and western halves in 285 AD, with Constantinople serving as capital of the eastern half. He lists this as one of three candidate starting points for Byzantine history and ultimately chooses it as his own preferred beginning.

1.summary: Brownworth describes Diocletian splitting the Roman Empire into eastern and western halves in 285 AD, with Constantinople serving as capital of the eastern half. He lists this as one of three candidate starting points for Byzantine history and ultimately chooses it as his own preferred beginning.

events.wikipedia_extract: In modern historiography, the Western Roman Empire was the Roman Empire's western provinces collectively during any period in which they were administered separately from the eastern provinces by a separate, independent imperial court. Particularly during the period from AD 395 to 476, there were separate, coequal courts dividing the governance of the empire into western and eastern provinces with a distinct imperial succession in the separate courts. The terms Western Roman Empire and Eastern Roman Empire were coined in modern times to describe political entities that were de facto independent; contemporary Romans did not consider the Empire to have been split into two empires but viewed it as a single polity governed by two imperial courts for administrative purposes. The Western Empire collapsed in 476, and the Western imperial court in Ravenna disappeared by 554, at the end of Justinian's Gothic War.

events.name: Maximian Elevated to Augustus
events.summary: After Maximian crushed the Gaulish revolt but failed to cross to Britain due to its nascent fleet, Diocletian recognized the seriousness of the western situation and elevated Maximian from Caesar to the full rank of Augustus. Brownworth calls this an extraordinary development: the first peaceful division of the empire.

2.summary: After Maximian crushed the Gaulish revolt but failed to cross to Britain due to its nascent fleet, Diocletian recognized the seriousness of the western situation and elevated Maximian from Caesar to the full rank of Augustus. Brownworth calls this an extraordinary development: the first peaceful division of the empire.

events.wikipedia_extract: Maximian, nicknamed Herculius, was Roman emperor from 286 to 305. He was Caesar from 285 to 286, then Augustus from 286 to 305. He shared the latter title with his co-emperor and superior, Diocletian, whose political brain complemented Maximian's military brawn. Maximian established his residence at Trier but spent most of his time on campaign. In late 285, he suppressed rebels in Gaul known as the Bagaudae. From 285 to 288, he fought against Germanic tribes along the Rhine frontier. Together with Diocletian, he launched a scorched earth campaign deep into Alamannic territory in 288, refortifying the frontier.

events.name: Establishment of the Tetrarchy
events.summary: Established by Diocletian, the Tetrarchy was a system of four co-rulers comprising two senior Augusti and two junior Caesars, created when Diocletian and Maximian adopted their sons-in-law Galerius and Constantius Chlorus and elevated them to the rank of Caesar. The arrangement was intended to bring stability and orderly succession to the Roman Empire. Instead, it collapsed into civil war involving multiple competing factions, the turmoil of which shaped the early life of Constantine.

2.summary: Diocletian and Maximian adopted their respective sons-in-law Galerius and Constantius Chlorus, raising them to the rank of Caesar to create a four-man ruling system. Brownworth notes the system is called the tetrarchy (four rulers), though he says the name is somewhat imprecise since it comprised two senior Augusti and two junior Caesars.

3.summary: Diocletian's system of four co-rulers — two senior Augusti and two junior Caesars — intended to usher in an age of peace and prosperity. Instead, Brownworth says, it degenerated into full-blown civil war with up to four sides at once. Constantine was born into this confusing world.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Tetrarchy was the system instituted by Roman emperor Diocletian to govern the ancient Roman Empire by dividing it between two emperors, the augusti, and their junior colleagues and designated successors, the caesares. It was kept in place between AD 293 and 324.

3.text: He was born into the confusing world of Diocletian's Tetrarchy, a single empire with four legitimate
His solution, not surprisingly, was a new official tetrarchy.

events.name: Great Persecution of the Christians
events.summary: The last and most severe Roman persecution of Christians, which Brownworth says began as a direct consequence of Diocletian's policy of demanding divine worship from every citizen of the empire. Christians who refused to sacrifice to the emperor created a significant conflict.

2.summary: The last and most severe Roman persecution of Christians, which Brownworth says began as a direct consequence of Diocletian's policy of demanding divine worship from every citizen of the empire. Christians who refused to sacrifice to the emperor created a significant conflict.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Diocletianic or Great Persecution was the last and most severe persecution of Christians in the Roman Empire. In 303, the emperors Diocletian, Maximian, Galerius, and Constantius issued a series of edicts rescinding Christians' legal rights and demanding that they comply with traditional religious practices. Later edicts targeted the clergy and demanded universal sacrifice, ordering all inhabitants to sacrifice to the Roman gods. The persecution varied in intensity across the empire—weakest in Gaul and Britain, where only the first edict was applied, and strongest in the Eastern provinces. Persecutory laws were nullified by different emperors at different times. Traditionally, the reign of Constantine the Great and Licinius' Edict of Milan in 313 have marked the end of the persecution.

2.text: And so began the last great persecution of the Christians.

events.name: Diocletian's Illness (304)
events.summary: During the celebration of his twentieth year in power in Rome, Diocletian fell deathly ill. He traveled toward Ravenna to escape the marshy climate but collapsed on the way, yet unexpectedly survived. Brownworth credits this illness with prompting Diocletian's decision to retire.

2.summary: During the celebration of his twentieth year in power in Rome, Diocletian fell deathly ill. He traveled toward Ravenna to escape the marshy climate but collapsed on the way, yet unexpectedly survived. Brownworth credits this illness with prompting Diocletian's decision to retire.

events.wikipedia_extract: Diocletian, nicknamed Jovius, was Roman emperor from 284 until his abdication in 305. He was born Diocles to a family of low status in the Roman province of Dalmatia. As with other Illyrian soldiers of the period, Diocles rose through the ranks of the military early in his career, serving under Aurelian and Probus, and eventually becoming a cavalry commander for the army of Emperor Carus. After the deaths of Carus and his son Numerian on a campaign in Persia, Diocles was proclaimed emperor by the troops, taking the name "Diocletianus". The title was also claimed by Carus's surviving son, Carinus, but Diocletian defeated him in the Battle of the Margus.

events.name: Abdication of Diocletian and Maximian
events.summary: On May 1st, 305, both Diocletian and a very reluctant Maximian simultaneously abdicated, elevating their Caesars to Augusti and establishing a new cycle of succession. Brownworth calls this Diocletian's most surprising decision and his most enduring legacy — the voluntary retirement of an emperor was virtually unprecedented.

2.summary: On May 1st, 305, both Diocletian and a very reluctant Maximian simultaneously abdicated, elevating their Caesars to Augusti and establishing a new cycle of succession. Brownworth calls this Diocletian's most surprising decision and his most enduring legacy — the voluntary retirement of an emperor was virtually unprecedented.

As with other Illyrian soldiers of the period, Diocles rose through the ranks of the military early in his career, serving under Aurelian and Probus, and eventually becoming a cavalry commander for the army of Emperor Carus. After the deaths of Carus and his son Numerian on a campaign in Persia, Diocles was proclaimed emperor by the troops, taking the name "Diocletianus". events.name: Abdication of Diocletian and Maximian
events.summary: Both senior Augusti abdicated, appointing Galerius and Constantius as the new senior emperors and Maximinus Daia and Severus as the new Caesars. The fatal flaw, Brownworth notes, was that the plan ignored the ambitious sons of Constantius and Maximian who were passed over.

3.summary: Both senior Augusti abdicated, appointing Galerius and Constantius as the new senior emperors and Maximinus Daia and Severus as the new Caesars. The fatal flaw, Brownworth notes, was that the plan ignored the ambitious sons of Constantius and Maximian who were passed over.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Tetrarchy was the system instituted by Roman emperor Diocletian to govern the ancient Roman Empire by dividing it between two emperors, the augusti, and their junior colleagues and designated successors, the caesares. It was kept in place between AD 293 and 324.

events.name: Campaign Against the Picts (305)
events.summary: Constantine joined his father Constantius Chlorus in Britain to fight the Picts in 305. They were successful together, and Constantius earned the title Britannicus Maximus, but he was too sick to enjoy it and died the following spring in York.

3.summary: Constantine joined his father Constantius Chlorus in Britain to fight the Picts in 305. They were successful together, and Constantius earned the title Britannicus Maximus, but he was too sick to enjoy it and died the following spring in York.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Picts were a group of peoples in what is now Scotland north of the Firth of Forth, in the Early Middle Ages. Where they lived and details of their culture can be gleaned from early medieval texts and Pictish stones. The name Picti appears in written records as an exonym from the late third century AD. They are assumed to have been descendants of the Caledonii and other northern Iron Age tribes. Their territory is referred to as "Pictland" by modern historians. Initially made up of several chiefdoms, it came to be dominated by the Pictish kingdom of Fortriu from the seventh century. During this Verturian hegemony, Picti was adopted as an endonym. This lasted around 160 years until the Pictish kingdom merged with that of Dál Riata to form the Kingdom of Alba, ruled by the House of Alpin. The concept of "Pictish kingship" continued for a few decades until it was abandoned during the reign of Caustantín mac Áeda.

events.name: Army Proclaims Constantine Emperor
events.summary: After Constantius's death in York, his troops hailed Constantine as emperor without waiting for instructions. Galerius was furious but the deed was done; he mitigated the damage by confirming Constantine only as Caesar, not Augustus.

3.summary: After Constantius's death in York, his troops hailed Constantine as emperor without waiting for instructions. Galerius was furious but the deed was done; he mitigated the damage by confirming Constantine only as Caesar, not Augustus.

events.wikipedia_extract: Constantine I, also known as Constantine the Great, was Roman emperor from AD 306 to 337 and the first Roman emperor to convert to Christianity. He played a pivotal role in elevating the status of Christianity in Rome, the Edict of Milan decriminalising Christian practice and ceasing Christian persecution. This was a turning point in the Christianisation of the Roman Empire. He founded the city of Constantinople and made it the capital of the Empire, which it remained for over a millennium.

events.name: Death of Constantius Chlorus
events.summary: Constantius Chlorus died in York in the spring of 306, leaving Constantine without a legitimate claim to power. The army, which had already been won over by Constantine, hailed him as emperor without waiting for instructions from anyone, setting off a new round of civil conflict.

3.summary: Constantius Chlorus died in York in the spring of 306, leaving Constantine without a legitimate claim to power. The army, which had already been won over by Constantine, hailed him as emperor without waiting for instructions from anyone, setting off a new round of civil conflict.

events.wikipedia_extract: Marcus Flavius Valerius Constantius, also called Constantius I, was a Roman emperor from 305 to 306—and was father of Constantine the Great, the first Christian emperor of Rome. He was one of the four original members of the Tetrarchy established by Diocletian, first serving as caesar from 293 to 305 and then ruling as augustus until his death. The nickname "Chlorus" was first popularized by Byzantine-era historians and not used during the emperor's lifetime.

events.name: Maxentius Seizes Rome
events.summary: Maxentius, also passed over in the Tetrarchic succession, moved into Rome, declared it tax-free, and was proclaimed emperor to cheering crowds. He then recalled his father Maximian as co-emperor and allied with Constantine by giving him his sister Fausta in marriage.

3.summary: Maxentius, also passed over in the Tetrarchic succession, moved into Rome, declared it tax-free, and was proclaimed emperor to cheering crowds. He then recalled his father Maximian as co-emperor and allied with Constantine by giving him his sister Fausta in marriage.

events.wikipedia_extract: Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maxentius was a Roman emperor from 306 until his death in 312. Despite ruling in Italy and North Africa, and having the recognition of the Senate in Rome, he was not recognized as a legitimate emperor by his fellow emperors.

events.name: Defeat and Death of Severus
events.summary: Galerius sent Severus to crush Maxentius in Italy, but Severus's army melted away at the very walls of Rome due to massive, well-placed bribes from Maxentius. Severus fled to Ravenna, was captured, forced to abdicate, and killed.

3.summary: Galerius sent Severus to crush Maxentius in Italy, but Severus's army melted away at the very walls of Rome due to massive, well-placed bribes from Maxentius. Severus fled to Ravenna, was captured, forced to abdicate, and killed.

events.wikipedia_extract: Flavius Valerius Severus, also called Severus II, was a Roman emperor from 306 to 307, and a member of the Tetrarchy. He shared control of the western half of the empire with Constantine I, but spent most of his short reign in a civil war against the usurper Maxentius, who later killed him and took over Italy.

events.name: Galerius Invades Italy
events.summary: Galerius, deciding to handle the matter himself, invaded Italy. His army proved just as susceptible to Maxentius's bribes, and he barely escaped Italy alive.

3.summary: Galerius, deciding to handle the matter himself, invaded Italy. His army proved just as susceptible to Maxentius's bribes, and he barely escaped Italy alive.

events.wikipedia_extract: Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maxentius was a Roman emperor from 306 until his death in 312. Despite ruling in Italy and North Africa, and having the recognition of the Senate in Rome, he was not recognized as a legitimate emperor by his fellow emperors.

events.name: Conference of Carnuntum
events.summary: The emperors came to Diocletian in 308 to beg him to return to power as the empire had fallen into chaos after his retirement. Diocletian refused, famously replying that if they could see the cabbages he had planted with his own hands at Salona, they would never again judge the empire a tempting prospect.

2.summary: The emperors came to Diocletian in 308 to beg him to return to power as the empire had fallen into chaos after his retirement. Diocletian refused, famously replying that if they could see the cabbages he had planted with his own hands at Salona, they would never again judge the empire a tempting prospect.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Conference of Carnuntum was a gathering of ancient Roman rulers on 11 November 308, intended to stabilize the power-sharing arrangement known as the Tetrarchy. It was convened by the Eastern augustus (emperor) Galerius in the city of Carnuntum, which at the time was located in the Roman province of Pannonia Prima. A dispute over the title of augustus in the West had been ongoing since the previous year, when consecutive invasions by Severus II and Galerius had failed to recover Italy from the usurpers Maxentius and Maximian. Present at the conference were the retired Diocletian, and his former colleague, Maximian.

events.name: Diocletian Called Out of Retirement
events.summary: The surviving emperors called Diocletian out of retirement to stabilize the situation. His solution was a new official Tetrarchy, with Licinius as the new western Augustus, and Constantine confirmed as Caesar. It did not survive the year.

3.summary: The surviving emperors called Diocletian out of retirement to stabilize the situation. His solution was a new official Tetrarchy, with Licinius as the new western Augustus, and Constantine confirmed as Caesar. It did not survive the year.

Present at the conference were the retired Diocletian, and his former colleague, Maximian.

events.name: Maximian Attempts to Overthrow Maxentius
events.summary: Maximian, probably jealous at having to share power with his son, attempted to overthrow Maxentius. He botched the attempt and fled to his son-in-law Constantine.

3.summary: Maximian, probably jealous at having to share power with his son, attempted to overthrow Maxentius. He botched the attempt and fled to his son-in-law Constantine.

events.name: Maximian's Forced Suicide
events.summary: Taking advantage of Constantine's absence, Maximian declared him dead and proclaimed himself emperor. Constantine was very much alive and pursued him; Maximian fled to Marseilles, but the city opened its gates to Constantine, and Maximian was forced to commit suicide. Brownworth notes the killing of his father-in-law affected Constantine deeply and drew him toward monotheism.

Constantine was very much alive and pursued him; Maximian fled to Marseilles, but the city opened its gates to Constantine, and Maximian was forced to commit suicide. Together with Diocletian, he launched a scorched earth campaign deep into Alamannic territory in 288, refortifying the frontier.

events.name: Death of Diocletian
events.summary: Diocletian died in 311 as a contented yet disillusioned private citizen. His daughter and wife had been exiled by his successors and his statues torn down, yet his abdication so awed contemporaries that one wrote he was unique in all human history: he died a private citizen but was still enrolled among the gods.

events.name: Death of Galerius
events.summary: Galerius, by then hopelessly obese and dying from a horrendous cancer, suspended all persecutions of Christians and ordered churches built and prayers said on his behalf, but did not survive long enough to hear them. His death triggered a showdown between Licinius and Maximinus Daia.

3.summary: Galerius, by then hopelessly obese and dying from a horrendous cancer, suspended all persecutions of Christians and ordered churches built and prayers said on his behalf, but did not survive long enough to hear them. His death triggered a showdown between Licinius and Maximinus Daia.

events.wikipedia_extract: Gaius Galerius Valerius Maximianus was Roman emperor from 305 to 311. He participated in the system of government later known as the Tetrarchy, first acting as caesar under Emperor Diocletian. In this period Galerius obtained victory warring against the Persian Sasanian Empire, defeating Narseh at the battle of Satala in 298 and possibly sacking the Sasanian capital of Ctesiphon in 299. He also campaigned across the Danube against the Carpi, defeating them in 297 and 300. Galerius was promoted to augustus upon the abdication of Diocletian in 305, but had to contend with multiple usurpers as the Tetrarchic system broke down. Although he was a staunch opponent of Christianity, he ended the Diocletianic Persecution by issuing the Edict of Serdica in 311.

3.text: The death of Galerius resulted in a showdown between Licinius and Maximinus Dio.

events.name: Battle of the Milvian Bridge
events.summary: Constantine routed Maxentius at Saxa Rubra and chased him back to the Milvian Bridge on October 28, 312. Maxentius's pontoon bridge collapsed when engineers pulled the bolts too early; the panicked army was crushed or thrown off the old Milvian Bridge. Maxentius drowned in the Tiber. Brownworth calls this 'a watershed moment in history,' marking the beginning of Constantine's active protection of Christianity.

Brownworth calls this 'a watershed moment in history,' marking the beginning of Constantine's active protection of Christianity.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of the Milvian Bridge took place between the Roman Emperors Constantine I and Maxentius on 28 October AD 312. It takes its name from the Milvian Bridge, an important route over the Tiber. Constantine won the battle and started on the path that led him to end the Tetrarchy and become the sole ruler of the Roman Empire. Maxentius drowned in the Tiber during the battle; his body was later taken from the river and decapitated, and his head was paraded through the streets of Rome on the day following the battle before being sent to Africa.

3.text: The Battle of the Milvian Bridge left Constantine in supreme control of the West, but more

events.name: Constantine Crosses the Alps
events.summary: In 312, Constantine crossed the Alps and invaded Italy, shrewdly having concluded that Maxentius's territory technically belonged to Licinius and he could keep it for himself. He sacked Turin and Verona before appearing outside Rome on October 28th.

3.summary: In 312, Constantine crossed the Alps and invaded Italy, shrewdly having concluded that Maxentius's territory technically belonged to Licinius and he could keep it for himself. He sacked Turin and Verona before appearing outside Rome on October 28th.

He played a pivotal role in elevating the status of Christianity in Rome, the Edict of Milan decriminalising Christian practice and ceasing Christian persecution. This was a turning point in the Christianisation of the Roman Empire. He founded the city of Constantinople and made it the capital of the Empire, which it remained for over a millennium.

events.name: Constantine's Vision at Saxa Rubra
events.summary: At Saxa Rubra (Red Rocks), seven or eight miles northeast of Rome, Constantine had his famous vision. According to historian Lactantius, writing one or two years after the event, it consisted of a private dream telling Constantine to mark his soldiers with the chi-rho, the first two letters of Christ's name in Greek. Armed with a new certainty of divine favor, he routed Maxentius.

According to historian Lactantius, writing one or two years after the event, it consisted of a private dream telling Constantine to mark his soldiers with the chi-rho, the first two letters of Christ's name in Greek. Armed with a new certainty of divine favor, he routed Maxentius.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Chi Rho is one of the earliest forms of the Christogram, formed by superimposing the first two (capital) letters—chi and rho (ΧΡ)—of the Greek ΧΡΙΣΤΟΣ in such a way that the vertical stroke of the rho intersects the center of the chi.

events.name: Edict of Milan
events.summary: Constantine and Licinius met and issued an edict of toleration worded very carefully, making no mention of a particular god but speaking of 'reverence for the deity' and 'whatsoever divinity dwells in heaven.' They then issued another proclamation giving Christianity full legal recognition across the empire. The two emperors sealed their alliance with the marriage of Licinius and Constantine's sister Constantia.

3.summary: Constantine and Licinius met and issued an edict of toleration worded very carefully, making no mention of a particular god but speaking of 'reverence for the deity' and 'whatsoever divinity dwells in heaven.' They then issued another proclamation giving Christianity full legal recognition across the empire. The two emperors sealed their alliance with the marriage of Licinius and Constantine's sister Constantia.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Edict of Milan was the 13 February 313 AD agreement to treat Christians benevolently within the Roman Empire. Western Roman Emperor Constantine I and Emperor Licinius, who controlled the Balkans, met in Mediolanum and, among other things, agreed to change policies towards Christians following the edict of toleration issued by Emperor Galerius two years earlier in Serdica. The Edict of Milan gave Christianity legal status and a reprieve from persecution but did not make it the state church of the Roman Empire, which occurred in AD 380 with the Edict of Thessalonica, when Nicene Christianity received normative status.

events.name: Licinius Defeats Maximinus Daia
events.summary: Maximinus Daia invaded Licinius's territory with 70,000 troops and seized Byzantium, fighting under the pagan banner while Licinius fought under the cross. Despite being heavily outnumbered, Licinius pulled off a stunning victory. Maximinus Daia, disguised as a slave, fled and chose a slow poison to commit suicide — it drove him mad and took four agonizing days to kill him. Licinius then celebrated with an unchristian bloodbath of anyone related to a former emperor.

3.summary: Maximinus Daia invaded Licinius's territory with 70,000 troops and seized Byzantium, fighting under the pagan banner while Licinius fought under the cross. Despite being heavily outnumbered, Licinius pulled off a stunning victory. Maximinus Daia, disguised as a slave, fled and chose a slow poison to commit suicide — it drove him mad and took four agonizing days to kill him. Licinius then celebrated with an unchristian bloodbath of anyone related to a former emperor.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Tzirallum was part of the civil wars of the Tetrarchy fought on 30 April 313 between the Roman armies of emperors Licinius and Maximinus. The battle location was on the "Campus Serenus" at Tzirallum, identified as the modern-day town of Çorlu, in Tekirdağ Province, in the Turkish region of Eastern Thrace. Sources put the battle between 18 and 36 Roman miles from Heraclea Perinthus, the modern-day town of Marmara Ereğlisi.

events.name: Dedication of Constantine's Triumphal Arch
events.summary: Constantine's triumphal arch, still standing just outside the Colosseum, made no mention of Christ after his victory at the Milvian Bridge, saying only that he was 'instinct with divinity.' Brownworth uses this as evidence of Constantine's careful political navigation between paganism and Christianity in the immediate aftermath of his conversion.

3.summary: Constantine's triumphal arch, still standing just outside the Colosseum, made no mention of Christ after his victory at the Milvian Bridge, saying only that he was 'instinct with divinity.' Brownworth uses this as evidence of Constantine's careful political navigation between paganism and Christianity in the immediate aftermath of his conversion.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Arch of Constantine is a triumphal arch in Rome dedicated to the emperor Constantine the Great. The arch was commissioned by the Roman Senate to commemorate Constantine's victory over Maxentius at the Battle of the Milvian Bridge in AD 312. Situated between the Colosseum and the Palatine Hill, the arch spans the Via Triumphalis, the route taken by victorious military leaders when they entered the city in a triumphal procession. Dedicated in 315, it is the largest Roman triumphal arch, with overall dimensions of 21 m (69 ft) high, 25.9 m (85 ft) wide and 7.4 m (24 ft) deep. It has three bays, the central one being 11.5 m (38 ft) high and 6.5 m (21 ft) wide and the laterals 7.4 m (24 ft) by 3.4 m (11 ft) each. The arch is constructed of brick-faced concrete covered in marble.

events.name: Constantine Moves Capital East to Sofia
events.summary: Constantine moved his capital east to modern Sofia, a curious choice Brownworth says was justifiable only if Constantine considered Licinius more of a threat than any barbarian tribe or western usurper. From there he set himself up as protector of Christians everywhere.

3.summary: Constantine moved his capital east to modern Sofia, a curious choice Brownworth says was justifiable only if Constantine considered Licinius more of a threat than any barbarian tribe or western usurper. From there he set himself up as protector of Christians everywhere.

events.wikipedia_extract: Serdica or Sardica is the historical Roman city that was located in today's Sofia, the capital of Bulgaria.

events.name: Arian Controversy
events.summary: The great theological dispute ignited around 320 by the Alexandrian presbyter Arius, who taught that Christ was a perfect man but not God. It threatened to permanently split the Christian world, led to the Council of Nicaea, and continued to wrack the Church through and beyond Constantine's reign.

4.summary: The great theological dispute ignited around 320 by the Alexandrian presbyter Arius, who taught that Christ was a perfect man but not God. It threatened to permanently split the Christian world, led to the Council of Nicaea, and continued to wrack the Church through and beyond Constantine's reign.

events.wikipedia_extract: Arianism is a Christological doctrine that rejects the traditional notion of the Trinity, teaching that Jesus was created by God and is therefore distinct from God. It is named after its proponent Arius and is regarded as heretical by most modern mainstream branches of Christianity. Arianism is held by a minority of modern denominations, although some of these groups espouse related doctrines such as Socinianism, and others avoid the term "Arian" because of its historically negative connotations. Modern denominations sometimes associated with the teaching include Jehovah's Witnesses and some churches within the Churches of Christ.

events.name: Licinius Persecutes Christians
events.summary: In 323 Licinius uncovered evidence that several bishops were openly conspiring against him, and he executed them and destroyed their churches. Having begun to suspect Christians as a fifth column, this crackdown gave Constantine the pretext he was looking for to invade the East.

3.summary: In 323 Licinius uncovered evidence that several bishops were openly conspiring against him, and he executed them and destroyed their churches. Having begun to suspect Christians as a fifth column, this crackdown gave Constantine the pretext he was looking for to invade the East.

events.wikipedia_extract: Valerius Licinianus Licinius was Roman emperor from 308 to 324. For most of his reign, he was the colleague and rival of Constantine I, with whom he co-authored the Edict of Milan that granted official toleration to Christians in the Roman Empire. He was finally defeated at the Battle of Chrysopolis, and was later executed on the orders of Constantine.

events.name: End of the Roman Civil Wars
events.summary: With the defeat and eventual execution of Licinius, the decades of civil war that began with Diocletian's abdication finally ended. Constantine was sole ruler of the Roman Empire at age 52, popular and dashing, with three sons to ensure the succession. Brownworth writes that it must have seemed to many that a new age was dawning.

3.summary: With the defeat and eventual execution of Licinius, the decades of civil war that began with Diocletian's abdication finally ended. Constantine was sole ruler of the Roman Empire at age 52, popular and dashing, with three sons to ensure the succession. Brownworth writes that it must have seemed to many that a new age was dawning.

events.wikipedia_extract: Constantine I, also known as Constantine the Great, was Roman emperor from AD 306 to 337 and the first Roman emperor to convert to Christianity. He played a pivotal role in elevating the status of Christianity in Rome, the Edict of Milan decriminalising Christian practice and ceasing Christian persecution. This was a turning point in the Christianisation of the Roman Empire. He founded the city of Constantinople and made it the capital of the Empire, which it remained for over a millennium.

events.name: First Council of Nicaea
events.summary: The first great ecumenical council of the Church, called by Constantine in 325 to resolve the Arian controversy. Through both persuasion and intimidation, Constantine secured agreement on the homoousios formula — that Christ is of one substance with the Father. Arius was officially condemned and his writings burned, and the date of Easter was also set.

4.summary: The first great ecumenical council of the Church, called by Constantine in 325 to resolve the Arian controversy. Through both persuasion and intimidation, Constantine secured agreement on the homoousios formula — that Christ is of one substance with the Father. Arius was officially condemned and his writings burned, and the date of Easter was also set.

events.wikipedia_extract: The First Council of Nicaea was a council of Christian bishops convened in the Bithynian city of Nicaea by the Roman Emperor Constantine I. The Council of Nicaea met from May until the end of July 325.

events.name: Execution of Crispus and Murder of Fausta
events.summary: In February 326 at Sirmium, Constantine arrested and executed his eldest son Crispus and a half-brother for reasons never made entirely clear, then also had his wife Fausta killed in her bath. Rumors of an affair or a plot against Constantine's life circulated but were never resolved.

4.summary: In February 326 at Sirmium, Constantine arrested and executed his eldest son Crispus and a half-brother for reasons never made entirely clear, then also had his wife Fausta killed in her bath. Rumors of an affair or a plot against Constantine's life circulated but were never resolved.

events.wikipedia_extract: Flavius Julius Crispus was the eldest son of the Roman emperor Constantine I, as well as his junior colleague (caesar) from March 317 until his execution by his father in 326. The grandson of the augustus Constantius I, Crispus was the elder half-brother of the future augustus Constantine II and became co-caesar with him and with his cousin Licinius II at Serdica, part of the settlement ending the Cibalensean War between Constantine and his father's rival Licinius I. Crispus ruled from Augusta Treverorum (Trier) in Roman Gaul between 318 and 323 and defeated the navy of Licinius I at the Battle of the Hellespont in 324, which with the land Battle of Chrysopolis won by Constantine forced the resignation of Licinius and his son, leaving Constantine the sole augustus and the Constantinian dynasty in control of the entire empire. It is unclear what the legal status was of the relationship between Crispus's mother Minervina and Constantine; Crispus may have been an illegitimate son.

events.name: Helena's Pilgrimage to the Holy Land
events.summary: At age 72, Constantine's mother Helena traveled to the Holy Land, founding churches along the way — including the Church of the Nativity at Bethlehem, one on the Mount of Olives, and the Church of the Holy Sepulchre at Jerusalem — and discovering the True Cross. She died in the Holy Land, becoming the first recorded Christian pilgrim.

4.summary: At age 72, Constantine's mother Helena traveled to the Holy Land, founding churches along the way — including the Church of the Nativity at Bethlehem, one on the Mount of Olives, and the Church of the Holy Sepulchre at Jerusalem — and discovering the True Cross. She died in the Holy Land, becoming the first recorded Christian pilgrim.

events.wikipedia_extract: Flavia Julia Helena, also known as Helena of Constantinople and in Christianity as Saint Helena, was a Greek Augusta of the Roman Empire and mother of Emperor Constantine the Great as well as a Canonized saint in both Catholic and Orthodox Churches for her pivotal role in the spread of Christianity. She was born in the lower classes traditionally in the city of Drepanon, Bithynia, in Asia Minor, which was renamed Helenopolis.

events.name: Discovery of the True Cross
events.summary: Helena's discovery of the True Cross in Jerusalem during her pilgrimage. A piece of it was brought back and placed atop the Milion (the first milestone) of Constantinople, and another piece was placed inside the orb held by Constantine's colossal statue.

4.summary: Helena's discovery of the True Cross in Jerusalem during her pilgrimage. A piece of it was brought back and placed atop the Milion (the first milestone) of Constantinople, and another piece was placed inside the orb held by Constantine's colossal statue.

events.wikipedia_extract: In Christian tradition, the True Cross is the original wooden cross on which Jesus of Nazareth was crucified.

4.text: was placed the most venerable Christian relic, the True Cross, recently brought back from
and an orb, in which had been placed another piece of the True Cross.
and finding the True Cross.

events.name: Dedication of Constantinople
events.summary: On Monday, May 11, 330, Constantinople was formally dedicated to God the Father under its official name Nova Roma. The 40-day celebration included both pagan and Christian elements, culminating in Constantine taking mass at the high altar of St. Irene while the pagan population prayed for his prosperity in their temples.

4.summary: On Monday, May 11, 330, Constantinople was formally dedicated to God the Father under its official name Nova Roma. The 40-day celebration included both pagan and Christian elements, culminating in Constantine taking mass at the high altar of St. Irene while the pagan population prayed for his prosperity in their temples.

events.wikipedia_extract: Constantinople was a historical city located on the Bosporus, which served as the capital of the Roman, Byzantine, Latin, and Ottoman empires between its consecration in 330 and the formal abolition of the Ottoman sultanate in 1922. Constantinople was founded in 324, initially as New Rome, during the reign of Constantine the Great on the site of the existing settlement of Byzantium and in 330 became the capital of the Roman Empire. Following the collapse of the Western Roman Empire in the late 5th century, Constantinople remained the capital of the Eastern Roman Empire, the Latin Empire (1204–1261), and the Ottoman Empire (1453–1922). In the aftermath of the Turkish War of Independence, the Turkish capital moved to Ankara. The city was officially renamed Istanbul on 28 March 1930. As of December 2025, it is the most populous city in Europe, with a population of more than 16 million residents, straddling the Bosporus Strait and lying in both Europe and Asia, and is the financial centre of Turkey.

events.name: Founding of Constantinople
events.summary: Brownworth identifies the founding of Constantinople — Constantine refounding Byzantium as his new Christian imperial capital — as the conventional starting point most historians give to Byzantine history. He distinguishes two related dates: 324, when Constantine began building, and 330, when the city was formally dedicated.

1.summary: Brownworth identifies the founding of Constantinople — Constantine refounding Byzantium as his new Christian imperial capital — as the conventional starting point most historians give to Byzantine history. He distinguishes two related dates: 324, when Constantine began building, and 330, when the city was formally dedicated.

Constantinople was founded in 324, initially as New Rome, during the reign of Constantine the Great on the site of the existing settlement of Byzantium and in 330 became the capital of the Roman Empire. Following the collapse of the Western Roman Empire in the late 5th century, Constantinople remained the capital of the Eastern Roman Empire, the Latin Empire (1204–1261), and the Ottoman Empire (1453–1922). In the aftermath of the Turkish War of Independence, the Turkish capital moved to Ankara. The city was officially renamed Istanbul on 28 March 1930. As of December 2025, it is the most populous city in Europe, with a population of more than 16 million residents, straddling the Bosporus Strait and lying in both Europe and Asia, and is the financial centre of Turkey.

events.name: Placement of the Serpent Column in the Hippodrome
events.summary: Constantine placed the ancient Serpent Column — made by the Greeks in 479 BC to commemorate their victory over the Persians — at one end of the Hippodrome of Constantinople, repurposing this symbol of Greek triumph as a monument in his new capital.

4.summary: Constantine placed the ancient Serpent Column — made by the Greeks in 479 BC to commemorate their victory over the Persians — at one end of the Hippodrome of Constantinople, repurposing this symbol of Greek triumph as a monument in his new capital.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Serpent Column, also known as the Serpentine Column, Plataean Tripod or Delphi Tripod, is an ancient bronze column at the Hippodrome of Constantinople in what is now Istanbul, Turkey. It is part of an ancient Greek sacrificial tripod, originally in Delphi and relocated to Constantinople by Constantine the Great in 324. It was built to commemorate the Greeks who fought and defeated the Persian Empire at the Battle of Plataea. The serpent heads of the 8-metre (26 ft) high column remained intact until the end of the 17th century.

events.name: Foundation of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre
events.summary: Helena's most important ecclesiastical foundation, built in Jerusalem over the site of Christ's burial and resurrection. Brownworth describes it as the most significant of her Holy Land churches.

4.summary: Helena's most important ecclesiastical foundation, built in Jerusalem over the site of Christ's burial and resurrection. Brownworth describes it as the most significant of her Holy Land churches.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Church of the Holy Sepulchre, also known as the Church of the Resurrection, is a fourth-century church in the Christian Quarter of the Old City of Jerusalem. The church is simultaneously the seat of the Armenian Patriarchate of Jerusalem, Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Jerusalem, and the Catholic Latin Patriarchate of Jerusalem. It is the holiest site in Christianity and it has been an important pilgrimage site for Christians since the fourth century.

events.name: Baptism of Constantine
events.summary: Constantine waited until the very last moment of his life to be baptized, believing it would wash away all his sins. He was baptized by an Arian bishop — Eusebius of Nicomedia — an irony Brownworth underscores given Constantine's career-long struggle with the Arian controversy.

4.summary: Constantine waited until the very last moment of his life to be baptized, believing it would wash away all his sins. He was baptized by an Arian bishop — Eusebius of Nicomedia — an irony Brownworth underscores given Constantine's career-long struggle with the Arian controversy.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Baptism of Constantine is a painting by assistants of the Italian Renaissance artist Raphael. It was most likely painted by Gianfrancesco Penni, between 1517 and 1524.

events.name: Death of Constantine
events.summary: Constantine died on May 22, 337, after a 31-year reign — the longest since Augustus. His body was placed in a golden coffin draped with a purple shroud in the Church of the Holy Apostles, where he had arranged twelve apostolic sarcophagi around his own, presenting himself as symbol of the Savior. His death signaled the final end of Diocletian's system.

4.summary: Constantine died on May 22, 337, after a 31-year reign — the longest since Augustus. His body was placed in a golden coffin draped with a purple shroud in the Church of the Holy Apostles, where he had arranged twelve apostolic sarcophagi around his own, presenting himself as symbol of the Savior. His death signaled the final end of Diocletian's system.

events.wikipedia_extract: Constantine I, also known as Constantine the Great, was Roman emperor from AD 306 to 337 and the first Roman emperor to convert to Christianity. He played a pivotal role in elevating the status of Christianity in Rome, the Edict of Milan decriminalising Christian practice and ceasing Christian persecution. This was a turning point in the Christianisation of the Roman Empire. He founded the city of Constantinople and made it the capital of the Empire, which it remained for over a millennium.

events.name: Usurpation of Magnentius
events.summary: While Constans was away hunting, the usurper Magnentius donned the purple. Constans fled, was captured, and killed. Constantius defeated Magnentius the following year.

5.summary: While Constans was away hunting, the usurper Magnentius donned the purple. Constans fled, was captured, and killed. Constantius defeated Magnentius the following year.

events.wikipedia_extract: Magnus Magnentius was a Roman general and usurper against Constantius II. Of Germanic descent, Magnentius served with distinction in Gaul, where the army chose him as a replacement for the unpopular emperor Constans. Acclaimed Augustus on 18 January 350, Magnentius quickly killed Constans and gained control over most of the Western Empire. The Eastern emperor Constantius II, brother of Constans, refused to acknowledge Magnentius's legitimacy, leading to three years of civil war. Decisively defeated at the Battle of Mons Seleucus, Magnentius killed himself on 10 August 353.

events.name: Julian's Gallic Campaign
events.summary: Hastily sent to Gaul, Julian surprised everyone by personally leading a successful campaign, recapturing important cities and smashing an army of 30,000 with only 11,000 soldiers at a cost of just 247 casualties. Within four years the entire frontier was settled.

5.summary: Hastily sent to Gaul, Julian surprised everyone by personally leading a successful campaign, recapturing important cities and smashing an army of 30,000 with only 11,000 soldiers at a cost of just 247 casualties. Within four years the entire frontier was settled.

events.wikipedia_extract: Julian was the Caesar of the West from 355 to 360 and Roman emperor from 361 to 363, as well as a notable philosopher and author in Greek. His rejection of Christianity, and his promotion of Neoplatonic Hellenism caused him to be remembered as Julian the Apostate in the Christian tradition.

events.name: Mutiny of the Petulantes
events.summary: While Julian was stopping in Paris en route to send troops east to Constantius, the Petulantes Legion mutinied and declared Julian emperor. Julian claimed his men had forced the title upon him, but it signaled he would no longer take orders from Constantius.

5.summary: While Julian was stopping in Paris en route to send troops east to Constantius, the Petulantes Legion mutinied and declared Julian emperor. Julian claimed his men had forced the title upon him, but it signaled he would no longer take orders from Constantius.

events.wikipedia_extract: Petulantes was an auxilia palatina of the Late Roman army.

events.name: Death of Constantius II
events.summary: While marching against Julian, Constantius II reached Cilicia in a confident mood but fell mortally ill after seeing a headless corpse he took as a bad omen. On his deathbed he was baptized and, not knowing of Julian's apostasy, magnanimously named Julian his successor. He was only 44 years old.

5.summary: While marching against Julian, Constantius II reached Cilicia in a confident mood but fell mortally ill after seeing a headless corpse he took as a bad omen. On his deathbed he was baptized and, not knowing of Julian's apostasy, magnanimously named Julian his successor. He was only 44 years old.

events.wikipedia_extract: Constantius II was Roman emperor from 337 to 361. His reign saw constant warfare on the borders against the Sasanian Empire and Germanic peoples, while internally the Roman Empire went through repeated civil wars, court intrigues, and usurpations. His religious policies inflamed domestic conflicts that would continue after his death.

events.name: Battle of Ctesiphon
events.summary: Despite facing a huge Persian army complete with dreaded war elephants, Julian ordered a charge and gained an overwhelming victory. An eyewitness reported 2,500 Persians killed at a cost of only 70 Romans. However, the city remained impregnable, and Shapur's main army was approaching.

5.summary: Despite facing a huge Persian army complete with dreaded war elephants, Julian ordered a charge and gained an overwhelming victory. An eyewitness reported 2,500 Persians killed at a cost of only 70 Romans. However, the city remained impregnable, and Shapur's main army was approaching.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Ctesiphon took place on 29 May 363 between the armies of Roman Emperor Julian and an army of the Sasanian Empire outside the walls of the Persian capital Ctesiphon. The battle resulted in a Roman victory, but eventually the Roman forces found themselves unable to continue their campaign as they were too far from their supply lines.

events.name: Death of Julian
events.summary: During the retreat from Ctesiphon, Julian's army came under heavy attack while crossing a stream. Without stopping to strap on his armor, Julian leapt into battle and was struck in the side with a spear. The point was removed from deep inside his liver, and he died at midnight — according to legend with the words, 'Thou hast conquered, Galilean.'

5.summary: During the retreat from Ctesiphon, Julian's army came under heavy attack while crossing a stream. Without stopping to strap on his armor, Julian leapt into battle and was struck in the side with a spear. The point was removed from deep inside his liver, and he died at midnight — according to legend with the words, 'Thou hast conquered, Galilean.'

events.wikipedia_extract: Julian was the Caesar of the West from 355 to 360 and Roman emperor from 361 to 363, as well as a notable philosopher and author in Greek. His rejection of Christianity, and his promotion of Neoplatonic Hellenism caused him to be remembered as Julian the Apostate in the Christian tradition.

events.name: Julian's Persian Campaign
events.summary: Julian left Antioch in March 363 with a large army, intending to threaten Ctesiphon and impose harsh peace terms. Shapur avoided direct engagement, relying on harassment. Julian won a victory outside Ctesiphon but found the city impregnable, half his army had not materialized, and Shapur's main force was approaching — forcing a disastrous retreat.

5.summary: Julian left Antioch in March 363 with a large army, intending to threaten Ctesiphon and impose harsh peace terms. Shapur avoided direct engagement, relying on harassment. Julian won a victory outside Ctesiphon but found the city impregnable, half his army had not materialized, and Shapur's main force was approaching — forcing a disastrous retreat.

events.wikipedia_extract: Julian's Persian expedition took place from March to July 363 AD. It was the final military campaign to be undertaken by Roman emperor Julian, who died in battle at Samarra. The Roman Empire fought against the Sasanian Empire, which was ruled at the time by Shapur II, and sought to capture the Sasanian capital city Ctesiphon.

events.name: Barbarian Invasions and Sack of Rome
events.summary: After Julian's death the weaknesses he had identified in the army only worsened. Barbarians poured over the borders with increasing frequency, the city of Rome was sacked, and within less than a century the Western emperor barely controlled Italy while the Eastern emperor was a figurehead.

5.summary: After Julian's death the weaknesses he had identified in the army only worsened. Barbarians poured over the borders with increasing frequency, the city of Rome was sacked, and within less than a century the Western emperor barely controlled Italy while the Eastern emperor was a figurehead.

events.wikipedia_extract: The fall of the Western Roman Empire, also called the fall of the Roman Empire or the fall of Rome, was the loss of central political control in the Western Roman Empire, a process in which the Empire failed to enforce its rule, and its vast territory was divided among several successor polities. The Roman Empire lost the strengths that had allowed it to exercise effective control over its Western provinces; modern historians posit factors including the effectiveness and numbers of the army, the health and numbers of the Roman population, the strength of the economy, the competence of the emperors, the internal struggles for power, the religious changes of the period, and the efficiency of the civil administration. Increasing pressure from invading peoples outside Roman culture also contributed greatly to the collapse. Climatic changes and both endemic and epidemic diseases drove many of these immediate factors. The reasons for the collapse are major subjects of the historiography of the ancient world and they inform much modern discourse on state failure.

events.name: Byzantine Preservation of Classical Texts
events.summary: Brownworth states that 40,000 of the 55,000 ancient Greek texts we possess today were copied by Byzantine scribes, arguing that without this effort Aristotle, Plato, and most of ancient Greek literature would have vanished. He uses this fact to directly rebut the claim that the Irish saved civilization.

17.summary: Brownworth states that 40,000 of the 55,000 ancient Greek texts we possess today were copied by Byzantine scribes, arguing that without this effort Aristotle, Plato, and most of ancient Greek literature would have vanished. He uses this fact to directly rebut the claim that the Irish saved civilization.

events.wikipedia_extract: Byzantine literature is the Greek literature of the Middle Ages, whether written in the Byzantine Empire or outside its borders. It was marked by a linguistic diglossy; two distinct forms of Byzantine Greek were used, a scholarly dialect based on Attic Greek, and a vernacular based on Koine Greek. Most scholars consider 'literature' to include all medieval Greek texts, but some define it with specific constraints. Byzantine literature is the successor to Ancient Greek literature and forms the basis of Modern Greek literature, although it overlaps with both periods.

events.name: Monophysite Controversy
events.summary: The heresy holding that Christ was divine but not human was gaining strength in the eastern provinces during Zeno's reign. His attempt to resolve the dispute through the Henotikon, a statement that avoided taking sides, instead triggered the Acacian Schism between Rome and Constantinople.

6.summary: The heresy holding that Christ was divine but not human was gaining strength in the eastern provinces during Zeno's reign. His attempt to resolve the dispute through the Henotikon, a statement that avoided taking sides, instead triggered the Acacian Schism between Rome and Constantinople.

events.wikipedia_extract: Miaphysitism is the Christological doctrine that holds Jesus, the Incarnate Word, is fully divine and fully human, in one nature or physis. It is the position held by the Oriental Orthodox Churches. It differs from the dyophysitism of the Catholic Church, Eastern Orthodox Church, the Church of the East and major Protestant denominations, which holds that Jesus is one person with two natures as defined by the Council of Chalcedon in 451.

events.name: Assassination of Aetius by Valentinian III
events.summary: The Western emperor Valentinian III killed the general Aetius with his own hands, wanting to rule in his own right. The act destabilized the West and cost Valentinian his life, as disgruntled officers of Aetius assassinated him several months later.

6.summary: The Western emperor Valentinian III killed the general Aetius with his own hands, wanting to rule in his own right. The act destabilized the West and cost Valentinian his life, as disgruntled officers of Aetius assassinated him several months later.

events.wikipedia_extract: Aetius, Aëtius, or Aetios (Ἀέτιος) may refer to:

events.name: Vandal Sack of Rome
events.summary: In 455, a Vandal army entered Rome after Pope Leo I negotiated a promise to spare lives and buildings; despite this, the city was systematically stripped of its wealth over fourteen days, with gold, silver, and gilded church roofs carried off to Carthage. The event was severe enough that the word "vandal" passed into the English language as a lasting reference to its destruction. The sack remained a live grievance for the eastern emperors for nearly a century, ultimately serving as one of the justifications for Justinian's reconquest of the west in the sixth century.

6.summary: The Vandal army stood at Rome's gates; with no public official to stop them, Pope Leo I negotiated a promise not to kill citizens or destroy buildings. For 14 days the city was systematically stripped of all its wealth — gold, silver, and even gilded copper church roofs — all carried off to Carthage. The word 'vandal' itself survives in English from the horror this event inspired.

8.summary: The Vandal sack of Rome in 455 is cited by Brownworth as the earliest moment from which Roman emperors had wanted to reconquer the west — a grievance kept alive for nearly a century before Justinian finally acted on it.

events.wikipedia_extract: The sack of Rome in 455 was carried out by the Vandals led by their king Gaiseric.

events.name: Coronation of Leo I with Patriarchal Ceremony
events.summary: For the first time in imperial history, Leo I's coronation included a second ceremony in the Church of the Holy Wisdom in which the patriarch placed the crown on the emperor's head during a high mass. This tradition of the patriarch crowning the emperor continued until the very end of the Byzantine Empire.

6.summary: For the first time in imperial history, Leo I's coronation included a second ceremony in the Church of the Holy Wisdom in which the patriarch placed the crown on the emperor's head during a high mass. This tradition of the patriarch crowning the emperor continued until the very end of the Byzantine Empire.

events.wikipedia_extract: Leo I, also known as the Thracian, was Eastern Roman emperor from 457 to 474. He was a native of Dacia Aureliana near historic Thrace. He is sometimes surnamed with the epithet the Great, probably to distinguish him from his young grandson and co-augustus Leo II.

events.name: Defeat of the Byzantine Expedition Against the Vandals
events.summary: Leo I sent over a thousand ships and a hundred thousand men to destroy the Vandal Kingdom, but placed the incompetent Basiliscus in command at Aspar's persuasion. Within five days Basiliscus had most of his men captured or killed and most of his fleet burnt. He fled back to Constantinople and saved himself only by taking refuge in a church.

6.summary: Leo I sent over a thousand ships and a hundred thousand men to destroy the Vandal Kingdom, but placed the incompetent Basiliscus in command at Aspar's persuasion. Within five days Basiliscus had most of his men captured or killed and most of his fleet burnt. He fled back to Constantinople and saved himself only by taking refuge in a church.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Cape Bon was an engagement during a joint military expedition of the Western and Eastern Roman Empires led by Basiliscus against the Vandal capital of Carthage in 468. The invasion of the kingdom of the Vandals was one of the largest amphibious operations in antiquity, with 1,113 ships and over 50,000 personnel.

events.name: Assassination of Aspar and His Sons
events.summary: After Aspar attempted to assassinate Zeno and tried to infiltrate his troops, Leo I summoned Aspar and his sons to the palace on the pretext of a morning walk and had them all killed, finally freeing himself from two decades of Germanic domination.

6.summary: After Aspar attempted to assassinate Zeno and tried to infiltrate his troops, Leo I summoned Aspar and his sons to the palace on the pretext of a morning walk and had them all killed, finally freeing himself from two decades of Germanic domination.

events.wikipedia_extract: Flavius Ardabur Aspar was an Eastern Roman patrician and magister militum of Alanic-Gothic descent. As the general of a Germanic army in Roman service, Aspar exerted great influence on the Eastern Roman Emperors for half a century, from the 420s to his death in 471, through the reigns of Theodosius II, Marcian and Leo I, who, in the end, had him killed. His death led to the ending of the Germanic domination of Eastern Roman policy.

events.name: Usurpation of Basiliscus
events.summary: After engineering Zeno's flight, Verina failed to get her lover made emperor and the army chose her brother Basiliscus instead. His disastrous reign featured mob violence against Isaurians, the assassination of Verina's lover, the appointment of Timothy the Weasel as religious advisor, abolition of the Patriarchate of Constantinople causing massive riots, a destructive fire, and ruinous taxation — until Zeno returned and reclaimed the throne.

6.summary: After engineering Zeno's flight, Verina failed to get her lover made emperor and the army chose her brother Basiliscus instead. His disastrous reign featured mob violence against Isaurians, the assassination of Verina's lover, the appointment of Timothy the Weasel as religious advisor, abolition of the Patriarchate of Constantinople causing massive riots, a destructive fire, and ruinous taxation — until Zeno returned and reclaimed the throne.

events.wikipedia_extract: Basiliscus was Eastern Roman emperor from 9 January 475 to August 476. He became magister militum per Thracias in 464, under his brother-in-law, Emperor Leo I. Basiliscus commanded the army for an invasion of the Vandal Kingdom in 468, which was defeated at the Battle of Cape Bon. There were accusations at the time that Basiliscus was bribed by Aspar, the magister militum; many historians dismiss this, instead concluding that Basiliscus was either incompetent or foolish for accepting Vandal King Gaiseric's offer of a truce, which the latter used to construct fireships. Basiliscus's defeat cost the Eastern Empire 130,000 pounds (59,000 kg) of gold, causing the empire to hover above bankruptcy for 30 years. When Basiliscus returned to Constantinople, he sought refuge in the Hagia Sophia. His sister, Empress Verina, secured a pardon for him and he left the church to retire in Neapolis.

events.name: Zeno's Flight to Isauria
events.summary: While presiding over the games in the hippodrome, Zeno received a panicked message from Verina claiming the people, army, and senate were all against him. Without pausing to verify the rumor, he fled to his native Isauria, where he spent 20 months in exile planning his return.

6.summary: While presiding over the games in the hippodrome, Zeno received a panicked message from Verina claiming the people, army, and senate were all against him. Without pausing to verify the rumor, he fled to his native Isauria, where he spent 20 months in exile planning his return.

events.wikipedia_extract: Zeno was Eastern Roman emperor from 474 to 475 and again from 476 to 491. His reign was plagued by domestic revolts and religious dissension, but was more successful on the foreign front. He is credited with further stabilizing the Eastern empire, while the Western Roman Empire fell following the deposition of Romulus Augustulus.

events.name: Abolition of the Patriarchate of Constantinople by Basiliscus
events.summary: At the urging of Timothy the Weasel, Basiliscus abolished the Patriarchate of Constantinople — an act so offensive to the population that it triggered massive riots and nearly cost him his throne.

6.summary: At the urging of Timothy the Weasel, Basiliscus abolished the Patriarchate of Constantinople — an act so offensive to the population that it triggered massive riots and nearly cost him his throne.

events.name: Deposition of the Last Western Emperor
events.summary: Brownworth mentions 476 as the year the last western emperor was 'sent into a comfortable retirement,' describing it as the formal end-point of the western empire and the point from which Justinian's reconquest ambitions could be dated.

8.summary: Brownworth mentions 476 as the year the last western emperor was 'sent into a comfortable retirement,' describing it as the formal end-point of the western empire and the point from which Justinian's reconquest ambitions could be dated.

events.wikipedia_extract: Romulus Augustus, nicknamed Augustulus, was Roman emperor of the West from 31 October 475 until 4 September 476. Romulus was placed on the imperial throne while still a minor by his father Orestes, the magister militum, for whom he served as little more than a figurehead. After a rule of ten months, the barbarian general Odoacer defeated and killed Orestes and deposed Romulus. As Odoacer did not proclaim any successor, Romulus is typically regarded as the last Western Roman emperor, his deposition marking the end of the Western Roman Empire as a political entity. The deposition of Romulus Augustulus is also sometimes used by historians to mark the transition from antiquity to the medieval period.

events.name: Fall of the Western Roman Empire
events.summary: Brownworth notes that the Western Empire fell in 476, an event most people were taught marks 'the fall of Rome.' He argues this has led to a false perception that the Middle East jumped straight from a classical past to a Muslim present, ignoring the millennium of Byzantine history that continued in the East.

1.summary: Brownworth notes that the Western Empire fell in 476, an event most people were taught marks 'the fall of Rome.' He argues this has led to a false perception that the Middle East jumped straight from a classical past to a Muslim present, ignoring the millennium of Byzantine history that continued in the East.

events.wikipedia_extract: The fall of the Western Roman Empire, also called the fall of the Roman Empire or the fall of Rome, was the loss of central political control in the Western Roman Empire, a process in which the Empire failed to enforce its rule, and its vast territory was divided among several successor polities. The Roman Empire lost the strengths that had allowed it to exercise effective control over its Western provinces; modern historians posit factors including the effectiveness and numbers of the army, the health and numbers of the Roman population, the strength of the economy, the competence of the emperors, the internal struggles for power, the religious changes of the period, and the efficiency of the civil administration. Increasing pressure from invading peoples outside Roman culture also contributed greatly to the collapse. Climatic changes and both endemic and epidemic diseases drove many of these immediate factors. The reasons for the collapse are major subjects of the historiography of the ancient world and they inform much modern discourse on state failure.

events.name: Fall of the Western Roman Empire
events.summary: In September 476 Odoacer entered Ravenna, deposed the young Romulus Augustulus, and sent him into comfortable exile in Campania — marking the end of the Roman Empire in the West. Odoacer chose to give lip service to Zeno's authority rather than install a new puppet emperor, sending the western imperial insignia to Constantinople. Brownworth notes that contemporaries likely did not realize the magnitude of the watershed moment.

events.name: Fall of the Western Roman Empire
events.summary: Brownworth laments that Western history marks 476 and Romulus Augustulus as the end of Rome, completely ignoring the Byzantine continuation. He argues this is a fundamental distortion: the Roman Empire did not fall in 476, and the last Roman Emperor was not a sad little boy but the heroic Constantine XI in 1453.

events.name: Great Fire and Destruction of the Constantinian Library
events.summary: During Basiliscus's reign, a fire started in the Bronzesmith's Bazaar and spread across Constantinople, completely engulfing the great library founded by Julian the Apostate and destroying over 120,000 books as well as numerous sculptures and antiques.

6.summary: During Basiliscus's reign, a fire started in the Bronzesmith's Bazaar and spread across Constantinople, completely engulfing the great library founded by Julian the Apostate and destroying over 120,000 books as well as numerous sculptures and antiques.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Imperial Library of Constantinople, in the capital city of the Byzantine Empire, was the last of the great libraries of the ancient world. Long after the destruction of the Great Library of Alexandria and the other ancient libraries, it preserved the knowledge of the ancient Greeks and Romans for almost 1,000 years. A series of unintentional fires over the years and wartime damage, including the raids of the Fourth Crusade in 1204, affected the building and its contents. While there were many reports of texts surviving into the Ottoman era, no substantive portion of the library has ever been recovered. The library was founded by Constantius II, who established a scriptorium so that the surviving works of Greek literature could be copied for preservation. The Emperor Valens in 372 employed four Greek and three Latin scribes. The majority of Greek classics known today are known through Byzantine copies originating from the Imperial Library of Constantinople.

events.name: Restoration of Zeno as Emperor
events.summary: Zeno marched back from Isauria with Illus and found that the only army Basiliscus sent to stop them defected after brief negotiation. He entered Constantinople unopposed and Basiliscus fled to a church, surrendering only when Zeno promised not to shed his blood. Zeno kept his word, exiling Basiliscus to Cappadocia and letting the winter kill him.
