His reign was plagued by domestic revolts and religious dissension, but was more successful on the foreign front. He is credited with further stabilizing the Eastern empire, while the Western Roman Empire fell following the deposition of Romulus Augustulus.

events.name: Acacian Schism
events.summary: Zeno's attempt to reconcile orthodox Christians and Monophysite heretics by issuing a statement (the Henotikon) that papered over their differences without actually resolving them infuriated both sides. The pope excommunicated the Patriarch of Constantinople, who excommunicated the pope right back, plunging the entire church into a schism that lasted 35 years.

6.summary: Zeno's attempt to reconcile orthodox Christians and Monophysite heretics by issuing a statement (the Henotikon) that papered over their differences without actually resolving them infuriated both sides. The pope excommunicated the Patriarch of Constantinople, who excommunicated the pope right back, plunging the entire church into a schism that lasted 35 years.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Acacian schism, between the Eastern and Western Christian Churches, lasted 35 years, from 484 to 519. It resulted from a drift in the leaders of Eastern Christianity toward Miaphysitism and Emperor Zeno's unsuccessful attempt to reconcile the parties with the Henotikon. The events are described in letters in the Collectio Avellana.

events.name: Revolt of Illus
events.summary: After Zeno confiscated Illus's property and declared him a public enemy over a dispute involving Zeno's brother, Illus defected to a rebel in Syria who was trying to restore paganism, declared the rebel leader emperor, and set up a rival court. Theodoric was sent to suppress them, ejecting Illus from Antioch and finally taking the Isaurian fortress by a ruse — Zeno's sister-in-law was sent inside with a promise of pardon, then opened the gates at night.

6.summary: After Zeno confiscated Illus's property and declared him a public enemy over a dispute involving Zeno's brother, Illus defected to a rebel in Syria who was trying to restore paganism, declared the rebel leader emperor, and set up a rival court. Theodoric was sent to suppress them, ejecting Illus from Antioch and finally taking the Isaurian fortress by a ruse — Zeno's sister-in-law was sent inside with a promise of pardon, then opened the gates at night.

events.wikipedia_extract: Flavius Illus was a Roman general who played an important role in the reigns of the Eastern Emperors Zeno and Basiliscus.

events.name: Theodoric's Invasion of Italy
events.summary: In 488 Zeno sent Theodoric and his entire Ostrogothic people — men, women, and children — into Italy to displace Odoacer and rule in the emperor's name. This solved two problems at once: the Ostrogoths got fertile land of their own and Zeno was rid of them, while Odoacer was punished without the loss of a single Byzantine soldier. After five years of fighting, Theodoric tricked Odoacer into a joint-rule arrangement and then murdered him at a banquet.

6.summary: In 488 Zeno sent Theodoric and his entire Ostrogothic people — men, women, and children — into Italy to displace Odoacer and rule in the emperor's name. This solved two problems at once: the Ostrogoths got fertile land of their own and Zeno was rid of them, while Odoacer was punished without the loss of a single Byzantine soldier. After five years of fighting, Theodoric tricked Odoacer into a joint-rule arrangement and then murdered him at a banquet.

events.wikipedia_extract: Theodoric the Great, also called the Amal, was king of the Ostrogoths (475–526), and ruler of the Ostrogothic Kingdom of Italy between 493 and 526, regent of the Visigoths (511–526), and a patrician of the Eastern Roman Empire. As ruler of the combined Gothic realms, Theodoric controlled an empire stretching from the Atlantic Ocean to the Adriatic Sea. Though Theodoric himself only used the title 'king' (rex), some scholars characterize him as a Western Roman emperor in all but name, since he ruled a large part of the former Western Roman Empire. He also received and used the former Western imperial regalia from Constantinople in 497, and exercised imperial powers recognized in the East, such as naming consuls. The Italian aristocracy referred to him using the imperial title princeps.

events.name: Death of Zeno
events.summary: Zeno died in 491 from a fit of epilepsy, not genuinely mourned. The crowd greeted his widow with cries of 'give the empire an orthodox emperor, give the empire a Roman emperor.' She chose the minister of finance, a native citizen of impeccable breeding, and married him six weeks later — a firm rejection of Zeno's foreign identity and policies.

His reign was plagued by domestic revolts and religious dissension, but was more successful on the foreign front. He is credited with further stabilizing the Eastern empire, while the Western Roman Empire fell following the deposition of Romulus Augustulus.

events.name: Murder of Odoacer by Theodoric
events.summary: Having blockaded Odoacer in Ravenna and proposed a joint rule, Theodoric invited Odoacer to a great banquet and personally struck him with his sword as he was taking his seat, cutting him nearly in half from collarbone to thigh. Theodoric reportedly laughed: 'The wretch cannot have had a bone in his body.'

6.summary: Having blockaded Odoacer in Ravenna and proposed a joint rule, Theodoric invited Odoacer to a great banquet and personally struck him with his sword as he was taking his seat, cutting him nearly in half from collarbone to thigh. Theodoric reportedly laughed: 'The wretch cannot have had a bone in his body.'

events.wikipedia_extract: Odoacer, also spelled Odovacer or Odovacar, was a barbarian soldier and statesman from the Middle Danube who was an officer of the Roman army and deposed the Western Roman child emperor Romulus Augustulus to become the ruler of Italy (476–493). Odoacer's overthrow of Romulus Augustulus is traditionally understood as marking the end of the Western Roman Empire.

events.name: Election of Justin I as Emperor
events.summary: After Anastasius I died and the usual succession dispute broke out, Justin I used his command of the palace guard — the only real troops in the city — along with liberal bribery (five coins and one pound of silver per soldier) to secure his election as emperor. Brownworth notes some historians believe Justinian's own machinations were partly responsible for his uncle's success.

7.summary: After Anastasius I died and the usual succession dispute broke out, Justin I used his command of the palace guard — the only real troops in the city — along with liberal bribery (five coins and one pound of silver per soldier) to secure his election as emperor. Brownworth notes some historians believe Justinian's own machinations were partly responsible for his uncle's success.

events.wikipedia_extract: Justin I, also called Justin the Thracian, was Eastern Roman emperor from 518 to 527. Born to a peasant family, he rose through the ranks of the army to become commander of the imperial guard and when Emperor Anastasius I Dicorus died, he out-maneouvered his rivals and was elected as his successor, in spite of being around 68 years old. His reign is significant for the founding of the Justinian dynasty that included his nephew, Justinian I, and three succeeding emperors. His consort was Empress Euphemia.

events.name: Marriage of Justinian and Theodora
events.summary: After years of being blocked by the empress Euphemia, Justinian married Theodora in a magnificent ceremony in 523, the same year Euphemia died. He had to pressure Justin I to repeal the law forbidding patricians from marrying below their station. Brownworth describes the ceremony as hinting of great things to come.

7.summary: After years of being blocked by the empress Euphemia, Justinian married Theodora in a magnificent ceremony in 523, the same year Euphemia died. He had to pressure Justin I to repeal the law forbidding patricians from marrying below their station. Brownworth describes the ceremony as hinting of great things to come.

events.wikipedia_extract: Theodora may refer to:Theodora, a given name of Greek origin, meaning "God's gift"

events.name: Coronation of Justinian I
events.summary: When Justin I died of an old war wound on August 1, 527, Justinian was crowned in an extravagant ceremony. The contrast with his predecessors was stark: where Justin had been frugal and uneducated, Justinian was lavish, a subtle theologian, a good jurist, and a bold visionary — the ceremony itself proclaimed the empire stood on the brink of a new and glorious age.

7.summary: When Justin I died of an old war wound on August 1, 527, Justinian was crowned in an extravagant ceremony. The contrast with his predecessors was stark: where Justin had been frugal and uneducated, Justinian was lavish, a subtle theologian, a good jurist, and a bold visionary — the ceremony itself proclaimed the empire stood on the brink of a new and glorious age.

events.wikipedia_extract: Justinian I, also known as Justinian the Great, was Roman emperor from 527 to 565.

events.name: Publication of the Codex Justinianus
events.summary: In just 14 months, Tribonian produced the new Codex — a complete recodification of Roman law that removed centuries of repetitions and contradictions, became the supreme authority for every court in the empire, and was made compatible with Christianity. Brownworth calls it the basis of most European legal systems today.

7.summary: In just 14 months, Tribonian produced the new Codex — a complete recodification of Roman law that removed centuries of repetitions and contradictions, became the supreme authority for every court in the empire, and was made compatible with Christianity. Brownworth calls it the basis of most European legal systems today.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Code of Justinian is one part of the Corpus Juris Civilis, the codification of Roman law ordered early in the 6th century AD by Justinian I, who was Eastern Roman emperor in Constantinople. Two other units, the Digest and the Institutes, were created during his reign. The fourth part, the Novellae Constitutiones, was compiled unofficially after his death but is now also thought of as part of the Corpus Juris Civilis.

events.name: Escalating Blue-Green Faction Violence
events.summary: As public anger at John of Cappadocia and Tribonian grew, violence between the Blue and Green chariot-racing factions spilled into the streets with increasing frequency. Justinian cracked down on both factions and limited their privileges, but this failed to stop the violence and ultimately contributed to the conditions that triggered the Nika Revolt.

7.summary: As public anger at John of Cappadocia and Tribonian grew, violence between the Blue and Green chariot-racing factions spilled into the streets with increasing frequency. Justinian cracked down on both factions and limited their privileges, but this failed to stop the violence and ultimately contributed to the conditions that triggered the Nika Revolt.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Nika riots, Nika revolt or Nika sedition took place against Byzantine emperor Justinian I in Constantinople over the course of a week in 532 AD. They are often regarded as the most violent riots in the city's history, with nearly half of Constantinople being burned or destroyed and tens of thousands of people killed.

events.name: Burning of Hagia Sophia and Saint Irene
events.summary: During the Nika Revolt, the rioting mob burned Constantinople's two greatest churches — Saint Irene and Saint Sophia (Hagia Sophia) — completely to the ground, along with most of the houses along the main roads. Brownworth presents this destruction as the very catalyst for Justinian's ambitious rebuilding program, suggesting that he characteristically saw disaster as opportunity.

Brownworth presents this destruction as the very catalyst for Justinian's ambitious rebuilding program, suggesting that he characteristically saw disaster as opportunity.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Nika riots, Nika revolt or Nika sedition took place against Byzantine emperor Justinian I in Constantinople over the course of a week in 532 AD. They are often regarded as the most violent riots in the city's history, with nearly half of Constantinople being burned or destroyed and tens of thousands of people killed.

events.name: Construction of the Hagia Sophia
events.summary: Justinian commissioned Anthemius and Isidore to build a church surpassing all others after the previous Hagia Sophia burned in the Nika Riots. Employing 10,000 workers split into two racing teams, they completed it in five years, ten months, and four days — the largest church in the world for 700 years, its gold-mosaic interior famously causing Justinian to exclaim 'Solomon, I have surpassed thee.'

9.summary: Justinian commissioned Anthemius and Isidore to build a church surpassing all others after the previous Hagia Sophia burned in the Nika Riots. Employing 10,000 workers split into two racing teams, they completed it in five years, ten months, and four days — the largest church in the world for 700 years, its gold-mosaic interior famously causing Justinian to exclaim 'Solomon, I have surpassed thee.'

events.wikipedia_extract: Hagia Sophia, officially the Hagia Sophia Grand Mosque, is a mosque and a major cultural and historical site in Istanbul, Turkey. It was formerly a church (360–1453) and a museum (1935–2020). The last of three church buildings to be successively erected on the site by the Eastern Roman Empire, it was completed in AD 537, becoming the world's largest interior space and among the first to employ a fully pendentive dome. It is considered the epitome of Byzantine architecture and is said to have "changed the history of architecture". From its dedication in 360 until 1453 Hagia Sophia served as the cathedral of Constantinople in the Byzantine liturgical tradition, except for the period 1204–1261 when the Latin Crusaders installed their own hierarchy. After the fall of Constantinople in 1453, it served as a mosque, having its minarets added soon after. The site became a museum in 1935, and was redesignated as a mosque in 2020.

events.name: Construction of the Hagia Sophia
events.summary: Referenced through Justinian's famous exclamation 'Solomon, I have surpassed you' upon its completion, the Hagia Sophia's construction is presented by Brownworth as the crowning visible achievement of Byzantine civilization — a monument so overwhelming that Russian envoys wrote they could not tell whether they were in heaven or on earth.

events.name: Execution of Hypatius
events.summary: After the Nika Revolt was crushed, Hypatius was brought before Justinian, who felt genuine sorrow for the unwilling rebel and was prepared to pardon him. Theodora intervened, arguing that Hypatius had served as a focal point for rebellion and could not be allowed to live lest it happen again. Justinian, as always deferring to her judgment, had him executed.

events.wikipedia_extract: Hypatius or Hypatios is a masculine name of Greek origin meaning highest, supreme.

events.name: Justinian's Building Campaign
events.summary: Following the Nika Riots, Justinian launched a massive building campaign described as unprecedented since the days of Constantine, made possible by the destruction that cleared the capital. Brownworth treats this as secondary to the western reconquest in this episode.

After the fall of Constantinople in 1453, it served as a mosque, having its minarets added soon after. The site became a museum in 1935, and was redesignated as a mosque in 2020.

events.name: Nika Revolt
events.summary: On January 10, 532, violence between Blues and Greens escalated when Justinian executed five rioters but two survivors — rescued by monks — were sheltered in a monastery. Three days later, 30,000 Blues and Greens united in the Hippodrome chanting 'Nika' (Win) at Justinian. The mob burned churches, palaces, and most of the main streets, demanded the dismissal of Tribonian and John of Cappadocia, and attempted to crown first Probus then Hypatius as emperor. Justinian nearly fled until Theodora's famous speech stiffened his resolve; Belisarius, Mundus, and Narcissus then stormed the Hippodrome, killing over 30,000 people and ending the revolt. Brownworth calls it the turning point of Justinian's reign.

7.summary: On January 10, 532, violence between Blues and Greens escalated when Justinian executed five rioters but two survivors — rescued by monks — were sheltered in a monastery. Three days later, 30,000 Blues and Greens united in the Hippodrome chanting 'Nika' (Win) at Justinian. The mob burned churches, palaces, and most of the main streets, demanded the dismissal of Tribonian and John of Cappadocia, and attempted to crown first Probus then Hypatius as emperor. Justinian nearly fled until Theodora's famous speech stiffened his resolve; Belisarius, Mundus, and Narcissus then stormed the Hippodrome, killing over 30,000 people and ending the revolt. Brownworth calls it the turning point of Justinian's reign.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Nika riots, Nika revolt or Nika sedition took place against Byzantine emperor Justinian I in Constantinople over the course of a week in 532 AD. They are often regarded as the most violent riots in the city's history, with nearly half of Constantinople being burned or destroyed and tens of thousands of people killed.

events.name: Nika Riots
events.summary: The Nika Riots were a devastating urban uprising in Constantinople that left much of the city in ruins, including the original Hagia Sophia, before being suppressed with Justinian I still on the throne. The general Narses, who later became a prominent military commander, gained his only prior combat experience during the suppression of the revolt. The destruction the riots caused paradoxically created the conditions for Justinian's ambitious rebuilding campaign, most notably the construction of a far grander Hagia Sophia, as well as his broader program of western reconquest.

8.summary: The Nika Riots are referenced from the previous episode as the event that left Constantinople 'smoldering in ruins' but Justinian secure on his throne, creating the opportunity for the building campaign and western reconquest that are the subject of this episode.

9.summary: Referenced twice in the episode: once as the event in which the original Hagia Sophia was burned, and once as the only prior military experience of Narses — who helped suppress the riots. The burning of the Hagia Sophia is what gave Justinian the opportunity to build the far grander replacement.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Nika riots, Nika revolt or Nika sedition took place against Byzantine emperor Justinian I in Constantinople over the course of a week in 532 AD. They are often regarded as the most violent riots in the city's history, with nearly half of Constantinople being burned or destroyed and tens of thousands of people killed.

events.name: Byzantine Reconquest of North Africa
events.summary: Belisarius sailed with only 15,000 men to destroy the Vandal kingdom, using intelligence gathered in Sicily to land unopposed and defeat Gelimer twice — each time at the moment when Gelimer found his brother dead and froze. The Vandal kingdom was extinguished in little more than a year, and the stunning reconquest made Justinian's dreams of reuniting the empire seem within reach.

8.summary: Belisarius sailed with only 15,000 men to destroy the Vandal kingdom, using intelligence gathered in Sicily to land unopposed and defeat Gelimer twice — each time at the moment when Gelimer found his brother dead and froze. The Vandal kingdom was extinguished in little more than a year, and the stunning reconquest made Justinian's dreams of reuniting the empire seem within reach.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Vandalic War (533–534) was a conflict fought in North Africa between the forces of the Byzantine Empire and the Germanic Vandal Kingdom. It was the first war of Emperor Justinian I's Renovatio imperii Romanorum, wherein the Byzantines attempted to reassert Roman sovereignty over territory formerly controlled by the Western Roman Empire.

events.name: Publication of the Digest and the Institutes
events.summary: In the five years following the Codex, Tribonian published two further landmark works — the Digest and the Institutes — which for the first time incorporated the principal writings of the ancient Roman jurists into the legal system. Together he condensed 2,000 treatises and over 3 million verses into a mere 50 books, a stunning achievement that came at the cost of tolerating his notorious corruption.

7.summary: In the five years following the Codex, Tribonian published two further landmark works — the Digest and the Institutes — which for the first time incorporated the principal writings of the ancient Roman jurists into the legal system. Together he condensed 2,000 treatises and over 3 million verses into a mere 50 books, a stunning achievement that came at the cost of tolerating his notorious corruption.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Digest, also known as the Pandects, was a compendium or digest of juristic writings on Roman law compiled by order of the Byzantine emperor Justinian I in 530–533 AD. It comprises 50 books.

7.text: In the next five years, he published two works, The Digest and The Institutes, which for the

events.name: Byzantine Reconquest of Italy
events.summary: Belisarius sailed with 7,500 men in 535, conquered Sicily, then most of southern Italy, took Naples by infiltrating its aqueduct, and entered Rome in December. After a year-long siege, he drove the Goths north and ultimately tricked Vittigis into surrendering Ravenna by pretending to accept the offer of the western imperial crown. The campaign is described as glorious but plagued by divided command, palace jealousies, and perpetually insufficient reinforcements.

8.summary: Belisarius sailed with 7,500 men in 535, conquered Sicily, then most of southern Italy, took Naples by infiltrating its aqueduct, and entered Rome in December. After a year-long siege, he drove the Goths north and ultimately tricked Vittigis into surrendering Ravenna by pretending to accept the offer of the western imperial crown. The campaign is described as glorious but plagued by divided command, palace jealousies, and perpetually insufficient reinforcements.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Gothic War between the Eastern Roman Empire during the reign of Emperor Justinian I and the Ostrogothic Kingdom of Italy took place from 535 to 554 in the Italian peninsula, Dalmatia, Sardinia, Sicily, and Corsica. It was one of the last of the many Gothic wars against the Byzantine Empire. The war had its roots in Justinian's ambition to recover the provinces of the former Western Roman Empire, which had been lost to invading barbarian tribes during the Migration Period.

events.name: Byzantine Reconquest of Italy (Gothic War)
events.summary: The overarching military campaign that spans the entire episode. Brownworth traces the war's trajectory from near-victory under Belisarius, through collapse under the incompetent five-general command and Totila's resurgence, to final success under Narses — framing it as a victory that came at too high a cost in men and treasure to be sustainable.

9.summary: The overarching military campaign that spans the entire episode. Brownworth traces the war's trajectory from near-victory under Belisarius, through collapse under the incompetent five-general command and Totila's resurgence, to final success under Narses — framing it as a victory that came at too high a cost in men and treasure to be sustainable.

It was one of the last of the many Gothic wars against the Byzantine Empire. The war had its roots in Justinian's ambition to recover the provinces of the former Western Roman Empire, which had been lost to invading barbarian tribes during the Migration Period.

events.name: Justinian's Reconquest of Italy
events.summary: Brownworth notes that as early as the sixth century, when Justinian reconquered Italy, there were those in the West who pointed out it felt more like a foreign invasion than a rescue — an early sign of the cultural and political rift that would eventually split Christendom.

17.summary: Brownworth notes that as early as the sixth century, when Justinian reconquered Italy, there were those in the West who pointed out it felt more like a foreign invasion than a rescue — an early sign of the cultural and political rift that would eventually split Christendom.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Gothic War between the Eastern Roman Empire during the reign of Emperor Justinian I and the Ostrogothic Kingdom of Italy took place from 535 to 554 in the Italian peninsula, Dalmatia, Sardinia, Sicily, and Corsica. It was one of the last of the many Gothic wars against the Byzantine Empire. The war had its roots in Justinian's ambition to recover the provinces of the former Western Roman Empire, which had been lost to invading barbarian tribes during the Migration Period.

events.name: Justinian's Rebuilding of the Church of the Holy Apostles
events.summary: Within 25 years of Constantine's death the golden dome of the Church of the Holy Apostles was in danger of collapse; his body was moved to a nearby church. After standing another 200 years, the church was completely demolished and rebuilt by Justinian, and Constantine's magnificent tomb and the apostolic sarcophagi disappeared entirely.

4.summary: Within 25 years of Constantine's death the golden dome of the Church of the Holy Apostles was in danger of collapse; his body was moved to a nearby church. After standing another 200 years, the church was completely demolished and rebuilt by Justinian, and Constantine's magnificent tomb and the apostolic sarcophagi disappeared entirely.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Church of the Holy Apostles, also known as the Imperial Polyandrion, was a Byzantine Eastern Orthodox church in Constantinople, capital of the Eastern Roman Empire. The first structure dated to the 4th century, though future emperors would add to and improve upon it. It was second in size and importance only to the Hagia Sophia among the great churches of the capital.

events.name: Gothic Siege of Rome
events.summary: Vittigis arrived with a Gothic army in March and laid siege to Rome for exactly one year and nine days, cutting all ten aqueducts and ending over a millennium of public fountains, plumbing, and hydraulic mills. Belisarius improvised by powering mills on the city's rivers and wrote to Justinian for reinforcements; 6,500 arrived early the following year, allowing him to go on the offensive.

8.summary: Vittigis arrived with a Gothic army in March and laid siege to Rome for exactly one year and nine days, cutting all ten aqueducts and ending over a millennium of public fountains, plumbing, and hydraulic mills. Belisarius improvised by powering mills on the city's rivers and wrote to Justinian for reinforcements; 6,500 arrived early the following year, allowing him to go on the offensive.

events.wikipedia_extract: The siege of Rome of 537–538 AD was the city's first siege during the Gothic War (535–554) between the defending Byzantine Empire's forces under the leadership of Belisarius against a numerically superior Ostrogothic (Goths) force under Vitigis. The siege was the first major encounter between the forces of the two opponents, and played a decisive role in the subsequent development of the war.

events.name: Byzantine-Persian War (Justinianic)
events.summary: After Khosrow sacked Antioch, Belisarius was sent east with every available soldier to confront him. He tricked a far superior Persian army, led by Khosrow himself, into retreating, and was preparing a massive assault on the Persian capital when the plague intervened and halted all operations.

8.summary: After Khosrow sacked Antioch, Belisarius was sent east with every available soldier to confront him. He tricked a far superior Persian army, led by Khosrow himself, into retreating, and was preparing a massive assault on the Persian capital when the plague intervened and halted all operations.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Lazic War, also known as the Colchidian War or in Georgian historiography as the Great War of Egrisi, was fought between the Byzantine Empire and the Sasanian Empire for control of the ancient Georgian region of Lazica. The Lazic War lasted for twenty years, from 541 to 562, and ended with the Fifty-Year Peace Treaty, which obligated the Byzantine Empire to pay tribute to Persia each year for the recognition of Lazica as a Byzantine vassal state by the Persians. The Lazic War is narrated in detail in the works of Procopius and Agathias.

events.name: Plague of Justinian
events.summary: Originating in Egypt, the Plague of Justinian swept through both the Byzantine and Persian empires in the mid-sixth century, killing an estimated one-quarter of the population across the affected regions. In Constantinople alone it raged for four months, with deaths reaching approximately 10,000 per day and some 300,000 people perishing in the city. The plague struck Justinian himself, triggering a succession crisis, and so devastated the empire's manpower and resources that his territorial reconquests proved impossible to sustain.

8.summary: Starting in Egypt, the bubonic plague swept through both the Persian and Byzantine empires. In Constantinople alone it raged for four months at 10,000 deaths per day, with corpses piled in an unused fortress until they reached the ceiling. Two-fifths of the city's population died — some 300,000 people — and most devastatingly, Justinian himself was struck, triggering a succession crisis.

9.summary: Mentioned as a devastating backdrop that decimated the Byzantine and Persian empires while barely affecting the more nomadic Moors, enabling the Moorish invasion of Africa. Brownworth returns to it at the episode's close, estimating it killed one-fourth of the population and made Justinian's reconquests ultimately impossible to hold.

events.wikipedia_extract: The plague of Justinian or Justinianic plague was an epidemic of plague that afflicted the entire Mediterranean Basin, Europe, and the Near East, especially the Sasanian Empire and the Byzantine Empire. The plague is named for the Byzantine Emperor Justinian I who, according to his court historian Procopius, contracted the disease and recovered in 542, at the height of the epidemic which killed about a fifth of the population in the imperial capital Constantinople. The contagion arrived in Roman Egypt in 541, spread around the Mediterranean Sea until 544, and persisted in Northern Europe and the Arabian Peninsula until 549. By 543, the plague had spread to every corner of Justinian's empire.

events.name: Disgrace of Belisarius
events.summary: After Justinian recovered from the plague and Theodora learned that Belisarius had participated in a meeting of eastern generals to protect the army's right to choose the next emperor, she took revenge. Belisarius was accused of keeping spoils from the African campaign, stripped of his command, his household disbanded, and his treasure confiscated — spending nearly a year in undeserved disgrace before a fully recovered Justinian pardoned him.

8.summary: After Justinian recovered from the plague and Theodora learned that Belisarius had participated in a meeting of eastern generals to protect the army's right to choose the next emperor, she took revenge. Belisarius was accused of keeping spoils from the African campaign, stripped of his command, his household disbanded, and his treasure confiscated — spending nearly a year in undeserved disgrace before a fully recovered Justinian pardoned him.

events.wikipedia_extract: Flavius Belisarius was a military commander of the Byzantine Empire under Emperor Justinian I. Belisarius was instrumental in the reconquest of much of the Mediterranean territory belonging to the former Western Roman Empire, which had been lost less than a century prior. He is considered one of the greatest military commanders of Byzantium and in history generally.

events.name: Fall of Naples to Totila
events.summary: After a prolonged siege in which no Byzantine general helped the city, Naples surrendered to Totila in May 543. Totila's merciful treatment — letting the garrison leave with ships and horses, and carefully nursing the starving citizens back to health — was a deliberate propaganda victory that cemented his image as liberator.

9.summary: After a prolonged siege in which no Byzantine general helped the city, Naples surrendered to Totila in May 543. Totila's merciful treatment — letting the garrison leave with ships and horses, and carefully nursing the starving citizens back to health — was a deliberate propaganda victory that cemented his image as liberator.

events.wikipedia_extract: Totila, original name Baduila, was the penultimate King of the Ostrogoths, reigning from 541 to 552. A skilled military and political leader, Totila reversed the tide of the Gothic War, recovering by 543 almost all the territories in Italy that the Eastern Roman Empire had captured from his Kingdom in 540.

events.name: Death of Theodora
events.summary: Theodora died of what Brownworth describes as probably cancer, leaving Justinian inconsolable. Her death had the paradoxical positive effect of freeing Justinian from her jealous intrigues against Belisarius — he promptly recalled the general and restored him to full favor, installing him as closest advisor and erecting a gilded statue of him.

9.summary: Theodora died of what Brownworth describes as probably cancer, leaving Justinian inconsolable. Her death had the paradoxical positive effect of freeing Justinian from her jealous intrigues against Belisarius — he promptly recalled the general and restored him to full favor, installing him as closest advisor and erecting a gilded statue of him.

events.wikipedia_extract: Theodora was Eastern Roman empress and wife of emperor Justinian I. She was of humble origins and became empress when her husband became emperor in 527. Theodora was one of his chief political advisers. She is recognized as a saint in the Oriental Orthodox Churches, and commemorated on 14 November.

events.name: Battle of Busta Gallorum (Narses defeats Totila)
events.summary: The decisive encounter of the Gothic War. Totila attacked first with cavalry but was repulsed by Byzantine archers; the fleeing cavalry trampled their own troops. Totila himself was killed in the chaos along with 6,000 of his soldiers, ending effective Gothic resistance. The Goths elected another king who held out only a few more months before being killed in October.

9.summary: The decisive encounter of the Gothic War. Totila attacked first with cavalry but was repulsed by Byzantine archers; the fleeing cavalry trampled their own troops. Totila himself was killed in the chaos along with 6,000 of his soldiers, ending effective Gothic resistance. The Goths elected another king who held out only a few more months before being killed in October.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Taginae or Battle of Busta Gallorum took place in July of 552 AD, where Byzantines under General Narses defeated the Ostrogoths (Goths) under King Totila. The Byzantine victory paved the way for the Byzantine reconquest of the Italian Peninsula and the dissolution of the Gothic kingdom.

events.name: Byzantine Conquest of Southern Spain
events.summary: Two emissaries from orthodox Spain appealed to Justinian for help against their Arian king. With only a few hundred men under the octogenarian general Liberius, Justinian seized the opportunity; Liberius played the Romanized populace against the Arian nobility and conquered about an eighth of Spain — described as an excellent base for future conquest that never came.

9.summary: Two emissaries from orthodox Spain appealed to Justinian for help against their Arian king. With only a few hundred men under the octogenarian general Liberius, Justinian seized the opportunity; Liberius played the Romanized populace against the Arian nobility and conquered about an eighth of Spain — described as an excellent base for future conquest that never came.

events.wikipedia_extract: Spania was a province of the Eastern Roman Empire from 552 until 624 in the south of the Iberian Peninsula and the Balearic Islands. It was established by the Emperor Justinian I in an effort to restore the western provinces of the Empire.

events.name: Collapse of the Hagia Sophia Dome
events.summary: In 558 the dome of the Hagia Sophia collapsed, and Justinian spent the next five years repairing it — Brownworth noting this was no ordinary repair project given the church's status as the beating heart of the empire.

9.summary: In 558 the dome of the Hagia Sophia collapsed, and Justinian spent the next five years repairing it — Brownworth noting this was no ordinary repair project given the church's status as the beating heart of the empire.

events.wikipedia_extract: Hagia Sophia, officially the Hagia Sophia Grand Mosque, is a mosque and a major cultural and historical site in Istanbul, Turkey. It was formerly a church (360–1453) and a museum (1935–2020). The last of three church buildings to be successively erected on the site by the Eastern Roman Empire, it was completed in AD 537, becoming the world's largest interior space and among the first to employ a fully pendentive dome. It is considered the epitome of Byzantine architecture and is said to have "changed the history of architecture". From its dedication in 360 until 1453 Hagia Sophia served as the cathedral of Constantinople in the Byzantine liturgical tradition, except for the period 1204–1261 when the Latin Crusaders installed their own hierarchy. After the fall of Constantinople in 1453, it served as a mosque, having its minarets added soon after. The site became a museum in 1935, and was redesignated as a mosque in 2020.

events.name: Kutrigur Hun Raid on Constantinople
events.summary: A group of Huns called the Kutrigurs (Khatrigers), concluding the empire was vulnerable, overran the frontier and came within 30 miles of Constantinople. Justinian recalled the retired Belisarius who, with only a few hundred improvised guards and volunteers, ambushed and crippled the Hunnic army; Justinian then personally assumed command and bribed them to leave, then paid a rival tribe to destroy the Kutrigurs.

9.summary: A group of Huns called the Kutrigurs (Khatrigers), concluding the empire was vulnerable, overran the frontier and came within 30 miles of Constantinople. Justinian recalled the retired Belisarius who, with only a few hundred improvised guards and volunteers, ambushed and crippled the Hunnic army; Justinian then personally assumed command and bribed them to leave, then paid a rival tribe to destroy the Kutrigurs.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Kutrigurs were a Turkic nomadic equestrian tribe who flourished on the Pontic–Caspian steppe in the 6th century AD. To their east were the similar Utigurs and both possibly were closely related to the Bulgars. They warred with the Byzantine Empire and the Utigurs. Towards the end of the 6th century they were absorbed by the Pannonian Avars under pressure from other Turkic groups.

events.name: Death of Justinian I
events.summary: Justinian died of a heart attack on November 14, 565, at the age of 83, eight months after Belisarius. Brownworth frames his death as the end of an age — after 38 years on the throne, never again would the empire be ruled by such a visionary, nor by a man whose first language was Latin.

9.summary: Justinian died of a heart attack on November 14, 565, at the age of 83, eight months after Belisarius. Brownworth frames his death as the end of an age — after 38 years on the throne, never again would the empire be ruled by such a visionary, nor by a man whose first language was Latin.

events.wikipedia_extract: Justinian I, also known as Justinian the Great, was Roman emperor from 527 to 565.

events.name: Lombard Invasion of Italy
events.summary: According to legend, the eunuch Narses, humiliated by the Empress Sophia, invited the Lombards into Italy in revenge. The resulting long and bitter war cost the empire most of the land of its birth, with the Lombards continuing their relentless conquest of the Italian peninsula throughout the subsequent reigns.

10.summary: According to legend, the eunuch Narses, humiliated by the Empress Sophia, invited the Lombards into Italy in revenge. The resulting long and bitter war cost the empire most of the land of its birth, with the Lombards continuing their relentless conquest of the Italian peninsula throughout the subsequent reigns.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Lombards, Longobards or Langobards were a Germanic people who conquered most of the Italian Peninsula between 568 and 774 AD. They had previously settled in the Middle Danube in the 5th century, near what is now Austria, Slovenia and Hungary. Still earlier they lived further north, near present day Hamburg. Roman-era historians in the 1st and 2nd centuries AD mention the Lombards as one of the Suebian peoples, and report them living on the Lower Elbe already in the early 1st century. Their legacy is apparent in Lombardy, northern Italy, the region deriving its name from them.

events.name: Coronation of Heraclius
events.summary: At thirty-six, Heraclius was crowned emperor together with his beautiful wife Eudosia in a dazzling ceremony. Brownworth notes that despite the pomp and splendor, there was a sense of impending doom — the rest of the empire was falling away even as the new imperial couple was celebrated.

10.summary: At thirty-six, Heraclius was crowned emperor together with his beautiful wife Eudosia in a dazzling ceremony. Brownworth notes that despite the pomp and splendor, there was a sense of impending doom — the rest of the empire was falling away even as the new imperial couple was celebrated.

events.wikipedia_extract: Heraclius was Byzantine emperor from 610 to 641. His rise to power began in 608, when he and his father, Heraclius the Elder, the Exarch of Africa, led a revolt against the unpopular emperor Phocas.

events.name: Heraclius' Military Reforms
events.summary: Over the twelve years between his coronation and his first campaign, Heraclius reorganized the military, creating what Brownworth calls the medieval Byzantine army. Frontier soldiers were given a limited salary in exchange for presenting themselves armed and mounted, eliminating dependence on expensive and unreliable mercenaries. This system became the backbone of the army for the next 800 years.

10.summary: Over the twelve years between his coronation and his first campaign, Heraclius reorganized the military, creating what Brownworth calls the medieval Byzantine army. Frontier soldiers were given a limited salary in exchange for presenting themselves armed and mounted, eliminating dependence on expensive and unreliable mercenaries. This system became the backbone of the army for the next 800 years.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Byzantine army was the primary military body of the Byzantine armed forces, serving alongside the Byzantine navy. A direct continuation of the Eastern Roman army, shaping and developing itself on the legacy of the late Hellenistic armies, it maintained a similar level of discipline, strategic prowess and organization. It was among the most effective armies of western Eurasia for much of the Middle Ages. Over time the cavalry arm became more prominent in the Byzantine army as the legion system disappeared in the early 7th century. Later reforms reflected some Germanic and Asian influences—rival forces frequently became sources of mercenary units, such as the Huns, Cumans, Alans and Turks, meeting the Empire's demand for light cavalry mercenaries. Since much of the Byzantine military focused on the strategy and skill of generals utilizing militia troops, heavy infantry were recruited from Frankish and later Varangian mercenaries.

events.name: Overthrow of Phocas
events.summary: A mob seized Phocas and brought him to Heraclius' ship in the harbor of Constantinople. Heraclius reportedly asked, 'Is this how you governed, wretch?' to which Phocas replied, 'Will you do any better?' Phocas was then executed, ending his disastrous eight-year reign.

10.summary: A mob seized Phocas and brought him to Heraclius' ship in the harbor of Constantinople. Heraclius reportedly asked, 'Is this how you governed, wretch?' to which Phocas replied, 'Will you do any better?' Phocas was then executed, ending his disastrous eight-year reign.

events.wikipedia_extract: Phocas was Eastern Roman emperor from 602 to 610. Initially a middle-ranking officer in the Roman army, Phocas rose to prominence as a spokesman for dissatisfied soldiers in their disputes with the court of the Emperor Maurice. When the army rebelled in 602, Phocas emerged as the leader of the mutiny. The revolt led to the overthrow and execution of Maurice in November 602.

events.name: Persian Conquest of Jerusalem
events.summary: The Persians conquered Jerusalem, capturing the True Cross and instruments of the Passion, killing all male citizens, and selling the rest into slavery, leaving the holy city almost deserted. Brownworth calls this among the most devastating blows of the Persian advance.

10.summary: The Persians conquered Jerusalem, capturing the True Cross and instruments of the Passion, killing all male citizens, and selling the rest into slavery, leaving the holy city almost deserted. Brownworth calls this among the most devastating blows of the Persian advance.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Sasanian conquest of Jerusalem in early 614 was a significant development in the Byzantine–Sasanian War of 602–628. It was the result of a major offensive by the Sasanian Empire across the Fertile Crescent, culminating in the annexation of Jerusalem and Palaestina Prima as a whole. The Sasanian advance had been bolstered by the timely outbreak of the Jewish revolt against Heraclius, owing to decades of persecution of Jews and Samaritans by the Byzantine Empire, although the Heraclian dynasty itself had only been in power for four years.

events.name: Persian Conquest of Egypt
events.summary: Two years after taking Jerusalem, the Persians turned to Egypt, depriving the empire of its main source of bread. Combined with the loss of Thrace to the Avars, this created a devastating famine. The end of the empire seemed at hand.

10.summary: Two years after taking Jerusalem, the Persians turned to Egypt, depriving the empire of its main source of bread. Combined with the loss of Thrace to the Avars, this created a devastating famine. The end of the empire seemed at hand.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Sasanian conquest of Egypt took place between 618 and 621 CE, when the Sassanid Empire defeated the Byzantine forces in Egypt and occupied the province. The fall of Alexandria, the capital of Roman Egypt, marked the first and most important stage in the Sasanian campaign to conquer the province, which eventually fell completely under Persian rule within a couple of years.

events.name: Heraclius' Persian Campaign Begins
events.summary: In spring 622, Heraclius launched his counter-offensive against Persia, using Byzantine naval superiority to land at Issus and surprise the Persians who expected a land march. He spent the summer drilling his troops, and in the first engagement scattered the Persians 'like a herd of goats,' dramatically boosting Byzantine morale.

10.summary: In spring 622, Heraclius launched his counter-offensive against Persia, using Byzantine naval superiority to land at Issus and surprise the Persians who expected a land march. He spent the summer drilling his troops, and in the first engagement scattered the Persians 'like a herd of goats,' dramatically boosting Byzantine morale.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Byzantine–Sasanian War of 602–628, also called the Last Great War of Antiquity, was fought between the Byzantine Empire and the Sasanian Empire. It was the final and most devastating conflict of the Roman–Persian wars. The previous war between the two powers had ended in 591 after the emperor Maurice helped the Sasanian king Khosrow II regain his throne. In 602, Maurice was murdered by his political rival Phocas. Khosrow declared war, ostensibly to avenge the death of the deposed emperor Maurice. This became a decades-long conflict, the longest war in the series, and was fought throughout the Middle East, the Aegean Sea, and before the walls of Constantinople itself.

events.name: Hijra: Muhammad Flees to Medina
events.summary: In 622 — the same year Heraclius set out on campaign — Muhammad fled from Mecca to Medina. Brownworth identifies this as the birth of the Islamic threat, noting that within five years Muhammad had unleashed the armies of Islam, and within a hundred years of his death in 632 they were at the gates of Constantinople and within 150 miles of Paris.

10.summary: In 622 — the same year Heraclius set out on campaign — Muhammad fled from Mecca to Medina. Brownworth identifies this as the birth of the Islamic threat, noting that within five years Muhammad had unleashed the armies of Islam, and within a hundred years of his death in 632 they were at the gates of Constantinople and within 150 miles of Paris.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Hijrah, also Hegira, was the journey the Islamic prophet Muhammad and his followers took from Mecca to Medina. The year in which the Hijrah took place is also identified as the epoch of the Lunar Hijri and Solar Hijri calendars; its date equates to 16 July 622 in the Julian calendar.

events.name: Heraclius Destroys the Fire Temple at Ganzak
events.summary: Heraclius invaded Azerbaijan, the center of Persian Zoroastrian fire worship, where Khosrow had wintered with an army 40,000 strong. The Persians melted away, leaving the palace and the great fire temple at the mercy of the Byzantines. Heraclius destroyed both, along with the nearby town identified as the birthplace of Zoroaster, as revenge for the Persian sack of Jerusalem.

10.summary: Heraclius invaded Azerbaijan, the center of Persian Zoroastrian fire worship, where Khosrow had wintered with an army 40,000 strong. The Persians melted away, leaving the palace and the great fire temple at the mercy of the Byzantines. Heraclius destroyed both, along with the nearby town identified as the birthplace of Zoroaster, as revenge for the Persian sack of Jerusalem.

events.wikipedia_extract: Adur Gushnasp was the name of a Zoroastrian sacred fire of the highest grade, which served as one of the three most sacred fires of pre-Islamic Iran; the two others being the Adur Farnbag and Adur Burzen-Mihr. Out of the three, Adur Gushnasp is the only fire whose temple structure has been discovered and for which archaeological, sigillographical, and textual evidence are all available.

events.name: Avar-Persian Siege of Constantinople
events.summary: With Heraclius away campaigning, Khosrow sent Shahrbaraz with an army to assist the Avars, who dragged massive siege engines toward Constantinople's walls. Some 80,000 barbarians besieged the city, defended by only 12,000 cavalry and all the city's citizens. The Patriarch Sergius processed daily around the walls with an icon of the Virgin Mary. The siege failed: the enemy fleet was ambushed and destroyed, Theodore crushed the Persian general Shaheen, and the Avars retreated.

10.summary: With Heraclius away campaigning, Khosrow sent Shahrbaraz with an army to assist the Avars, who dragged massive siege engines toward Constantinople's walls. Some 80,000 barbarians besieged the city, defended by only 12,000 cavalry and all the city's citizens. The Patriarch Sergius processed daily around the walls with an icon of the Virgin Mary. The siege failed: the enemy fleet was ambushed and destroyed, Theodore crushed the Persian general Shaheen, and the Avars retreated.

events.wikipedia_extract: The siege of Constantinople in 626 by the Sassanid Persians and Avars, aided by large numbers of allied Slavs, ended in a strategic victory for the Byzantines. The failure of the siege saved the empire from collapse, and, combined with other victories achieved by Emperor Heraclius the previous year and in 627, enabled Byzantium to regain its territories and end the destructive Roman–Persian Wars by enforcing a treaty with borders status quo c. 590.

events.name: Battle of Nineveh
events.summary: The decisive battle of the Byzantine-Persian war, fought at ancient Nineveh over eleven hours. Both sides knew it was the decisive conflict. Heraclius reportedly challenged the Persian general to single combat and killed him. By day's end the Byzantines had won completely — virtually no Persians survived. Several days later Heraclius intercepted and forged a letter from Khosrow to keep Shahrbaraz out of the fight.

10.summary: The decisive battle of the Byzantine-Persian war, fought at ancient Nineveh over eleven hours. Both sides knew it was the decisive conflict. Heraclius reportedly challenged the Persian general to single combat and killed him. By day's end the Byzantines had won completely — virtually no Persians survived. Several days later Heraclius intercepted and forged a letter from Khosrow to keep Shahrbaraz out of the fight.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Nineveh was the climactic battle of the Byzantine–Sasanian War of 602–628.

events.name: Fall of Khosrow II
events.summary: After a series of Byzantine forgeries added 400 officers' names to an execution order meant for Shahrbaraz, Persian nobles revolted and Khosrow's son rose in revolt with widespread support. Khosrow was flung into the Tower of Darkness, starved, forced to watch all his unrelated children executed before him, and finally shot slowly to death with arrows. The new Persian king immediately sued for peace.

10.summary: After a series of Byzantine forgeries added 400 officers' names to an execution order meant for Shahrbaraz, Persian nobles revolted and Khosrow's son rose in revolt with widespread support. Khosrow was flung into the Tower of Darkness, starved, forced to watch all his unrelated children executed before him, and finally shot slowly to death with arrows. The new Persian king immediately sued for peace.

events.wikipedia_extract: Khosrow II, commonly known as Khosrow Parviz, is considered to be the last great monarch of pre-Islamic Iran, ruling the Sasanian Empire from 590 to 628, including an interruption of one year.

events.name: Greek Declared Official Language of the Empire
events.summary: Heraclius made Greek the official language of the empire, sweeping away the old Latin trappings in the interest of military efficiency. Within a generation, Latin was virtually extinct in the empire, and imperial titles changed from Imperator, Caesar, and Augustus to Basilius — the Greek word for king. Brownworth calls this a turning point marking Heraclius as the first Greek emperor.

10.summary: Heraclius made Greek the official language of the empire, sweeping away the old Latin trappings in the interest of military efficiency. Within a generation, Latin was virtually extinct in the empire, and imperial titles changed from Imperator, Caesar, and Augustus to Basilius — the Greek word for king. Brownworth calls this a turning point marking Heraclius as the first Greek emperor.

events.wikipedia_extract: Heraclius was Byzantine emperor from 610 to 641. His rise to power began in 608, when he and his father, Heraclius the Elder, the Exarch of Africa, led a revolt against the unpopular emperor Phocas.

events.name: True Cross Returned to Jerusalem
events.summary: As part of the Persian peace settlement, the True Cross and instruments of the Passion were surrendered along with all conquered territory and prisoners. Heraclius waited for the True Cross to be brought from Persia before entering Constantinople, following it through the Golden Gate in a procession featuring the first elephants ever seen in the city, then raising it above the high altar at the Hagia Sophia. He subsequently installed it in Jerusalem.

10.summary: As part of the Persian peace settlement, the True Cross and instruments of the Passion were surrendered along with all conquered territory and prisoners. Heraclius waited for the True Cross to be brought from Persia before entering Constantinople, following it through the Golden Gate in a procession featuring the first elephants ever seen in the city, then raising it above the high altar at the Hagia Sophia. He subsequently installed it in Jerusalem.

events.wikipedia_extract: In Christian tradition, the True Cross is the original wooden cross on which Jesus of Nazareth was crucified.

10.text: Jerusalem had been avenged, but the True Cross had not been recovered.
The True Cross was enshrined, and the Lord's enemies lay scattered before it.
to slip into Jerusalem to remove the True Cross, and started on the long road to Constantinople.

events.name: Rise of Islam and Muslim Conquests
events.summary: After Muhammad's death in 632, Muslim forces crossed into Byzantine territory by 633 and poured into Syria, conquering Damascus and besieging Jerusalem. The 80,000-strong Byzantine army sent against them was massacred in a sandstorm, and Damascus and Jerusalem fell. Brownworth attributes much of Islam's rapid success to the timing — both Persia and Byzantium were exhausted from their long war.

10.summary: After Muhammad's death in 632, Muslim forces crossed into Byzantine territory by 633 and poured into Syria, conquering Damascus and besieging Jerusalem. The 80,000-strong Byzantine army sent against them was massacred in a sandstorm, and Damascus and Jerusalem fell. Brownworth attributes much of Islam's rapid success to the timing — both Persia and Byzantium were exhausted from their long war.

events.wikipedia_extract: The early Muslim conquests or early Islamic conquests, also known as the Arab conquests, were a series of religious wars initiated by Muhammad, the prophet of Islam, and continued by the early Muslims. In 622, he established the first Islamic state at Medina in Arabia, from where Muslim armies expanded rapidly under the succeeding Rashidun Caliphate and then the Umayyad Caliphate, culminating in Islamic law being extended throughout most of West Asia and North Africa, parts of South Asia and Central Asia, and parts of Mediterranean Europe over the following century. According to Scottish historian James Buchan: "In speed and extent, the first Arab conquests were matched only by those of Alexander the Great, and they were more lasting." At the height of the expansion, Muslim-ruled territory under the caliphates is estimated to have covered 13,000,000 square kilometres (5,000,000 sq mi), stretching from the Indian subcontinent in the east to the Iberian Peninsula in the west.

events.name: Muslim Conquest of Damascus and Jerusalem
events.summary: Muslim forces conquered Damascus, besieged Jerusalem, and ultimately took both cities. The Byzantine army sent to stop them was massacred almost to a man after being goaded into attacking in a sandstorm that blew in their faces. Heraclius personally slipped into Jerusalem to remove the True Cross before beginning the long retreat to Constantinople.

10.summary: Muslim forces conquered Damascus, besieged Jerusalem, and ultimately took both cities. The Byzantine army sent to stop them was massacred almost to a man after being goaded into attacking in a sandstorm that blew in their faces. Heraclius personally slipped into Jerusalem to remove the True Cross before beginning the long retreat to Constantinople.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Rashidun Caliphate conquered Byzantine Syria between 634–638 CE as part of the Arab–Byzantine wars and the wider Muslim conquests.

events.name: Arab Conquest of Alexandria
events.summary: Alexandria was sacked by the Arabs in 638 and retaken eight years later by Caliph Omar after a failed Byzantine counteroffensive. Omar razed the walls, moved the capital to Fustat (later Cairo), and allegedly ordered the destruction of the famous Library of Alexandria — though Brownworth is skeptical of the story, noting it dates 450 years after the fact and may be Christian propaganda.

11.summary: Alexandria was sacked by the Arabs in 638 and retaken eight years later by Caliph Omar after a failed Byzantine counteroffensive. Omar razed the walls, moved the capital to Fustat (later Cairo), and allegedly ordered the destruction of the famous Library of Alexandria — though Brownworth is skeptical of the story, noting it dates 450 years after the fact and may be Christian propaganda.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Arab conquest of Egypt, or Muslim conquest of Egypt, was a military campaign led by Amr ibn al-As of the Rashidun Caliphate against the Byzantine Empire between 639 and 642 AD. It ended the Roman period in Egypt, which had begun in 30 BC and lasted for approximately seven centuries, and more broadly concluded the Greco-Roman period of Egyptian history, which had endured for nearly a millennium.

events.name: Monothelite/Single-Energy Controversy
events.summary: Heraclius proposed a Christological compromise — Christ had two natures but one energy — that both sides initially accepted, recognizing they were saying the same thing in different words. However, Patriarch Sophronius of Jerusalem attacked it as heresy, dissolving support precisely when unity was most needed. At the end of his life, the Pope officially condemned the compromise, and the dying Heraclius formally denied any part in it, blaming Patriarch Sergius.

10.summary: Heraclius proposed a Christological compromise — Christ had two natures but one energy — that both sides initially accepted, recognizing they were saying the same thing in different words. However, Patriarch Sophronius of Jerusalem attacked it as heresy, dissolving support precisely when unity was most needed. At the end of his life, the Pope officially condemned the compromise, and the dying Heraclius formally denied any part in it, blaming Patriarch Sergius.

events.wikipedia_extract: Monothelitism, or monotheletism, is a theological doctrine in Christianity that was proposed in the 7th century, but was ultimately rejected by the sixth ecumenical council. It held Christ as having only one will and was thus contrary to dyothelitism, the Christological doctrine accepted by most Christian denominations, which holds Christ as having two wills. Historically, monothelitism was closely related to monoenergism, a theological doctrine that holds Jesus Christ as having only one energy. Both doctrines were at the center of Christological disputes during the 7th century.

events.name: Arab Capture of Rhodes
events.summary: Rhodes fell to Arab forces four years after Cyprus. The victorious Arab commander broke up the famous Colossus — which had already lain fallen since an earthquake 900 years earlier — and sold it for scrap, requiring 900 camels to haul away the pieces.

11.summary: Rhodes fell to Arab forces four years after Cyprus. The victorious Arab commander broke up the famous Colossus — which had already lain fallen since an earthquake 900 years earlier — and sold it for scrap, requiring 900 camels to haul away the pieces.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Colossus of Rhodes was a statue of the Greek sun god Helios, erected in the city of Rhodes, on the Greek island of the same name, by Chares of Lindos in 280 BC. One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, it was constructed to celebrate the successful defence of Rhodes against an attack by Demetrius I of Macedon, who had besieged it for a year with a large army and navy.

events.name: First Arab Siege of Constantinople
events.summary: By 672, Arab forces were at the gates of Constantinople and a five-year siege began. It was ultimately unsuccessful largely due to the recently invented Greek fire — a flammable material that could burn even in water and could be sprayed at enemy ships — whose exact composition remains a state secret to this day.

11.summary: By 672, Arab forces were at the gates of Constantinople and a five-year siege began. It was ultimately unsuccessful largely due to the recently invented Greek fire — a flammable material that could burn even in water and could be sprayed at enemy ships — whose exact composition remains a state secret to this day.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Arab–Byzantine wars or Muslim–Byzantine wars were a series of wars from the 7th to 11th centuries between the successive Islamic caliphates and the Byzantine Empire. Following the Byzantine defeat at the Battle of the Yarmuk, Muslim armies conquered most Byzantine territory in the Levant, Egypt and North Africa within decades. Arab expansion subsequently slowed to a more gradual rate, following two failed sieges of the Byzantine capital of Constantinople in the late 7th and early 8th centuries. By the mid-9th century, the Byzantines had partially recovered and proceeded to recapture some of their lost territory in Anatolia in the following decades.

events.name: Invention of Greek Fire
events.summary: A secret Byzantine weapon made of a flammable material that would burn even in water, it could be sprayed or pumped at enemy ships with devastating results. Brownworth notes it was considered such a state secret that even today the exact composition is unknown. It was a decisive factor in repelling the Arab siege of Constantinople.

11.summary: A secret Byzantine weapon made of a flammable material that would burn even in water, it could be sprayed or pumped at enemy ships with devastating results. Brownworth notes it was considered such a state secret that even today the exact composition is unknown. It was a decisive factor in repelling the Arab siege of Constantinople.

events.wikipedia_extract: Greek fire was an incendiary weapon used by the Byzantine Empire from the seventh to the fourteenth centuries. The recipe for Greek fire was a closely-guarded state secret. Historians have variously speculated that it was based on saltpeter, sulfur, or quicklime, but most modern scholars agree that it was based on petroleum mixed with resins, comparable in composition to modern napalm. Byzantine sailors would toss grenades loaded with Greek fire onto enemy ships or spray it from tubes. Its ability to burn on water made it an effective and destructive naval incendiary weapon, and rival powers tried unsuccessfully to copy the material.

events.name: Second Arab Siege of Constantinople
events.summary: Forty years after the first failed siege, the Arabs returned with 80,000 men and 1,800 ships. Leo III outsmarted the besiegers at every turn and induced the Bulgarians — traditional enemies of the empire — to deliver the killing blow. Fewer than half the Arab invaders made it back to Damascus, and of 1,800 ships only five survived Greek fire and winter storms.

11.summary: Forty years after the first failed siege, the Arabs returned with 80,000 men and 1,800 ships. Leo III outsmarted the besiegers at every turn and induced the Bulgarians — traditional enemies of the empire — to deliver the killing blow. Fewer than half the Arab invaders made it back to Damascus, and of 1,800 ships only five survived Greek fire and winter storms.

events.wikipedia_extract: In 717–718, Constantinople, the capital of the Byzantine Empire, was besieged by the Muslim Arabs of the Umayyad Caliphate. The campaign marked the culmination of twenty years of attacks and progressive Arab occupation of the Byzantine borderlands, while Byzantine strength was sapped by prolonged internal turmoil. In 716, after years of preparations, the Arabs, led by Maslama ibn Abd al-Malik, invaded Byzantine Asia Minor. The Arabs initially hoped to exploit Byzantine civil strife and made common cause with the general Leo III the Isaurian, who had risen up against Emperor Theodosius III. Leo, however, deceived them and secured the Byzantine throne for himself.

events.name: Birth of the Venetian Republic
events.summary: In response to Leo III's iconoclast edict, the horrified citizens of the Venetian lagoon rebelled and appointed a local leader as Doge — giving birth to the Venetian Republic, which Brownworth describes as both an ally and inveterate enemy of the Byzantine Empire.

11.summary: In response to Leo III's iconoclast edict, the horrified citizens of the Venetian lagoon rebelled and appointed a local leader as Doge — giving birth to the Venetian Republic, which Brownworth describes as both an ally and inveterate enemy of the Byzantine Empire.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Republic of Venice, officially the Most Serene Republic of Venice and traditionally known as La Serenissima, was a sovereign state and maritime republic with its capital in Venice, on the northeastern Italian coast. Founded, according to tradition, in 697 by Paolo Lucio Anafesto, over the course of its 1,100 years of history it established itself as one of the major European commercial and naval powers. Initially extended in the Dogado area, during its history it annexed a large part of Northeast Italy, Istria, Dalmatia, the coasts of present-day Montenegro and Albania as well as numerous islands in the Adriatic and eastern Ionian seas. At the height of its expansion, between the 13th and 16th centuries, it also governed Crete, Cyprus, the Peloponnese, a number of Greek islands, as well as several cities and ports in the eastern Mediterranean.

events.name: Iconoclastic Controversy
events.summary: Brownworth identifies the iconoclastic controversy — which declared religious art synonymous with idolatry — as the event that drove the first real wedge between the Eastern and Western churches, poisoning relations and opening the floodgates of excommunications and counter-excommunications.

17.summary: Brownworth identifies the iconoclastic controversy — which declared religious art synonymous with idolatry — as the event that drove the first real wedge between the Eastern and Western churches, poisoning relations and opening the floodgates of excommunications and counter-excommunications.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Byzantine Iconoclasm are two periods in the history of the Byzantine Empire when the use of religious images or icons was opposed by religious and imperial authorities within the Ecumenical Patriarchate and the temporal imperial hierarchy. The First Iconoclasm, as it is sometimes called, occurred between about 726 and 787, while the Second Iconoclasm occurred between 814 and 842. According to the traditional view, Byzantine Iconoclasm was started by a ban on religious images promulgated by the Byzantine Emperor Leo III the Isaurian, and continued under his successors. It was accompanied by widespread destruction of religious images and persecution of supporters of the veneration of images. The Papacy remained firmly in support of the use of religious images throughout the period, and the whole episode widened the growing divergence between the Byzantine and Carolingian traditions in what was still a unified European Church, as well as facilitating the reduction or removal of Byzantine political control over parts of the Italian Peninsula.

17.text: 200 years later the iconoclastic controversy

events.name: Iconoclasm Edict of Leo III
events.summary: After beginning with sermons against icons and then ordering the destruction of the prominent golden icon of Christ at the Chalke Gate, Leo III issued a formal edict declaring that all holy images were to be destroyed. This caused an open breach between the eastern and western churches and triggered widespread riots across the empire.

11.summary: After beginning with sermons against icons and then ordering the destruction of the prominent golden icon of Christ at the Chalke Gate, Leo III issued a formal edict declaring that all holy images were to be destroyed. This caused an open breach between the eastern and western churches and triggered widespread riots across the empire.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Byzantine Iconoclasm are two periods in the history of the Byzantine Empire when the use of religious images or icons was opposed by religious and imperial authorities within the Ecumenical Patriarchate and the temporal imperial hierarchy. The First Iconoclasm, as it is sometimes called, occurred between about 726 and 787, while the Second Iconoclasm occurred between 814 and 842. According to the traditional view, Byzantine Iconoclasm was started by a ban on religious images promulgated by the Byzantine Emperor Leo III the Isaurian, and continued under his successors. It was accompanied by widespread destruction of religious images and persecution of supporters of the veneration of images. The Papacy remained firmly in support of the use of religious images throughout the period, and the whole episode widened the growing divergence between the Byzantine and Carolingian traditions in what was still a unified European Church, as well as facilitating the reduction or removal of Byzantine political control over parts of the Italian Peninsula.

events.name: Battle of Tours
events.summary: The battle at which Islamic expansion into Europe was finally halted, mentioned by Brownworth as the ultimate consequence of Constantinople's walls: by blocking the direct route, the walls forced Islamic armies through North Africa and Spain, buying time for Europe to emerge from its dark ages before the final confrontation.

4.summary: The battle at which Islamic expansion into Europe was finally halted, mentioned by Brownworth as the ultimate consequence of Constantinople's walls: by blocking the direct route, the walls forced Islamic armies through North Africa and Spain, buying time for Europe to emerge from its dark ages before the final confrontation.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Tours, also called the Battle of Poitiers and the Battle of the Highway of the Martyrs, was fought on 10 October 732, and was an important battle during the Umayyad invasion of Gaul. It resulted in victory for the Frankish and Aquitanian forces, led by Charles Martel, over the invading Umayyad forces, led by Abd al-Rahman al-Ghafiqi, governor of al-Andalus. Many historians, including Edward Gibbon, have credited the Christian victory as an important factor in curtailing the spread of Islam in Western Europe.

4.text: And by the time they were stopped at the Battle of Tours, Europe had emerged from its dark

events.name: Fall of Ravenna to the Lombards
events.summary: The Lombard king captured Ravenna in 751, taking the last imperial stronghold in Italy. This forced the pope — who had no desire for help from the iconoclast emperor in Constantinople — to cross the Alps and seek the protection of the Frankish king Pepin instead.

11.summary: The Lombard king captured Ravenna in 751, taking the last imperial stronghold in Italy. This forced the pope — who had no desire for help from the iconoclast emperor in Constantinople — to cross the Alps and seek the protection of the Frankish king Pepin instead.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Lombards, Longobards or Langobards were a Germanic people who conquered most of the Italian Peninsula between 568 and 774 AD. They had previously settled in the Middle Danube in the 5th century, near what is now Austria, Slovenia and Hungary. Still earlier they lived further north, near present day Hamburg. Roman-era historians in the 1st and 2nd centuries AD mention the Lombards as one of the Suebian peoples, and report them living on the Lower Elbe already in the early 1st century. Their legacy is apparent in Lombardy, northern Italy, the region deriving its name from them.

events.name: Creation of the Papal States
events.summary: After Pepin the Short swept into Italy and brought the Lombards to their knees, he turned over a large swath of conquered territory to the pope, bringing the Papal States into existence. Brownworth notes that in vastly shrunken form they still exist today as Vatican City.

11.summary: After Pepin the Short swept into Italy and brought the Lombards to their knees, he turned over a large swath of conquered territory to the pope, bringing the Papal States into existence. Brownworth notes that in vastly shrunken form they still exist today as Vatican City.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Donation of Pepin, or Donation of Pippin, was the transfer of Frankish territory in central Italy to Pope Stephen II made by Pepin the Short, King of the Franks, in 756. Credited with "effectively establishing the temporal authority of the papacy," the Donation took place amid the Byzantine Empire's decline in Italy and marked both "an important moment in the papal–Carolingian alliance" and an "important step" in the formation of the Papal States. With the fall of the imperial capital in Italy, Ravenna, to Aistulf, King of the Lombards in 751, "any semblance of imperial protection for Rome" had evaporated and the pope, who had technically been a Byzantine subject to this point, turned to Pepin for assistance. Pepin invaded Italy and, following an initial victory in 755, he decisively "defeated Aistulf and imposed a peace on him" in 756.

events.name: Second Council of Nicaea
events.summary: Called by Irene as the eighth and final ecumenical council of the church, it officially condemned iconoclasm. For Irene it was a triumphal moment, though it was somewhat spoiled by the growing problem of her son Constantine VI reaching adulthood and threatening her grip on power.

11.summary: Called by Irene as the eighth and final ecumenical council of the church, it officially condemned iconoclasm. For Irene it was a triumphal moment, though it was somewhat spoiled by the growing problem of her son Constantine VI reaching adulthood and threatening her grip on power.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Second Council of Nicaea is recognized as the last of the first seven ecumenical councils by the Eastern Orthodox Church and the Catholic Church. In addition, it is also recognized as such by Old Catholics and others. Protestant opinions on it are varied.

events.name: Blinding and Death of Constantine VI
events.summary: After ambushing Constantine VI as he returned from the races at the Hippodrome, Irene had him brought back to the imperial palace and blinded in the very room in which he had been born 27 years earlier. The blinding was so brutal that he died. Brownworth calls it perhaps the cruelest murder that even Byzantine history has to offer, one that left a permanent stain on Irene's reputation.

11.summary: After ambushing Constantine VI as he returned from the races at the Hippodrome, Irene had him brought back to the imperial palace and blinded in the very room in which he had been born 27 years earlier. The blinding was so brutal that he died. Brownworth calls it perhaps the cruelest murder that even Byzantine history has to offer, one that left a permanent stain on Irene's reputation.

events.wikipedia_extract: Constantine VI, sometimes called the Blind, was Byzantine emperor from 780 to 797. The only child of Emperor Leo IV, Constantine was named co-emperor with him at the age of five in 776 and succeeded him as sole Emperor in 780, aged nine. His mother Irene exercised control over him as regent until 790, assisted by her chief minister Staurakios. The regency ended when Constantine reached maturity, but Irene sought to remain an active participant in the government. After a brief interval of sole rule Constantine named his mother empress in 792, making her his official colleague.

events.name: Deposition of Empress Irene
