events.summary: The citizens of Constantinople, outraged that Irene appeared to be seriously considering Charlemagne's marriage proposal — which would have made a 'blasphemous, boorish Frank' their emperor — took her prisoner in her own palace and declared her deposed at the Hippodrome. She was exiled to Lesbos, where she died less than a year later.

11.summary: The citizens of Constantinople, outraged that Irene appeared to be seriously considering Charlemagne's marriage proposal — which would have made a 'blasphemous, boorish Frank' their emperor — took her prisoner in her own palace and declared her deposed at the Hippodrome. She was exiled to Lesbos, where she died less than a year later.

events.wikipedia_extract: Irene of Athens, surname Sarantapechaena, was Byzantine empress consort to Emperor Leo IV from 775 to 780, regent during the childhood of their son Constantine VI from 780 until 790, co-ruler from 792 until 797, and finally empress regnant and sole ruler of the Eastern Roman Empire from 797 to 802. A member of the politically prominent Sarantapechos family, she was selected as Leo IV's bride for unknown reasons in 768. Even though her husband was an iconoclast, she harbored iconophile sympathies. During her regency, in 787, she called the Second Council of Nicaea which condemned iconoclasm as heretical and brought an end to the first iconoclast period (730–787). Irene's concluding five years of sole rule were unprecedented in Roman and Byzantine history. Her public figure in this period was polarizing, due to the setbacks faced by the Empire and her iconophilic stances, often attributed to her gender and the influence of her retinue.

events.name: End of Iconoclasm
events.summary: The iconoclastic controversy, which had flared up again after Irene's fall, was finally ended by the Empress Theodora, who restored the veneration of icons and was sainted for her efforts.

12.summary: The iconoclastic controversy, which had flared up again after Irene's fall, was finally ended by the Empress Theodora, who restored the veneration of icons and was sainted for her efforts.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Feast of Orthodoxy is celebrated on the first Sunday of Great Lent in the Eastern Orthodox Church and other churches using the Byzantine Rite to commemorate, originally, only the final defeat of iconoclasm on the first Sunday of Lent in 843, and later also opposition to all heterodoxy.

events.name: Restoration of Icons under Theodora
events.summary: Iconoclasm smoldered on for another forty years after Irene before finally expiring during the reign of Michael III, when his mother Theodora — like Irene a regent — proclaimed the restoration of icons. Brownworth notes this as a case of history being written by the winners, since Constantine V the fierce iconoclast is remembered as 'Copronymus' while Irene the iconodule is venerated as a saint.

11.summary: Iconoclasm smoldered on for another forty years after Irene before finally expiring during the reign of Michael III, when his mother Theodora — like Irene a regent — proclaimed the restoration of icons. Brownworth notes this as a case of history being written by the winners, since Constantine V the fierce iconoclast is remembered as 'Copronymus' while Irene the iconodule is venerated as a saint.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Feast of Orthodoxy is celebrated on the first Sunday of Great Lent in the Eastern Orthodox Church and other churches using the Byzantine Rite to commemorate, originally, only the final defeat of iconoclasm on the first Sunday of Lent in 843, and later also opposition to all heterodoxy.

events.name: Rus Siege of Constantinople
events.summary: A group of Scandinavian and Slavic raiders who called themselves the Rus besieged Constantinople — not exactly threatening to the city, but giving everyone a good scare. Photius saw in this event the opportunity to send missionaries to the Rus.

12.summary: A group of Scandinavian and Slavic raiders who called themselves the Rus besieged Constantinople — not exactly threatening to the city, but giving everyone a good scare. Photius saw in this event the opportunity to send missionaries to the Rus.

events.wikipedia_extract: The siege of Constantinople in 860 was the only major military expedition of the Rus' recorded in Byzantine and western European sources. The casus belli was the construction of the fortress Sarkel by Byzantine engineers, restricting the Rus' trade route along the Don River in favour of the Khazars. Accounts vary, with discrepancies between contemporary and later sources, and the outcome is unknown in detail.

events.name: Creation of the Cyrillic Alphabet
events.summary: Realizing the Slavic peoples lacked a written alphabet, Constantine (Cyril) devised one based on Greek characters, which was named Cyrillic after him. It became the basis for writing systems across most of Eastern Europe.

12.summary: Realizing the Slavic peoples lacked a written alphabet, Constantine (Cyril) devised one based on Greek characters, which was named Cyrillic after him. It became the basis for writing systems across most of Eastern Europe.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Cyrillic script is a writing system used for various languages across Eurasia. It is the designated national script in various Slavic, Turkic, Mongolic, Uralic, Caucasian and Iranic-speaking countries in Southeastern Europe, Eastern Europe, the Caucasus, Central Asia, North Asia, and East Asia, and used by many other minority languages.

events.name: Mission of Cyril and Methodius to the Slavs
events.summary: Photius commissioned two monks, Constantine (Cyril) and Methodius, to bring Christianity to the Bulgarians, Moravians, and Rus. Though unsuccessful with the Moravians, they brought Bulgaria firmly into the Byzantine Orthodox sphere, and Constantine's creation of the Cyrillic alphabet became the basis for writing systems across most of Eastern Europe.

12.summary: Photius commissioned two monks, Constantine (Cyril) and Methodius, to bring Christianity to the Bulgarians, Moravians, and Rus. Though unsuccessful with the Moravians, they brought Bulgaria firmly into the Byzantine Orthodox sphere, and Constantine's creation of the Cyrillic alphabet became the basis for writing systems across most of Eastern Europe.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Christianization of Bulgaria was the process by which 9th-century medieval Bulgaria converted to Christianity. It reflected the need of unity within the religiously divided Bulgarian state as well as the need for equal acceptance on the international stage in Christian Europe. This process was characterized by the shifting political alliances of Boris I of Bulgaria with the kingdom of the East Franks and with the Byzantine Empire, as well as his diplomatic correspondence with the Pope.

events.name: Photian Schism
events.summary: Photius engineered a schism between the Eastern and Western churches by being installed as patriarch without papal consultation, then telling Pope Nicholas I to mind his own business and converting the Slavs to the Eastern rite, further challenging papal authority. Basil subsequently resolved it by deposing Photius and recognizing papal supremacy — though he ensured the submission was largely nominal.

12.summary: Photius engineered a schism between the Eastern and Western churches by being installed as patriarch without papal consultation, then telling Pope Nicholas I to mind his own business and converting the Slavs to the Eastern rite, further challenging papal authority. Basil subsequently resolved it by deposing Photius and recognizing papal supremacy — though he ensured the submission was largely nominal.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Photian Schism was a four-year (863–867) schism between the episcopal sees of Rome and Constantinople. The issue centred on the right of the Byzantine Emperor to depose and appoint a patriarch without approval from the papacy.

events.name: Conversion of Bulgaria to Orthodox Christianity
events.summary: Bulgaria was pulled firmly into the Byzantine Orthodox sphere of influence through the mission of Cyril and Methodius and later confirmed by Basil's diplomatic maneuver routing the Bulgarian ecclesiastical question to friendly eastern bishoprics. Brownworth notes this allegiance endures to this day.

12.summary: Bulgaria was pulled firmly into the Byzantine Orthodox sphere of influence through the mission of Cyril and Methodius and later confirmed by Basil's diplomatic maneuver routing the Bulgarian ecclesiastical question to friendly eastern bishoprics. Brownworth notes this allegiance endures to this day.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Christianization of Bulgaria was the process by which 9th-century medieval Bulgaria converted to Christianity. It reflected the need of unity within the religiously divided Bulgarian state as well as the need for equal acceptance on the international stage in Christian Europe. This process was characterized by the shifting political alliances of Boris I of Bulgaria with the kingdom of the East Franks and with the Byzantine Empire, as well as his diplomatic correspondence with the Pope.

events.name: Basil Crowned Co-Emperor
events.summary: Following the murder of Bardas, Michael returned to Constantinople with the army and crowned Basil co-emperor — a remarkable elevation for the Armenian peasant-turned-groom.

12.summary: Following the murder of Bardas, Michael returned to Constantinople with the army and crowned Basil co-emperor — a remarkable elevation for the Armenian peasant-turned-groom.

events.wikipedia_extract: Basil I, nicknamed "the Macedonian", was Byzantine emperor from 867 to 886. Born to a peasant family in the theme of Macedonia, he rose to prominence in the imperial court after gaining the favour of Emperor Michael III, whose mistress he married on his emperor's orders. In 866, Michael proclaimed him co-emperor. Fearing a loss of influence, Basil orchestrated Michael's assassination the next year and installed himself as sole ruler of the empire. He was the first ruler of the Macedonian dynasty.

events.name: Expedition Against Saracen Crete
events.summary: Bardas was planning this expedition to retake Crete from the Saracens when he was murdered by Basil. The expedition was called off, and Basil's later attempt also came to nothing.

12.summary: Bardas was planning this expedition to retake Crete from the Saracens when he was murdered by Basil. The expedition was called off, and Basil's later attempt also came to nothing.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Emirate of Crete was an Arab Islamic state that existed on the Mediterranean island of Crete from the late 820s to the reconquest of the island by the Byzantine Empire in 961. Although the emirate recognized the suzerainty of the Abbasid Caliphate and maintained close ties with Tulunid Egypt, it was de facto independent.

events.name: Murder of Bardas
events.summary: During a military campaign crossing Asia Minor, Basil drew his sword and killed Bardas with one blow as Bardas was turning to address the emperor. Michael III, who likely knew of the plot, made no protest and immediately wrote to Photius declaring Bardas guilty of treason.

12.summary: During a military campaign crossing Asia Minor, Basil drew his sword and killed Bardas with one blow as Bardas was turning to address the emperor. Michael III, who likely knew of the plot, made no protest and immediately wrote to Photius declaring Bardas guilty of treason.

events.wikipedia_extract: Bardas was a Byzantine noble and high-ranking minister. As the brother of Empress Theodora, he rose to high office under Theophilos. Although sidelined after Theophilos's death by Theodora and Theoktistos, in 855 he engineered Theoktistos's murder and became the de facto regent for his nephew, Michael III. Rising to the rank of Caesar, he was the effective ruler of the Byzantine Empire for ten years, a period which saw military success, renewed diplomatic and missionary activity, and an intellectual revival that heralded the Macedonian Renaissance. He was assassinated in 866 at the instigation of Michael III's new favourite, Basil the Macedonian, who a year later would usurp the throne for himself and install his own dynasty on the Byzantine throne.

events.name: Byzantine Reconquest of Dalmatia
events.summary: Basil achieved spectacular success by invading the Dalmatian coast, expelling the Saracens and returning it to imperial control.

12.summary: Basil achieved spectacular success by invading the Dalmatian coast, expelling the Saracens and returning it to imperial control.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Byzantine Empire, also known as the Eastern Roman Empire, was the continuation of the Roman Empire centred on Constantinople during late antiquity and the Middle Ages. Having survived the events that caused the fall of the Western Roman Empire in the 5th century AD, it endured until the fall of Constantinople to the Ottoman Empire in 1453. The term 'Byzantine Empire' was coined only after its demise; its citizens used the term 'Roman Empire' and called themselves 'Romans'.

events.name: First Deposition of Photius
events.summary: Basil's first act as sole ruler was to depose Photius, sacrificing his patriarch to gain papal support for his western ambitions. He ensured that he himself, not papal delegates, ran the council that passed judgment, keeping the submission largely nominal.

12.summary: Basil's first act as sole ruler was to depose Photius, sacrificing his patriarch to gain papal support for his western ambitions. He ensured that he himself, not papal delegates, ran the council that passed judgment, keeping the submission largely nominal.

events.wikipedia_extract: Photios I of Constantinople was the Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople from 858 to 867 and from 877 to 886. He is recognized in the Eastern Orthodox Church as 'Saint Photius the Great'.

events.name: Murder of Michael III
events.summary: Fearing for his safety when Michael began favoring a new courtier, Basil slipped out of a banquet, bent back the bolts on the imperial bedroom door so it could not be locked, then entered with followers after Michael slept, hacked off the emperor's hands, and had him finished off. He then crossed the Golden Horn to secure the palace, and the murder was greeted with remarkably little public outrage.

12.summary: Fearing for his safety when Michael began favoring a new courtier, Basil slipped out of a banquet, bent back the bolts on the imperial bedroom door so it could not be locked, then entered with followers after Michael slept, hacked off the emperor's hands, and had him finished off. He then crossed the Golden Horn to secure the palace, and the murder was greeted with remarkably little public outrage.

events.wikipedia_extract: Michael III, also known as Michael the Drunkard, was Byzantine emperor from 842 to 867. Michael III was the third and traditionally last member of the Amorian dynasty. He was given the disparaging epithet the Drunkard by the hostile historians of the succeeding Macedonian dynasty, but modern historical research has rehabilitated his reputation to some extent, demonstrating the vital role his reign played in the resurgence of Byzantine power in the 9th century. He was also the youngest person to bear the imperial title, as well as the youngest to succeed as senior emperor in the Roman Empire. In 867, Michael was assassinated by his successor, Basil I.

events.name: Rise of the Macedonian Dynasty
events.summary: Mentioned in the episode opening as context: Basil I founded the magnificent Macedonian dynasty which oversaw the Byzantine Renaissance, culminating with Basil II. Brownworth notes that Basil I himself came to the throne by murdering his predecessor, and knew firsthand how a weak ruler provided temptation to ambitious generals.

13.summary: Mentioned in the episode opening as context: Basil I founded the magnificent Macedonian dynasty which oversaw the Byzantine Renaissance, culminating with Basil II. Brownworth notes that Basil I himself came to the throne by murdering his predecessor, and knew firsthand how a weak ruler provided temptation to ambitious generals.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Macedonian dynasty ruled the Byzantine Empire from 867 to 1056, following the Amorian dynasty. During this period, the Byzantine state reached its greatest extent since the early Muslim conquests, and the Macedonian Renaissance in letters and arts began. The dynasty was named after its founder, Basil I the Macedonian, who came from the theme of Macedonia in the region of Thrace.

events.name: Basil's Campaign Against the Paulicians
events.summary: Personally leading the bulk of his army through Asia Minor, Basil captured the city of Tefrike, the main Paulician stronghold, and killed their leader, effectively neutralizing this heretical sect that had been harassing his eastern border.

12.summary: Personally leading the bulk of his army through Asia Minor, Basil captured the city of Tefrike, the main Paulician stronghold, and killed their leader, effectively neutralizing this heretical sect that had been harassing his eastern border.

events.wikipedia_extract: Paulicianism was a Christian sect which originated in Armenia in the 7th century. Followers of the sect were called Paulicians and referred to themselves as Good Christians. Little is known about the Paulician faith and various influences have been suggested, including Gnosticism, Marcionism, Manichaeism and Adoptionism, with other scholars arguing that doctrinally the Paulicians were a largely conventional Christian reform movement unrelated to any of these currents.

events.name: Saracen Campaigns of Basil I
events.summary: The next ten years after the Paulician campaign saw a continual if slow advance against the Saracens, with Basil's brother capturing the important fortresses of Samosata and Zepetra.

12.summary: The next ten years after the Paulician campaign saw a continual if slow advance against the Saracens, with Basil's brother capturing the important fortresses of Samosata and Zepetra.

events.wikipedia_extract: Basil I, nicknamed "the Macedonian", was Byzantine emperor from 867 to 886. Born to a peasant family in the theme of Macedonia, he rose to prominence in the imperial court after gaining the favour of Emperor Michael III, whose mistress he married on his emperor's orders. In 866, Michael proclaimed him co-emperor. Fearing a loss of influence, Basil orchestrated Michael's assassination the next year and installed himself as sole ruler of the empire. He was the first ruler of the Macedonian dynasty.

events.name: Reconquest of Otranto and Bari
events.summary: Basil reconquered the Italian coastal cities of Otranto and Bari, giving his brilliant general Nikephoros Phokas an excellent bridgehead from which he would go on to conquer virtually all of southern Italy before the end of the century.

12.summary: Basil reconquered the Italian coastal cities of Otranto and Bari, giving his brilliant general Nikephoros Phokas an excellent bridgehead from which he would go on to conquer virtually all of southern Italy before the end of the century.

events.wikipedia_extract: Bari is the capital city of the Metropolitan City of Bari and of the Apulia region, on the Adriatic Sea in southern Italy. It is one of the most important economic centres of mainland southern Italy. It is a port and university city as well as the city of Saint Nicholas. The city itself has a population of 315,473 inhabitants, and an area of over 116 square kilometres (45 mi2), while the urban area has approximately 750,000 inhabitants. Its metropolitan province has 1.2 million inhabitants.

events.name: Restoration of Photius as Patriarch
events.summary: Realizing that Photius had been sacrificed in vain — the pope furious over Bulgaria negating any expected benefit — Basil recalled him to the capital, entrusted the education of his children to him, and when the patriarchal office became vacant, had him elected again. Photius's influence was immediately felt in increased missionary ties to the Balkan Slavic tribes.

12.summary: Realizing that Photius had been sacrificed in vain — the pope furious over Bulgaria negating any expected benefit — Basil recalled him to the capital, entrusted the education of his children to him, and when the patriarchal office became vacant, had him elected again. Photius's influence was immediately felt in increased missionary ties to the Balkan Slavic tribes.

events.wikipedia_extract: Photios I of Constantinople was the Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople from 858 to 867 and from 877 to 886. He is recognized in the Eastern Orthodox Church as 'Saint Photius the Great'.

events.name: Death of Constantine, Son of Basil I
events.summary: The unexpected death of Basil's beloved eldest son Constantine — the only person Basil ever truly loved — threw the emperor into a lasting depression that eventually verged on insanity, and opened the succession question that Photius would exploit to try to block Leo.

12.summary: The unexpected death of Basil's beloved eldest son Constantine — the only person Basil ever truly loved — threw the emperor into a lasting depression that eventually verged on insanity, and opened the succession question that Photius would exploit to try to block Leo.

events.wikipedia_extract: Constantine was a junior Byzantine emperor, alongside Basil I as the senior emperor, from January 868 to 3 September 879. His parentage is a matter of debate, but historians generally assume him to be the son of Emperor Basil I and his first wife Maria or second wife Eudokia Ingerina; other theories include him being the son of Emperor Michael III and Eudokia. Constantine was made co-emperor by Basil in c. January 868. He was engaged to Ermengard of Italy, the daughter of Holy Roman Emperor Louis II, in 870/871, but it is not known if he married her; some sources suggest he did, and others argue that there is no concrete evidence.

events.name: Imprisonment of Leo VI by Basil I
events.summary: Urged on by Photius, who feared Leo's succession would cost him power, and in a state of near-insanity following Constantine's death, Basil imprisoned Leo and threatened to put out his eyes. Public pressure soon forced his release, but the episode cannot have improved relations between father and son.

12.summary: Urged on by Photius, who feared Leo's succession would cost him power, and in a state of near-insanity following Constantine's death, Basil imprisoned Leo and threatened to put out his eyes. Public pressure soon forced his release, but the episode cannot have improved relations between father and son.

events.wikipedia_extract: Leo VI, also known as Leo the Wise, was Byzantine Emperor from 886 to 912. The second ruler of the Macedonian dynasty, he was very well read, leading to his epithet. During his reign, the renaissance of letters, begun by his predecessor Basil I, continued; but the empire also saw several military defeats in the Balkans against Bulgaria and against the Arabs in Sicily and the Aegean. His reign also witnessed the formal discontinuation of several ancient Roman institutions, such as the separate office of Roman consul.

events.name: Death of Basil I
events.summary: Basil died in 886 in mysterious circumstances. The official story was that a white deer caught its antlers in his belt and dragged him sixteen miles during a hunt, rupturing his stomach; he died nine days later. Brownworth finds this implausible given Basil's legendary strength and the advanced age at which he was supposedly hunting alone, suggesting Leo was likely involved and the hunt was a cover story.

12.summary: Basil died in 886 in mysterious circumstances. The official story was that a white deer caught its antlers in his belt and dragged him sixteen miles during a hunt, rupturing his stomach; he died nine days later. Brownworth finds this implausible given Basil's legendary strength and the advanced age at which he was supposedly hunting alone, suggesting Leo was likely involved and the hunt was a cover story.

events.wikipedia_extract: Basil I, nicknamed "the Macedonian", was Byzantine emperor from 867 to 886. Born to a peasant family in the theme of Macedonia, he rose to prominence in the imperial court after gaining the favour of Emperor Michael III, whose mistress he married on his emperor's orders. In 866, Michael proclaimed him co-emperor. Fearing a loss of influence, Basil orchestrated Michael's assassination the next year and installed himself as sole ruler of the empire. He was the first ruler of the Macedonian dynasty.

events.name: Byzantine Reconquest of Crete
events.summary: In one brilliant campaign, Nikephoros Phokas stormed the island of Crete and restored it to imperial control after 136 years of Muslim rule — described by Brownworth as the beginning of Phokas' extraordinary string of military victories and the turning of the tide against Islam.

13.summary: In one brilliant campaign, Nikephoros Phokas stormed the island of Crete and restored it to imperial control after 136 years of Muslim rule — described by Brownworth as the beginning of Phokas' extraordinary string of military victories and the turning of the tide against Islam.

events.wikipedia_extract: The siege of Chandax in 960–961 was the centerpiece of the Byzantine Empire's campaign to recover the island of Crete which since the 820s had been ruled by Muslim Arabs. The campaign followed a series of failed attempts to reclaim the island from the Muslims stretching as far back as 827, only a few years after the initial conquest of the island by the Arabs, and was led by the general and future emperor Nikephoros Phokas. It lasted from autumn 960 until spring 961, when the main Muslim fortress and capital of the island, Chandax was captured. The reconquest of Crete was a major achievement for the Byzantines, as it restored Byzantine control over the Aegean littoral and diminished the threat of Saracen pirates, for which Crete had provided a base of operations.

events.name: Murder of Nikephoros II Phokas
events.summary: On a cold, snowy December night, John Tzimiskes and a group of men crept into the emperor's bedroom and brutally killed him, a coup planned with Theophano and the chamberlain Basil. Brownworth emphasizes Theophano's miscalculation: she had expected to control the new emperor as she had the old, but John's first act after being crowned was to send her into exile.

13.summary: On a cold, snowy December night, John Tzimiskes and a group of men crept into the emperor's bedroom and brutally killed him, a coup planned with Theophano and the chamberlain Basil. Brownworth emphasizes Theophano's miscalculation: she had expected to control the new emperor as she had the old, but John's first act after being crowned was to send her into exile.

events.wikipedia_extract: Nikephoros II Phokas, Latinized Nicephorus II Phocas, was Byzantine emperor from 963 to 969. His career, not uniformly successful in matters of statecraft or of war, nonetheless greatly contributed to the resurgence of the Byzantine Empire during the 10th century. In the east, Nikephoros completed the conquest of Cilicia and retook the islands of Crete and Cyprus, opening the path for subsequent Byzantine incursions reaching as far as Upper Mesopotamia and the Levant; these campaigns earned him the sobriquet "pale death of the Saracens."

events.name: First Rebellion of Bardas Skleros
events.summary: After Tzimiskes' death, Bardas Skleros had his troops hail him as Basileus, captured the southern fleet, conquered all of Asia Minor, set up headquarters at Nicaea, and besieged Constantinople. The imperial fleet repulsed him with Greek fire, but he remained dangerous until the chamberlain Basil released the exiled Bardas Phokas, whose ensuing three-year civil war concluded with Skleros defeated in single combat and fleeing to the Saracens.

13.summary: After Tzimiskes' death, Bardas Skleros had his troops hail him as Basileus, captured the southern fleet, conquered all of Asia Minor, set up headquarters at Nicaea, and besieged Constantinople. The imperial fleet repulsed him with Greek fire, but he remained dangerous until the chamberlain Basil released the exiled Bardas Phokas, whose ensuing three-year civil war concluded with Skleros defeated in single combat and fleeing to the Saracens.

events.wikipedia_extract: Bardas Skleros or Sclerus was a Byzantine general who led a wide-scale Asian rebellion against Emperor Basil II during the years 976 to 979.

events.name: Imperial Fleet Repulses Skleros with Greek Fire
events.summary: The imperial fleet, loyal to the emperor, sailed out against Bardas Skleros' captured fleet and using the invaluable Greek fire annihilated it — one of the key turning points in the first rebellion, though Skleros and his land army remained intact.

13.summary: The imperial fleet, loyal to the emperor, sailed out against Bardas Skleros' captured fleet and using the invaluable Greek fire annihilated it — one of the key turning points in the first rebellion, though Skleros and his land army remained intact.

events.wikipedia_extract: Greek fire was an incendiary weapon used by the Byzantine Empire from the seventh to the fourteenth centuries. The recipe for Greek fire was a closely-guarded state secret. Historians have variously speculated that it was based on saltpeter, sulfur, or quicklime, but most modern scholars agree that it was based on petroleum mixed with resins, comparable in composition to modern napalm. Byzantine sailors would toss grenades loaded with Greek fire onto enemy ships or spray it from tubes. Its ability to burn on water made it an effective and destructive naval incendiary weapon, and rival powers tried unsuccessfully to copy the material.

events.name: Battle of Trajan's Gate
events.summary: Basil II's first Bulgarian campaign ended in humiliation when Samuel ambushed the Byzantine army in the narrow mountain pass of Trajan's Gate, nearly wiping it out after three weeks of harassment in unbearable summer heat. Brownworth says the defeat taught Basil never to walk into an ambush again, and that he vowed revenge — a vow that would take thirty years to fulfill.

13.summary: Basil II's first Bulgarian campaign ended in humiliation when Samuel ambushed the Byzantine army in the narrow mountain pass of Trajan's Gate, nearly wiping it out after three weeks of harassment in unbearable summer heat. Brownworth says the defeat taught Basil never to walk into an ambush again, and that he vowed revenge — a vow that would take thirty years to fulfill.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of the Gates of Trajan  was a battle between Byzantine and Bulgarian forces in the year 986.

events.name: Byzantine–Bulgarian War (Basil II's Thirty-Year Campaign)
events.summary: The defining military undertaking of Basil II's reign, spanning thirty years, from the humiliation at Trajan's Gate through to the final submission of the Bulgarian capital in 1018. Brownworth presents it as the product of Basil's relentless, methodical, lava-like strategy — endless drills, no gambles, winter and summer campaigns regardless of weather — gradually grinding down Samuel despite raids, Fatimid interruptions, and the mountainous terrain.

13.summary: The defining military undertaking of Basil II's reign, spanning thirty years, from the humiliation at Trajan's Gate through to the final submission of the Bulgarian capital in 1018. Brownworth presents it as the product of Basil's relentless, methodical, lava-like strategy — endless drills, no gambles, winter and summer campaigns regardless of weather — gradually grinding down Samuel despite raids, Fatimid interruptions, and the mountainous terrain.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Byzantine–Bulgarian wars were a series of conflicts fought between the Byzantine Empire and the Bulgarian Empire which began after the Bulgars conquered parts of the Balkan peninsula after 680 AD. The Byzantine and First Bulgarian Empire continued to clash over the next century with varying success, until the Bulgarians, led by Krum, inflicted a series of crushing defeats on the Byzantines. After Krum died in 814, his son Omurtag negotiated a thirty-year peace treaty. Simeon I had multiple successful campaigns against the Byzantines during his rule from 893 to 927. His son Peter I negotiated another long-lasting peace treaty. His rule was followed by a period of decline of the Bulgarian state.

events.name: Second Rebellion: Phokas and Skleros Unite Against Basil II
events.summary: Bardas Skleros returned from Baghdad with Caliph funding and found the Anatolian nobility ready to revolt against Basil. He and Bardas Phokas combined forces against the emperor, intending to split the empire between them — but Phokas treacherously arrested Skleros and advanced alone. Basil sought foreign help, found it in Vladimir of Kiev's Varangian soldiers, and crushed the rebellion.

13.summary: Bardas Skleros returned from Baghdad with Caliph funding and found the Anatolian nobility ready to revolt against Basil. He and Bardas Phokas combined forces against the emperor, intending to split the empire between them — but Phokas treacherously arrested Skleros and advanced alone. Basil sought foreign help, found it in Vladimir of Kiev's Varangian soldiers, and crushed the rebellion.

events.wikipedia_extract: Bardas Phokas was a Byzantine general who took a conspicuous part in three revolts for and against the ruling Macedonian dynasty.

events.name: Founding of the Varangian Guard
events.summary: Impressed by the Scandinavian Varangians in the defeat of Phokas, Basil II organized them into his personal bodyguard — the famous Varangian Guard. Brownworth notes they faithfully served the empire for 300 years, becoming legendary for their fierce battle axes and their loyalty to the throne (if not always its occupant), and that the most famous member was Harald Hardrada.

13.summary: Impressed by the Scandinavian Varangians in the defeat of Phokas, Basil II organized them into his personal bodyguard — the famous Varangian Guard. Brownworth notes they faithfully served the empire for 300 years, becoming legendary for their fierce battle axes and their loyalty to the throne (if not always its occupant), and that the most famous member was Harald Hardrada.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Varangian Guard was an elite unit of the Byzantine army from the 10th to the 14th century who served as personal bodyguards to the Byzantine emperors. The Varangian Guard was known for being primarily composed of recruits from Northern Europe, including mainly Norsemen from Scandinavia but also Anglo-Saxons from England. The recruitment of distant fighters from outside Byzantium to serve as the emperor's personal guard was pursued as a deliberate policy, as they lacked local political loyalties and could be counted upon to suppress revolts by disloyal Byzantine factions.

13.text: and he organized them into what would become the famous Varangian Guard.

events.name: Fatimid Siege of Antioch
events.summary: In the fourth year of the Bulgarian war, the Fatimid Caliphate invaded Syria and besieged Antioch, bringing it close to surrender. Basil II, renowned for his glacial pace, moved with blinding speed — mounting his entire 40,000-man army on mules, one to ride and one for equipment — and covered 600 miles in 16 days, completely surprising the Fatimid army and cutting it to pieces.

13.summary: In the fourth year of the Bulgarian war, the Fatimid Caliphate invaded Syria and besieged Antioch, bringing it close to surrender. Basil II, renowned for his glacial pace, moved with blinding speed — mounting his entire 40,000-man army on mules, one to ride and one for equipment — and covered 600 miles in 16 days, completely surprising the Fatimid army and cutting it to pieces.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Fatimid Caliphate, also known as the Fatimid Empire, was a caliphate that existed from the tenth to the twelfth centuries CE under the rule of the Fatimids, an Isma'ili Shi'a dynasty. Spanning a large area of North Africa and West Asia, it ranged from the western Mediterranean in the west to the Red Sea in the east. The Fatimids traced their ancestry to the Islamic prophet Muhammad's daughter Fatima and her husband Ali, the first Shi'a imam. The Fatimids were acknowledged as the rightful imams by different Isma'ili communities as well as by denominations in many other Muslim lands and adjacent regions. Starting in Ifriqiya during the Abbasid Caliphate, the Fatimids overthrew the Aghlabids and extended their rule across the Mediterranean coast and ultimately made Egypt the center of the caliphate. At its height, the caliphate included—in addition to Egypt—varying areas of the Maghreb, Sicily, the Levant, and the Hejaz.

events.name: Basil II's Land Reforms and Abolition of Patrician Privileges
events.summary: Returning from the Antioch relief in a thoughtful mood after being wined and dined by the Anatolian aristocracy, Basil abolished traditional patrician land privileges, reducing many rich families to poverty and giving Asia Minor's peasants — the backbone of the army — access again to their ancestral lands. Brownworth describes it as breaking aristocratic power in one fell swoop and providing the empire with a wealth of recruits.

13.summary: Returning from the Antioch relief in a thoughtful mood after being wined and dined by the Anatolian aristocracy, Basil abolished traditional patrician land privileges, reducing many rich families to poverty and giving Asia Minor's peasants — the backbone of the army — access again to their ancestral lands. Brownworth describes it as breaking aristocratic power in one fell swoop and providing the empire with a wealth of recruits.

events.wikipedia_extract: Basil II Porphyrogenitus, given the epithet the Bulgar Slayer, was the senior Byzantine emperor from 976 to 1025. He and his brother Constantine VIII were crowned before their father Romanos II died in 963, but they were too young to rule. The throne thus went to two generals, Nikephoros Phokas and John Tzimiskes before Basil became senior emperor, though his influential great-uncle Basil Lekapenos remained as the de facto ruler until 985. His reign of 49 years and 11 months was the longest of any Roman emperor.

events.name: Venetian Invasion of Dalmatia
events.summary: Faced with an expanded Bulgarian threat after Samuel raided as far south as Corinth and conquered Albania and Bosnia, Basil II invited Venice to invade the Dalmatian coast in the name of the empire, solving two problems: protecting Byzantine cities and forcing Samuel into a two-front war. The Venetians were eager for the forests to provide timber for their navy.

13.summary: Faced with an expanded Bulgarian threat after Samuel raided as far south as Corinth and conquered Albania and Bosnia, Basil II invited Venice to invade the Dalmatian coast in the name of the empire, solving two problems: protecting Byzantine cities and forcing Samuel into a two-front war. The Venetians were eager for the forests to provide timber for their navy.

events.wikipedia_extract: Dalmatia is a historical region located in modern-day Croatia and Montenegro, on the eastern shore of the Adriatic Sea. It is a narrow land belt stretching from the island of Rab in the north to the Bay of Kotor in the south. The Dalmatian Hinterland ranges in width from 50 kilometres in the north, to just a few kilometres in the south; it is mostly covered by the rugged Dinaric Alps. Seventy-nine islands run parallel to the coast, the largest being Brač, Pag, and Hvar. The largest city is Split, followed by Zadar, Šibenik, and Dubrovnik.

events.name: Zoe Sent to Marry Otto III
events.summary: Basil II sent his niece Zoe to marry the Holy Roman Emperor Otto III, who dreamed of reuniting the two halves of the Roman Empire. Brownworth speculates that had the marriage taken place, the throne of Byzantium might have passed to Zoe on Basil's death and east and west could have been reunited — but Zoe arrived to find Otto already dead.

13.summary: Basil II sent his niece Zoe to marry the Holy Roman Emperor Otto III, who dreamed of reuniting the two halves of the Roman Empire. Brownworth speculates that had the marriage taken place, the throne of Byzantium might have passed to Zoe on Basil's death and east and west could have been reunited — but Zoe arrived to find Otto already dead.

events.wikipedia_extract: Otto III was the Holy Roman emperor and King of Italy from 996 until his death in 1002. A member of the Ottonian dynasty, Otto III was the only son of Emperor Otto II and his wife Theophanu.

events.name: Battle of Kleidion (Symbolongus) and Mass Blinding
events.summary: The decisive engagement of the Bulgarian war, fought in a narrow ravine. Like Thermopylae, Basil found a path around Samuel's blocking force and attacked from both sides, routing the Bulgarians and capturing over 14,000 prisoners. In his most notorious act, Basil ordered all 14,000 blinded, leaving one eye to every hundredth man so they could lead their companions back to Samuel — who collapsed in shock and died two days later when he saw the pathetic procession.

13.summary: The decisive engagement of the Bulgarian war, fought in a narrow ravine. Like Thermopylae, Basil found a path around Samuel's blocking force and attacked from both sides, routing the Bulgarians and capturing over 14,000 prisoners. In his most notorious act, Basil ordered all 14,000 blinded, leaving one eye to every hundredth man so they could lead their companions back to Samuel — who collapsed in shock and died two days later when he saw the pathetic procession.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Kleidion took place on 29 July 1014, between the Byzantine Empire and the Bulgarian Empire. It was the culmination of the nearly half-century struggle between the Byzantine Emperor Basil II and the Bulgarian emperor Samuel in the late 10th and early 11th centuries. The result was a decisive Byzantine victory.

events.name: Formal Submission of Bulgaria to Byzantium
events.summary: Four years after Samuel's death, Bulgaria formally submitted in 1018 and Basil entered the gates of their capital — ending a lifetime of struggle that had begun when he was 28 and concluded when he was 60. Bulgaria became part of the empire for almost 200 years; Basil treated the conquered people generously, keeping taxes low, local leaders in place, and the aristocracy in high imperial offices.

13.summary: Four years after Samuel's death, Bulgaria formally submitted in 1018 and Basil entered the gates of their capital — ending a lifetime of struggle that had begun when he was 28 and concluded when he was 60. Bulgaria became part of the empire for almost 200 years; Basil treated the conquered people generously, keeping taxes low, local leaders in place, and the aristocracy in high imperial offices.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Byzantine–Bulgarian wars were a series of conflicts fought between the Byzantine Empire and the Bulgarian Empire which began after the Bulgars conquered parts of the Balkan peninsula after 680 AD. The Byzantine and First Bulgarian Empire continued to clash over the next century with varying success, until the Bulgarians, led by Krum, inflicted a series of crushing defeats on the Byzantines. After Krum died in 814, his son Omurtag negotiated a thirty-year peace treaty. Simeon I had multiple successful campaigns against the Byzantines during his rule from 893 to 927. His son Peter I negotiated another long-lasting peace treaty. His rule was followed by a period of decline of the Bulgarian state.

events.name: Death of Basil II
events.summary: Basil II fell ill while planning an invasion of Sicily and died on December 15, 1025, at three in the afternoon, in the great palace of Constantinople — nearly 65 years old in an age when life expectancy was 33. Brownworth frames his death as the end of an age, quoting Norwich: 'He died on the 15th of December. By the 16th, the decline had already begun.'

13.summary: Basil II fell ill while planning an invasion of Sicily and died on December 15, 1025, at three in the afternoon, in the great palace of Constantinople — nearly 65 years old in an age when life expectancy was 33. Brownworth frames his death as the end of an age, quoting Norwich: 'He died on the 15th of December. By the 16th, the decline had already begun.'

events.wikipedia_extract: Basil II Porphyrogenitus, given the epithet the Bulgar Slayer, was the senior Byzantine emperor from 976 to 1025. He and his brother Constantine VIII were crowned before their father Romanos II died in 963, but they were too young to rule. The throne thus went to two generals, Nikephoros Phokas and John Tzimiskes before Basil became senior emperor, though his influential great-uncle Basil Lekapenos remained as the de facto ruler until 985. His reign of 49 years and 11 months was the longest of any Roman emperor.

events.name: Great Schism
events.summary: The permanent rupture between the Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox churches when the Pope and Patriarch excommunicated each other in 1054. Brownworth frames this as the event that set the wheels of the First Crusade in motion 42 years later, since it established the Pope rather than the Byzantine Emperor as supreme spiritual authority in the West — meaning Alexios' appeal to Urban II for mercenaries gave the Pope the opportunity to call a crusade under his own authority.

14.summary: The permanent rupture between the Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox churches when the Pope and Patriarch excommunicated each other in 1054. Brownworth frames this as the event that set the wheels of the First Crusade in motion 42 years later, since it established the Pope rather than the Byzantine Emperor as supreme spiritual authority in the West — meaning Alexios' appeal to Urban II for mercenaries gave the Pope the opportunity to call a crusade under his own authority.

events.wikipedia_extract: The East–West Schism, also known as the Great Schism or the Schism of 1054, is the break of communion between the Catholic Church and the Eastern Orthodox Church since 1054. A series of ecclesiastical differences, theological disputes and geopolitical tensions between the Greek East and Latin West preceded the formal split in 1054. Prominent among these were the procession of the Holy Spirit, whether leavened or unleavened bread should be used in the Eucharist, iconoclasm, the coronation of Charlemagne as emperor of the Romans in 800, the pope's claim to universal jurisdiction, and the place of the See of Constantinople in relation to the pentarchy. Although 1054 has become conventional, various scholars have proposed different dates for the Great Schism, including 1009, 1204, 1277, and 1484.

14.text: in what came to be called the Great Schism.
With the Great Schism, however, the West had rejected that claim, and the implications

events.name: Great Schism
events.summary: Brownworth notes the official split came in 1054 over the Filioque clause in the Nicene Creed — whether the Holy Spirit proceeds from the Father and the Son together or from the Father alone — but argues the two halves of Christendom had been drifting apart long before the barbarian invasions engulfed the West.
