A series of ecclesiastical differences, theological disputes and geopolitical tensions between the Greek East and Latin West preceded the formal split in 1054. Prominent among these were the procession of the Holy Spirit, whether leavened or unleavened bread should be used in the Eucharist, iconoclasm, the coronation of Charlemagne as emperor of the Romans in 800, the pope's claim to universal jurisdiction, and the place of the See of Constantinople in relation to the pentarchy. Although 1054 has become conventional, various scholars have proposed different dates for the Great Schism, including 1009, 1204, 1277, and 1484.

events.name: Battle of Stamford Bridge
events.summary: Referenced as the battle in England where Harald Hardrada — the most famous Varangian Guard member — was killed at the hands of Harold the last Saxon king, illustrating the far-reaching footprint of Byzantine imperial institutions in European history.

13.summary: Referenced as the battle in England where Harald Hardrada — the most famous Varangian Guard member — was killed at the hands of Harold the last Saxon king, illustrating the far-reaching footprint of Byzantine imperial institutions in European history.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Stamford Bridge took place at the village of Stamford Bridge, East Riding of Yorkshire, in England, on 25 September 1066, between an English army under King Harold Godwinson and an invading Norwegian force led by King Harald Hardrada and the English king's brother Tostig Godwinson. After a bloody battle, both Hardrada and Tostig, along with most of the Norwegians, were killed. Although Harold Godwinson repelled the Norwegian invaders, his army was defeated by the Normans at Hastings less than three weeks later.

13.text: of Stamford Bridge in 1066.

events.name: Norman Conquest of England
events.summary: Referenced by Brownworth as the event in which William the Conqueror invaded England, killed the rightful king, and claimed the throne for himself — part of the broader Norman success story of the 11th century. The Norman conquest is also relevant to the Varangian Guard, which was made up largely of exiled Anglo-Saxons who had fled it and were eager for revenge against the Normans at Dyrrachium.

14.summary: Referenced by Brownworth as the event in which William the Conqueror invaded England, killed the rightful king, and claimed the throne for himself — part of the broader Norman success story of the 11th century. The Norman conquest is also relevant to the Varangian Guard, which was made up largely of exiled Anglo-Saxons who had fled it and were eager for revenge against the Normans at Dyrrachium.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Norman Conquest of England was an 11th-century invasion by an army made up of thousands of Norman, French, Flemish, and Breton troops, all led by the Duke of Normandy, later styled William the Conqueror.

events.name: Battle of Manzikert
events.summary: The catastrophic Byzantine defeat by the Seljuk Turks near the small town of Manzikert in Asia Minor, where Romanos IV Diogenes led a mercenary-heavy army and was betrayed by commanders and mercenaries alike, resulting in annihilation and capture. Brownworth frames this as the event that dispelled the myth of Byzantine invincibility and triggered the collapse that Alexios later had to arrest — but he emphasizes it need not have become the disaster it did, since the Turks were distracted by a rival Islamic power.

14.summary: The catastrophic Byzantine defeat by the Seljuk Turks near the small town of Manzikert in Asia Minor, where Romanos IV Diogenes led a mercenary-heavy army and was betrayed by commanders and mercenaries alike, resulting in annihilation and capture. Brownworth frames this as the event that dispelled the myth of Byzantine invincibility and triggered the collapse that Alexios later had to arrest — but he emphasizes it need not have become the disaster it did, since the Turks were distracted by a rival Islamic power.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Manzikert or Malazgirt was fought between the Byzantine Empire and the Seljuk Empire on 26 August 1071 near Manzikert, Iberia. The decisive defeat of the Byzantine army and the capture of the emperor Romanos IV Diogenes played an important role in undermining Byzantine authority in Anatolia and Armenia, and allowed for the gradual Turkification of Anatolia. Many Turks, travelling westward during the 11th century, saw the victory at Manzikert as an entrance to Asia Minor.

events.name: Norman Capture of Bari
events.summary: Robert Giscard's capture of Bari in 1071, the last Byzantine stronghold in Italy, ending Byzantine presence on the Italian peninsula. Brownworth pairs this with the Battle of Manzikert in the same year as a bookend disaster — the empire losing its western Italian foothold and its eastern Anatolian heartland in the same year.

14.summary: Robert Giscard's capture of Bari in 1071, the last Byzantine stronghold in Italy, ending Byzantine presence on the Italian peninsula. Brownworth pairs this with the Battle of Manzikert in the same year as a bookend disaster — the empire losing its western Italian foothold and its eastern Anatolian heartland in the same year.

events.wikipedia_extract: Bari is the capital city of the Metropolitan City of Bari and of the Apulia region, on the Adriatic Sea in southern Italy. It is one of the most important economic centres of mainland southern Italy. It is a port and university city as well as the city of Saint Nicholas. The city itself has a population of 315,473 inhabitants, and an area of over 116 square kilometres (45 mi2), while the urban area has approximately 750,000 inhabitants. Its metropolitan province has 1.2 million inhabitants.

events.name: Seljuk Overrunning of Asia Minor
events.summary: In the wake of Manzikert, the weak emperors who followed Romanos, lost in petty court struggles, did nothing as the Seljuks spread over 30,000 miles and completely overran Asia Minor. Michael VII even officially recognized the Turks' hold in 1078 in exchange for help against a renegade mercenary — described by Brownworth as a blindingly inept maneuver.

14.summary: In the wake of Manzikert, the weak emperors who followed Romanos, lost in petty court struggles, did nothing as the Seljuks spread over 30,000 miles and completely overran Asia Minor. Michael VII even officially recognized the Turks' hold in 1078 in exchange for help against a renegade mercenary — described by Brownworth as a blindingly inept maneuver.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Sultanate of Rum, or Seljuk Sultanate of Rum, was a culturally Turco-Persian, Sunni Muslim state established over conquered Byzantine territories and peoples (Rum) of Anatolia by the Seljuk Turks following their entry into Anatolia after the Battle of Manzikert in 1071. The name Rum was a synonym for the medieval Roman Empire and its peoples, as it remains in modern Turkish. The name is derived from the Aramaic and Parthian names for ancient Rome, which had reached these languages via the Greek Ῥωμαῖοι. The Seljuk Turks used the name to refer to Anatolia. The Sultanate of Rum is considered to be the beginning of Turkish history in Anatolia.

events.name: Alexios Seizes the Byzantine Throne
events.summary: After Maria adopted Alexios into the royal family, he was given command of a campaign against the Turks, which he used to gather an army and be acclaimed emperor. He bribed defenders to open the city gates rather than undertaking a siege, entered Constantinople, and marched to the imperial palace while his mercenaries looted the city for a full day. Nikephoros abdicated and retired to a monastery, and three days later the Patriarch crowned the 24-year-old in a subdued ceremony at the Hagia Sophia.

14.summary: After Maria adopted Alexios into the royal family, he was given command of a campaign against the Turks, which he used to gather an army and be acclaimed emperor. He bribed defenders to open the city gates rather than undertaking a siege, entered Constantinople, and marched to the imperial palace while his mercenaries looted the city for a full day. Nikephoros abdicated and retired to a monastery, and three days later the Patriarch crowned the 24-year-old in a subdued ceremony at the Hagia Sophia.

events.wikipedia_extract: Alexios I Komnenos, Latinized as Alexius I Comnenus, was Byzantine emperor from 1081 to 1118. After usurping the throne he was faced with a collapsing empire and constant warfare throughout his reign. Alexios was able to curb the Byzantine decline and begin the military, financial, and territorial recovery known as the Komnenian restoration. His appeals to Western Europe for help against the Seljuk Turks were the catalyst that sparked the First Crusade. Although he was not the first emperor of the Komnenian dynasty, it was during his reign that the Komnenos family came to full power and initiated a hereditary succession to the throne.

events.name: Annihilation of the Varangian Guard at Dyrrachium
events.summary: During the Battle of Dyrrachium, the Varangian Guard — the cream of Alexios' army, made up largely of exiled Anglo-Saxons who had fled the Norman conquest of England — were left exposed and isolated when the rest of the army broke ranks, and were cut down to a man. Brownworth uses this as the clearest illustration of the fatal flaws of a mercenary-based army.

14.summary: During the Battle of Dyrrachium, the Varangian Guard — the cream of Alexios' army, made up largely of exiled Anglo-Saxons who had fled the Norman conquest of England — were left exposed and isolated when the rest of the army broke ranks, and were cut down to a man. Brownworth uses this as the clearest illustration of the fatal flaws of a mercenary-based army.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Varangian Guard was an elite unit of the Byzantine army from the 10th to the 14th century who served as personal bodyguards to the Byzantine emperors. The Varangian Guard was known for being primarily composed of recruits from Northern Europe, including mainly Norsemen from Scandinavia but also Anglo-Saxons from England. The recruitment of distant fighters from outside Byzantium to serve as the emperor's personal guard was pursued as a deliberate policy, as they lacked local political loyalties and could be counted upon to suppress revolts by disloyal Byzantine factions.

events.name: Battle of Dyrrachium
events.summary: Alexios' first major military test against Robert Giscard's Normans at the port of Dyrrachium. The Varangian Guard — largely exiled Anglo-Saxons eager for revenge against the Normans — stopped the Norman charge with great effect, but undisciplined rank-and-file broke to pursue retreating Normans, were massacred when Norman cavalry turned around, and the isolated Varangians were cut down to a man. Alexios barely escaped in disguise and Dyrrachium fell.

14.summary: Alexios' first major military test against Robert Giscard's Normans at the port of Dyrrachium. The Varangian Guard — largely exiled Anglo-Saxons eager for revenge against the Normans — stopped the Norman charge with great effect, but undisciplined rank-and-file broke to pursue retreating Normans, were massacred when Norman cavalry turned around, and the isolated Varangians were cut down to a man. Alexios barely escaped in disguise and Dyrrachium fell.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Dyrrhachium took place on October 18, 1081 between the Byzantine Empire, led by the Emperor Alexios I Komnenos, and the Normans of southern Italy under Robert Guiscard, Duke of Apulia and Calabria. The battle was fought outside the city of Dyrrhachium, the major Byzantine stronghold in the western Balkans, and ended in a Norman victory.

events.name: Sack of Rome by Robert Guiscard
events.summary: While returning to Greece after suppressing the Alexios-backed revolt in Italy and driving the German Emperor out, Robert Guiscard carried out a brutal three-day sack of Rome in which he set fire to the city and gutted most of its ancient buildings. Brownworth presents this as evidence of the destructive power Alexios had to contend with.

14.summary: While returning to Greece after suppressing the Alexios-backed revolt in Italy and driving the German Emperor out, Robert Guiscard carried out a brutal three-day sack of Rome in which he set fire to the city and gutted most of its ancient buildings. Brownworth presents this as evidence of the destructive power Alexios had to contend with.

events.wikipedia_extract: The sack of Rome of May 1084 was a Norman sack, the result of the pope's call for aid from the duke of Apulia, Robert Guiscard.

events.name: Death of Robert Guiscard
events.summary: Plague struck the Norman camp when the 68-year-old Robert returned to Greece, killing Robert himself. Brownworth describes his death as delivering the Empire — both east and west — from one of its most formidable enemies, and notes that his successor Bohemond found himself outmatched by Alexios at every turn.

14.summary: Plague struck the Norman camp when the 68-year-old Robert returned to Greece, killing Robert himself. Brownworth describes his death as delivering the Empire — both east and west — from one of its most formidable enemies, and notes that his successor Bohemond found himself outmatched by Alexios at every turn.

events.wikipedia_extract: Robert Guiscard, also referred to as Robert de Hauteville, was a Norman adventurer remembered for his conquest of southern Italy and Sicily in the 11th century.

events.name: Pecheneg-Seljuk Assault on Constantinople
events.summary: The Seljuk Turks allied with the Pechenegs and appeared beneath the walls of Constantinople. Alexios responded with characteristically indirect cunning: he invited the Cumans — the Pechenegs' main rivals — to annihilate the Pechenegs, then incited the Emir's son-in-law to revolt against the Emir, neutralizing the Turkish threat. The Byzantine-backed candidate signed a peace treaty stabilizing the eastern frontier.

14.summary: The Seljuk Turks allied with the Pechenegs and appeared beneath the walls of Constantinople. Alexios responded with characteristically indirect cunning: he invited the Cumans — the Pechenegs' main rivals — to annihilate the Pechenegs, then incited the Emir's son-in-law to revolt against the Emir, neutralizing the Turkish threat. The Byzantine-backed candidate signed a peace treaty stabilizing the eastern frontier.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Levounion was the first decisive Byzantine victory of the Komnenian restoration. On 29 April 1091, an invading force of Pechenegs was crushed by the combined forces of the Byzantine Empire under Alexios I Komnenos and his Cuman allies.

events.name: Cuman Annihilation of the Pechenegs
events.summary: At Alexios' instigation, the Cumans — rivals of the Pechenegs — were invited into Byzantine territory to annihilate the Pecheneg forces threatening Constantinople. Brownworth uses this as a prime example of Alexios' diplomatic mastery: inducing another nation to do his military work for him.

14.summary: At Alexios' instigation, the Cumans — rivals of the Pechenegs — were invited into Byzantine territory to annihilate the Pecheneg forces threatening Constantinople. Brownworth uses this as a prime example of Alexios' diplomatic mastery: inducing another nation to do his military work for him.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Levounion was the first decisive Byzantine victory of the Komnenian restoration. On 29 April 1091, an invading force of Pechenegs was crushed by the combined forces of the Byzantine Empire under Alexios I Komnenos and his Cuman allies.

events.name: Council of Clermont
events.summary: The great church council called by Pope Urban II in November 1095 where he gave what Brownworth calls the single most effective speech ever heard in Europe — calling on Western Christendom to stop killing each other and take up arms for Jerusalem. Where Alexios had wanted only a few hundred mercenaries, Urban called for whole armies, with no mention of the Emperor or the Empire.

14.summary: The great church council called by Pope Urban II in November 1095 where he gave what Brownworth calls the single most effective speech ever heard in Europe — calling on Western Christendom to stop killing each other and take up arms for Jerusalem. Where Alexios had wanted only a few hundred mercenaries, Urban called for whole armies, with no mention of the Emperor or the Empire.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Council of Clermont was a mixed synod of ecclesiastics and laymen of the Catholic Church, called by Pope Urban II and held from 17 to 27 November 1095 at Clermont, Auvergne, at the time part of the Duchy of Aquitaine.

events.name: The Crusades
events.summary: Brownworth mentions the Crusades only in passing, noting that Western disregard for the Byzantine Empire predates even the Crusades and is made up of 'equal parts envy and disgust.' The Crusades thus represent a long-standing cultural rupture between Western and Eastern Christendom.

1.summary: Brownworth mentions the Crusades only in passing, noting that Western disregard for the Byzantine Empire predates even the Crusades and is made up of 'equal parts envy and disgust.' The Crusades thus represent a long-standing cultural rupture between Western and Eastern Christendom.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Crusades were a series of military campaigns launched by the papacy between 1095 and 1291 against Muslim rulers for the recovery and defence of the Holy Land, encouraged by promises of spiritual reward. The First Crusade was proclaimed by Pope Urban II at the Council of Clermont in November 1095—a call to arms for Christians to reconquer Jerusalem from the Muslims. By this time, the papacy's position as head of the Catholic Church had strengthened, and earlier conflicts with secular rulers and wars on the frontiers of Western Christendom had prepared it for the direction of armed force in religious causes. The successes of the First Crusade led to the establishment of four Crusader states in the Levant, where their defence required further expeditions from Catholic Europe. The organisation of such large-scale campaigns demanded complex religious, social, and economic institutions, including crusade indulgences, military orders, and the taxation of clerical income. Over time, the crusading movement expanded to include campaigns against pagans, Christian dissidents, and other enemies of the papacy, promoted with similar spiritual rewards and continuing into the 18th century.

1.text: but from a Western disregard dating from before the Crusades, made up of equal parts envy and

events.name: First Crusade
events.summary: Launched in 1096, the First Crusade brought Western armies through Byzantine territory on their way to the Holy Land, creating a tense encounter between cultures that were barely on speaking terms. Crusaders entering Constantinople reacted with a mixture of wonder, jealousy, and disgust, while Emperor Alexios I weighed whether the Turks or his nominal allies posed the greater danger to his empire. Although the Crusaders ultimately broke their oaths to return conquered lands to Byzantium, their campaign did succeed in weakening the empire's eastern enemies.

15.summary: The crusading armies passed smoothly through Alexius I's dominions. Though they did not keep their oaths about returning conquered territories to Byzantium, they at least weakened the empire's eastern enemies.

17.summary: Brownworth describes the moment awestruck Crusaders entered Constantinople during the First Crusade as a cultural collision where Easterners and Westerners were barely on speaking terms. The Crusaders felt wonder mixed with jealousy and disgust, while Emperor Alexios wondered whether the Turks or the Crusaders posed the greater threat.

events.wikipedia_extract: The First Crusade (1096–1099) was the first of a series of religious wars, or Crusades, which were initiated, supported and at times directed by the Latin Church in the Middle Ages. Their aim was to return the Holy Land—which had been conquered by the Rashidun Caliphate in the 7th century—to Christian rule. By the 11th century, although Jerusalem had then been ruled by Muslims for hundreds of years, the practices of the Seljuk rulers in the region began to threaten local Christian populations, pilgrimages from the West and the Byzantine Empire itself. The earliest impetus for the First Crusade came in 1095 when Byzantine emperor Alexios I Komnenos sent ambassadors to the Council of Piacenza to request military support in the empire's conflict with the Seljuk-led Turks. This was followed later in the year by the Council of Clermont, at which Pope Urban II gave a speech supporting the Byzantine request and urging faithful Christians to undertake an armed pilgrimage to Jerusalem.

15.text: The armies of the First Crusade had smoothly passed through his dominions, and though they hadn't kept their oaths about returning conquered

17.text: By the time of the first crusade when the awestruck crusaders entered the fabled city

events.name: First Crusade's Passage Through Constantinople
events.summary: Nearly 100,000 soldiers — the flower of European chivalry — passed through Constantinople over several months. Alexios managed them with a masterful blend of friendship and saber-rattling: troops camped outside the walls, only unarmed small groups admitted, leaders kept as virtual prisoners until they swore an oath to return any former Byzantine territory they captured. He showered them with gifts, hosted banquets, even adopted some into the royal family. All but one leader swore; Alexios watched them depart with no illusions they would keep their oaths.

14.summary: Nearly 100,000 soldiers — the flower of European chivalry — passed through Constantinople over several months. Alexios managed them with a masterful blend of friendship and saber-rattling: troops camped outside the walls, only unarmed small groups admitted, leaders kept as virtual prisoners until they swore an oath to return any former Byzantine territory they captured. He showered them with gifts, hosted banquets, even adopted some into the royal family. All but one leader swore; Alexios watched them depart with no illusions they would keep their oaths.

events.wikipedia_extract: The First Crusade (1096–1099) was the first of a series of religious wars, or Crusades, which were initiated, supported and at times directed by the Latin Church in the Middle Ages. Their aim was to return the Holy Land—which had been conquered by the Rashidun Caliphate in the 7th century—to Christian rule. By the 11th century, although Jerusalem had then been ruled by Muslims for hundreds of years, the practices of the Seljuk rulers in the region began to threaten local Christian populations, pilgrimages from the West and the Byzantine Empire itself. The earliest impetus for the First Crusade came in 1095 when Byzantine emperor Alexios I Komnenos sent ambassadors to the Council of Piacenza to request military support in the empire's conflict with the Seljuk-led Turks. This was followed later in the year by the Council of Clermont, at which Pope Urban II gave a speech supporting the Byzantine request and urging faithful Christians to undertake an armed pilgrimage to Jerusalem.

events.name: People's Crusade
events.summary: The first crusading force to arrive — 40,000 French and German peasants led by Peter the Hermit — confirmed Alexios' worst suspicions. Despite being given guides, provisions, and interpreters, they burned Belgrade, rioted, lost 10,000 men to Byzantine cavalry, then looted the suburbs of Constantinople. Alexios ferried them across the Bosphorus; Peter ignored his advice and his rabble was wiped out by the Turks.

14.summary: The first crusading force to arrive — 40,000 French and German peasants led by Peter the Hermit — confirmed Alexios' worst suspicions. Despite being given guides, provisions, and interpreters, they burned Belgrade, rioted, lost 10,000 men to Byzantine cavalry, then looted the suburbs of Constantinople. Alexios ferried them across the Bosphorus; Peter ignored his advice and his rabble was wiped out by the Turks.

events.wikipedia_extract: The People's Crusade was the beginning phase of the First Crusade whose objective was to retake the Holy Land, and Jerusalem in particular, from Islamic rule. In 1095, after the head of the Roman Catholic Church Pope Urban II started to urge faithful Christians to undertake an armed pilgrimage to Jerusalem, the People's Crusade was conducted for roughly six months from April to October 1096. It is also known as the Peasants' Crusade, Paupers' Crusade or the Popular Crusade as it was executed by a mainly untrained peasant army prior to the main church-organized crusade. It was led primarily by Peter the Hermit with forces of Walter Sans Avoir. The peasant army of this crusade was largely destroyed by the forces of the Seljuk Turks under Kilij Arslan at the Battle of Civetot in northwestern Anatolia, though some survivors joined the armies of the Prince's Crusade in Constantinople.

events.name: Crusader Siege and Byzantine Capture of Nicaea
events.summary: The Sultan of Rum attacked the crusading army besieging his capital of Nicaea, but found to his horror these soldiers were nothing like Peter the Hermit's rabble — his army broke and fled. The citizens of Nicaea, fearing a sack and with no hope of rescue, quietly surrendered to the Byzantines overnight. When crusaders awoke to find the Byzantine flag flying and looting opportunities gone, they began to grumble openly about Byzantine duplicity.

14.summary: The Sultan of Rum attacked the crusading army besieging his capital of Nicaea, but found to his horror these soldiers were nothing like Peter the Hermit's rabble — his army broke and fled. The citizens of Nicaea, fearing a sack and with no hope of rescue, quietly surrendered to the Byzantines overnight. When crusaders awoke to find the Byzantine flag flying and looting opportunities gone, they began to grumble openly about Byzantine duplicity.

events.wikipedia_extract: The siege of Nicaea was the first major battle of the First Crusade, taking place from 14 May to 19 June 1097. The city was under the control of the Seljuk Turks who opted to surrender to the Byzantines in fear of the crusaders breaking into the city. The siege was followed by the Battle of Dorylaeum and the Siege of Antioch, all taking place in modern Turkey.

events.name: Crusader Siege and Defense of Antioch
events.summary: The crusaders somehow reached Antioch despite parched exhaustion and managed to take it, but were then themselves besieged by a much larger Turkish force. Alexios set out with a large army to relieve them but was turned back halfway when three deserting knights reported all was lost. Against all odds the crusaders triumphed — then considered themselves released from their oaths and gave Antioch to Bohemond, making the ancient city the heart of a crusader state.

14.summary: The crusaders somehow reached Antioch despite parched exhaustion and managed to take it, but were then themselves besieged by a much larger Turkish force. Alexios set out with a large army to relieve them but was turned back halfway when three deserting knights reported all was lost. Against all odds the crusaders triumphed — then considered themselves released from their oaths and gave Antioch to Bohemond, making the ancient city the heart of a crusader state.

events.wikipedia_extract: The siege of Antioch took place during the First Crusade in 1097 and 1098, on the Crusaders' way to Jerusalem through Syria. Two sieges took place in succession. The Crusaders sieged the city held by the Seljuk Empire from 20 October 1097 to 3 June 1098, when the Crusaders successfully took the city. A Seljuk relieving army then sieged the Crusaders for three weeks in late June. The second siege led to the Battle of Antioch in which the Crusaders defeated the Seljuks led by Kerbogha. The Crusaders then established the Principality of Antioch, ruled by Bohemond of Taranto.

events.name: Establishment of the Crusader States
events.summary: Several crusader nobles split off and, despite oaths to Alexios, carved out kingdoms from Byzantine territory. Bohemond took Antioch, and after the 'most unchristian bloodbath' capture of Jerusalem just before the turn of the century, a crusader state was established in Palestine. Alexios could live with a crusader Palestine as a useful buffer state, but crusader control of Antioch — the most important city in Asia Minor — was another matter entirely.

14.summary: Several crusader nobles split off and, despite oaths to Alexios, carved out kingdoms from Byzantine territory. Bohemond took Antioch, and after the 'most unchristian bloodbath' capture of Jerusalem just before the turn of the century, a crusader state was established in Palestine. Alexios could live with a crusader Palestine as a useful buffer state, but crusader control of Antioch — the most important city in Asia Minor — was another matter entirely.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Crusader states, or Outremer, were four Catholic polities established in the Levant region and southeastern Anatolia from 1098 to 1291. Following the principles of feudalism, the foundation for these polities was laid by the First Crusade, which was proclaimed by the Latin Church in 1095 to reclaim the Holy Land after it was lost to the 7th-century Muslim conquest. From north to south, they were: the County of Edessa (1098–1150), the Principality of Antioch (1098–1268), the County of Tripoli (1102–1289), and the Kingdom of Jerusalem (1099–1291).

14.text: before, became the heart of one of the first crusader states.

events.name: Crusader Capture of Jerusalem
events.summary: The crusading army left Antioch and battered down the walls of Jerusalem, capturing the city 'in a most unchristian bloodbath just before the turn of the century.' Brownworth notes Alexios could regard a crusader Palestine as a possibly useful buffer state, in contrast to his bitterness over losing Antioch.

14.summary: The crusading army left Antioch and battered down the walls of Jerusalem, capturing the city 'in a most unchristian bloodbath just before the turn of the century.' Brownworth notes Alexios could regard a crusader Palestine as a possibly useful buffer state, in contrast to his bitterness over losing Antioch.

events.wikipedia_extract: The siege of Jerusalem marked the successful end of the First Crusade, whose objective was the recovery of the city of Jerusalem and the Church of the Holy Sepulchre from Islamic control. The five-week siege began on 7 June 1099 and was carried out by the Christian forces of Western Europe mobilized by Pope Urban II after the Council of Clermont in 1095. The city had been out of Christian control since the Muslim conquest of the Levant in 637 and had been held for a century first by the Seljuk Turks and later by the Egyptian Fatimids. One of the root causes of the Crusades was the hindering of Christian pilgrimages to the Holy Land which began in the 4th century. A number of eyewitness accounts of the battle were recorded, including in the anonymous chronicle Gesta Francorum.

events.name: Death of Alexios I Komnenos
events.summary: Five years after his last major victory, Alexios fell seriously ill returning from a minor campaign and died on August 15th in Constantinople. Brownworth describes him as the greatest emperor since Basil II, whose forty years saved the empire and left it incomparably stronger — but whose system of personal diplomacy was too complex to survive him, so that within eighty years enemies were again at the doorstep with no Alexios to face them.

14.summary: Five years after his last major victory, Alexios fell seriously ill returning from a minor campaign and died on August 15th in Constantinople. Brownworth describes him as the greatest emperor since Basil II, whose forty years saved the empire and left it incomparably stronger — but whose system of personal diplomacy was too complex to survive him, so that within eighty years enemies were again at the doorstep with no Alexios to face them.

events.wikipedia_extract: Alexios I Komnenos, Latinized as Alexius I Comnenus, was Byzantine emperor from 1081 to 1118. After usurping the throne he was faced with a collapsing empire and constant warfare throughout his reign. Alexios was able to curb the Byzantine decline and begin the military, financial, and territorial recovery known as the Komnenian restoration. His appeals to Western Europe for help against the Seljuk Turks were the catalyst that sparked the First Crusade. Although he was not the first emperor of the Komnenian dynasty, it was during his reign that the Komnenos family came to full power and initiated a hereditary succession to the throne.

events.name: Second Crusade
events.summary: Mentioned by Brownworth as one of the Crusades whose failure was widely blamed in the West on Byzantine diplomatic duplicity, deepening the mutual contempt between East and West.

17.summary: Mentioned by Brownworth as one of the Crusades whose failure was widely blamed in the West on Byzantine diplomatic duplicity, deepening the mutual contempt between East and West.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Second Crusade (1147–1149) was the second major crusade launched from Europe. The Second Crusade was started in response to the fall of the County of Edessa in 1144 to the Seljuk forces of Zengi. The county had been founded during the First Crusade (1096–1099) by Baldwin in 1098. While it was the first Crusader state to be founded, it was also the first to fall.

events.name: Massacre of the Latins
events.summary: Andronicus solved the Venetian problem he had inherited by massacring every Italian to be found in Constantinople at the start of his reign, beginning his rule in blood.

15.summary: Andronicus solved the Venetian problem he had inherited by massacring every Italian to be found in Constantinople at the start of his reign, beginning his rule in blood.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Massacre of the Latins was a large-scale massacre of Italian-descent Catholics in Constantinople, the capital of the Byzantine Empire, by the Eastern Orthodox population of the city in April 1182.

events.name: Strangling of Alexius II
events.summary: Andronicus first forced the young Alexius II to sign his own mother's death warrant, then had himself crowned co-emperor. Two months later, claiming two emperors were bad for morale, he had Alexius strangled and threw the body into the Bosphorus.

15.summary: Andronicus first forced the young Alexius II to sign his own mother's death warrant, then had himself crowned co-emperor. Two months later, claiming two emperors were bad for morale, he had Alexius strangled and threw the body into the Bosphorus.

events.wikipedia_extract: Alexios II Komnenos, Latinized Alexius II Comnenus, was Byzantine emperor from 1180 to 1183. He ascended to the throne as a minor. For the duration of his short reign, the imperial power was de facto held by regents.

events.name: Bulgarian Revolt and Revival of the Bulgarian Empire
events.summary: Angered by Isaac II's crushing new taxes, two Bulgarian noblemen announced the revival of the Bulgarian Empire. Isaac's successive military commanders were disasters: the first rebelled and marched on Constantinople, the second let his army be wiped out in the mountains, and the third also rebelled and appeared outside the capital's walls. Isaac's own subsequent campaign was ambushed and barely escaped.

15.summary: Angered by Isaac II's crushing new taxes, two Bulgarian noblemen announced the revival of the Bulgarian Empire. Isaac's successive military commanders were disasters: the first rebelled and marched on Constantinople, the second let his army be wiped out in the mountains, and the third also rebelled and appeared outside the capital's walls. Isaac's own subsequent campaign was ambushed and barely escaped.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Second Bulgarian Empire was a medieval Bulgarian state that existed between 1185 and 1422. A successor to the First Bulgarian Empire, it reached the peak of its power under Tsars Kaloyan and Ivan Asen II before gradually being conquered by the Ottomans in the late 14th century.

events.name: Overthrow of Andronicus I
events.summary: After a fortune teller warned Andronicus that his cousin Isaac Angelos would succeed him, the emperor ordered Isaac's arrest and execution. Isaac killed one of the pursuing officers, fled to the Hagia Sophia, and rallied the surviving nobility and populace. Crowned by torchlight, Isaac's supporters then stormed the palace; Andronicus fled by boat but was captured, mutilated, and delivered to the mob, who tortured and killed him.

15.summary: After a fortune teller warned Andronicus that his cousin Isaac Angelos would succeed him, the emperor ordered Isaac's arrest and execution. Isaac killed one of the pursuing officers, fled to the Hagia Sophia, and rallied the surviving nobility and populace. Crowned by torchlight, Isaac's supporters then stormed the palace; Andronicus fled by boat but was captured, mutilated, and delivered to the mob, who tortured and killed him.

events.wikipedia_extract: Andronikos I Komnenos, Latinized as Andronicus I Comnenus, was Byzantine emperor from 1183 to 1185. A nephew of John II Komnenos, Andronikos rose to fame in the reign of his cousin Manuel I Komnenos, during which his life was marked by political failures, adventures, scandalous romances, and rivalry with the emperor.

events.name: Fall of Jerusalem
events.summary: Muslim forces declared jihad and invaded the crusader states, capturing Jerusalem. The news was greeted with horror in Europe; the Pope allegedly died of shock, and three kings—Frederick Barbarossa, Philip Augustus, and Richard the Lion-Hearted—immediately declared themselves ready to crusade.

15.summary: Muslim forces declared jihad and invaded the crusader states, capturing Jerusalem. The news was greeted with horror in Europe; the Pope allegedly died of shock, and three kings—Frederick Barbarossa, Philip Augustus, and Richard the Lion-Hearted—immediately declared themselves ready to crusade.

events.wikipedia_extract: The siege of Jerusalem lasted from 20 September to 2 October 1187, when Balian of Ibelin surrendered the city to Saladin. Earlier that summer, Saladin had defeated the kingdom's army and conquered several cities. Balian was charged with organizing a defense. The city was full of refugees but had few soldiers. Despite this fact the defenders managed to repulse several attempts by Saladin's army to take the city by storm. Balian bargained with Saladin to buy safe passage for many, and the city was peacefully surrendered with limited bloodshed. Though Jerusalem fell, it was not the end of the Kingdom of Jerusalem, as the capital shifted first to Tyre and later to Acre after the Third Crusade. Latin Christians responded in 1189 by launching the Third Crusade led by Richard the Lionheart, Philip Augustus, and Frederick Barbarossa separately. In Jerusalem, Saladin restored Muslim holy sites and generally showed tolerance towards Christians; he allowed Orthodox and Eastern Christian pilgrims to visit the holy sites freely—though Frankish pilgrims were required to pay a fee for entry. The control of Christian affairs in the city was handed over to the patriarch of Constantinople.

events.name: Third Crusade
events.summary: Launched in response to the fall of Jerusalem, the Third Crusade drew three kings and saw Frederick Barbarossa lead a large force overland through Byzantine territory, where Isaac II's mishandling of the passage earned the empire widespread condemnation in the West. Barbarossa drowned before reaching the Holy Land, his army dissolved, and the crusade failed to retake Jerusalem. The blame was laid largely on Byzantium, deepening the mutual suspicion between East and West that would contribute to the catastrophe of the Fourth Crusade.

15.summary: Launched after the fall of Jerusalem, the Third Crusade saw three kings mobilize. Isaac II disastrously mishandled Frederick Barbarossa's overland march through Byzantine territory, earning him the enmity of Europe. Barbarossa drowned before reaching the Holy Land and his army dissolved; the crusade failed to retake Jerusalem, and Byzantium was made the scapegoat in the West.

17.summary: Mentioned alongside the Second Crusade as a campaign whose failure was attributed by the West to Byzantine treachery, further poisoning East-West relations in the lead-up to the Fourth Crusade.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Third Crusade (1189–1192) was an attempt led by King Philip II of France, King Richard I of England, and Emperor Frederick Barbarossa to reconquer the Holy Land following the capture of Jerusalem by the Ayyubid sultan Saladin in 1187. For this reason, the Third Crusade is also known as the Kings' Crusade.

15.text: Perhaps had the Third Crusade been a success,
It was well known that when Richard the Lion-Hearted had left the Holy Land at the end of the Third Crusade,

events.name: Death of Frederick Barbarossa
events.summary: After crossing the mountains onto the coastal plain of Seleucia in the heat of summer, Frederick Barbarossa rode ahead of his men to swim in the Chalycadnus River and drowned. Without him, the immense army of 150,000 immediately disintegrated, ending the German contribution to the Third Crusade.

15.summary: After crossing the mountains onto the coastal plain of Seleucia in the heat of summer, Frederick Barbarossa rode ahead of his men to swim in the Chalycadnus River and drowned. Without him, the immense army of 150,000 immediately disintegrated, ending the German contribution to the Third Crusade.

events.wikipedia_extract: Frederick Barbarossa, also known as Frederick I, was the Holy Roman emperor from 1155 until his death in 1190. He was elected King of Germany in Frankfurt on 4 March 1152 and crowned in Aachen on 9 March 1152. He was crowned King of Italy on 24 April 1155 in Pavia and emperor by Pope Adrian IV on 18 June 1155 in Rome. Two years later, the term sacrum ("holy") first appeared in a document in connection with his empire. He was later formally crowned King of Burgundy, at Arles on 30 June 1178. His nickname of Barbarossa "was first used by the Florentines only in 1298 to differentiate the emperor from his grandson, Frederick II ... and was never employed in medieval Germany". In German, he was known as Kaiser Rotbart, which in English means "Emperor Redbeard". The prevalence of the Italian nickname, even in later German usage, reflects the centrality of the Italian campaigns under his reign, and "remains to this day one of the [most] powerful historical monikers."

events.name: Deposition and Blinding of Isaac II
events.summary: While Isaac II was away on a hunting expedition with his son, his older brother Alexius took the opportunity to seize the throne. When Isaac and his son returned, Alexius III threw them both into prison and had Isaac blinded. The people of Constantinople accepted the change with little more than a shrug.

15.summary: While Isaac II was away on a hunting expedition with his son, his older brother Alexius took the opportunity to seize the throne. When Isaac and his son returned, Alexius III threw them both into prison and had Isaac blinded. The people of Constantinople accepted the change with little more than a shrug.

events.wikipedia_extract: Isaac II Angelos or Angelus was Byzantine Emperor from 1185 to 1195, and co-Emperor with his son Alexios IV Angelos from 1203 to 1204. In an 1185 revolt against the Emperor Andronikos Komnenos, Isaac seized power and rose to the Byzantine throne, establishing the Angelos family as the new imperial dynasty.

events.name: Fourth Crusade
events.summary: Launched in the early thirteenth century, the Fourth Crusade was diverted from the Holy Land by Venetian doge Enrico Dandolo, who exploited the crusaders' debt to orchestrate attacks on the Christian city of Zara and then Constantinople itself, culminating in the sack of the Byzantine capital and the establishment of a Latin Empire on its ruins. The destruction was considered the greatest catastrophe in Byzantine history and marked an irreparable rupture between the Eastern and Western churches. So deep was the resulting bitterness that two centuries later, when Byzantine survival depended on union with Rome, many chose submission to the Ottoman Sultan over reconciliation with the papacy.

15.summary: Masterminded by Enrico Dandolo, the Fourth Crusade was diverted from its intended target to attack the Christian city of Zara and then Constantinople. Dandolo exploited the crusaders' inability to pay their transport debt, welcomed the fugitive Alexius Angelus, and ultimately orchestrated the sack of Constantinople—the greatest catastrophe in Byzantine history. The crusaders never reached the Holy Land and instead set up a Latin Empire on the ruins.

17.summary: Brownworth identifies the Venetian-led Fourth Crusade as the 'final divorce between East and West,' a blow so bitter that it endured in Byzantine memory for centuries. When survival in the fifteenth century required uniting with the Catholic Church, the Byzantines chose the Sultan's turban over the Pope's miter rather than forget this betrayal.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Fourth Crusade (1202–1204) was a Latin Christian armed expedition called by Pope Innocent III. The stated intent of the expedition was to recapture the Muslim-controlled city of Jerusalem, by first defeating the powerful Egyptian Ayyubid Sultanate. However, a sequence of economic and political events culminated in the Crusader army's 1202 siege of Zara and the 1204 sack of Constantinople, rather than the conquest of Egypt as originally planned. This led to the partition of the Byzantine Empire by the Crusaders and their Venetian allies, leading to a period known as the Frankokratia.

17.text: two Byzantine states that had been created during the dark years of exile after the Fourth Crusade and
But the final divorce between East and West came in the wake of the tragic Venetian led fourth crusade

events.name: Sack of Zara
events.summary: The first diversion of the Fourth Crusade, in which the crusaders attacked and sacked the Christian city of Zara on the Dalmatian coast at Dandolo's request, despite a papal letter forbidding it. The Pope was horrified and the crusaders were eventually excommunicated. It was here that Dandolo first encountered the fugitive Alexius Angelus.

15.summary: The first diversion of the Fourth Crusade, in which the crusaders attacked and sacked the Christian city of Zara on the Dalmatian coast at Dandolo's request, despite a papal letter forbidding it. The Pope was horrified and the crusaders were eventually excommunicated. It was here that Dandolo first encountered the fugitive Alexius Angelus.

events.wikipedia_extract: The siege of Zara or siege of Zadar was the first major action of the Fourth Crusade and the first attack against a Catholic city by Catholic crusaders. The crusaders had an agreement with Venice for transport across the sea, but the price far exceeded what they were able to pay. Venice set the condition that the crusaders help them capture Zadar, a constant battleground between Venice on one side and the Kingdom of Croatia and Dalmatia (Sclavonia) and Hungary on the other, whose king, Emeric, pledged himself to join the Crusade. Although some of the crusaders refused to take part in the siege, the attack on Zadar began in November 1202 despite letters from Pope Innocent III forbidding such an action and threatening excommunication. Zadar fell on 24 November and the Venetians and the crusaders sacked the city. After wintering in Zadar, the Fourth Crusade continued its campaign, which led to the siege of Constantinople.

events.name: Restoration of Isaac II
events.summary: When the Fourth Crusade fleet arrived at Constantinople and Alexius III fled, the city's ministers decided to restore the legitimate emperor to try to convince the crusaders to leave. The blind, aging Isaac was found in the dungeons and hastily re-crowned, though eight years of imprisonment had broken him mentally and physically. Alexius IV was reluctantly crowned alongside him as co-emperor.

15.summary: When the Fourth Crusade fleet arrived at Constantinople and Alexius III fled, the city's ministers decided to restore the legitimate emperor to try to convince the crusaders to leave. The blind, aging Isaac was found in the dungeons and hastily re-crowned, though eight years of imprisonment had broken him mentally and physically. Alexius IV was reluctantly crowned alongside him as co-emperor.

events.wikipedia_extract: Isaac II Angelos or Angelus was Byzantine Emperor from 1185 to 1195, and co-Emperor with his son Alexios IV Angelos from 1203 to 1204. In an 1185 revolt against the Emperor Andronikos Komnenos, Isaac seized power and rose to the Byzantine throne, establishing the Angelos family as the new imperial dynasty.

15.text: with the tyrant dead and the tall 29-year-old Isaac II on the throne,

events.name: Death of Isaac II and Alexius IV
events.summary: After Alexius Murtzuphlus seized Alexius IV and threw him into prison where his father was already waiting, both Angelli emperors met their end in the dark: Alexius IV was strangled with a bowstring, and the old, broken Isaac succumbed to poison. Brownworth observes that despite their faults, they were spared having to witness the full tragedy of what was to come.

15.summary: After Alexius Murtzuphlus seized Alexius IV and threw him into prison where his father was already waiting, both Angelli emperors met their end in the dark: Alexius IV was strangled with a bowstring, and the old, broken Isaac succumbed to poison. Brownworth observes that despite their faults, they were spared having to witness the full tragedy of what was to come.

events.wikipedia_extract: Alexios IV Angelos, Latinized as Alexius IV Angelus, was Byzantine Emperor from August 1203 to January 1204.

events.name: Sack of Constantinople
events.summary: The climax of the Fourth Crusade, in which crusaders breached the sea walls after two attempts, set fire to parts of the city, and after Murtzuphlus fled carried out three days of horrific looting, burning, rape, and murder. Brownworth describes it as an orgy of destruction that left the city unrecognizable; one Frenchman boasted so much treasure had never been gained since the creation of the world. A Latin Empire was set up on the ruins, and the city never fully recovered.

15.summary: The climax of the Fourth Crusade, in which crusaders breached the sea walls after two attempts, set fire to parts of the city, and after Murtzuphlus fled carried out three days of horrific looting, burning, rape, and murder. Brownworth describes it as an orgy of destruction that left the city unrecognizable; one Frenchman boasted so much treasure had never been gained since the creation of the world. A Latin Empire was set up on the ruins, and the city never fully recovered.

events.wikipedia_extract: The sack of Constantinople occurred in April 1204 and marked the culmination of the Fourth Crusade. Crusaders sacked and destroyed most of Constantinople, the capital of the Byzantine Empire. After the capture of the city, the Latin Empire was established and Baldwin IX of Flanders crowned emperor in Hagia Sophia.

events.name: Sack of Constantinople by the Fourth Crusade
events.summary: Mentioned only in the closing teaser as the culminating disaster of the next episode — the fall of Constantinople itself to the soldiers of the Fourth Crusade, confirming old Byzantine suspicions and representing the collapse of the system Alexios had maintained.

14.summary: Mentioned only in the closing teaser as the culminating disaster of the next episode — the fall of Constantinople itself to the soldiers of the Fourth Crusade, confirming old Byzantine suspicions and representing the collapse of the system Alexios had maintained.

events.wikipedia_extract: The sack of Constantinople occurred in April 1204 and marked the culmination of the Fourth Crusade. Crusaders sacked and destroyed most of Constantinople, the capital of the Byzantine Empire. After the capture of the city, the Latin Empire was established and Baldwin IX of Flanders crowned emperor in Hagia Sophia.

events.name: Mongol Capture of Baghdad
events.summary: Brownworth mentions the Mongol capture of Baghdad in 1258 as the event that broke Seljuk power and dissolved Asia Minor into petty kingdoms, creating the power vacuum into which Othman's Ottoman tribe streamed to dominate.

16.summary: Brownworth mentions the Mongol capture of Baghdad in 1258 as the event that broke Seljuk power and dissolved Asia Minor into petty kingdoms, creating the power vacuum into which Othman's Ottoman tribe streamed to dominate.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Siege of Baghdad, also known as the Sack of Baghdad, took place in early 1258. A large army commanded by Hulegu, a prince of the Mongol Empire, attacked the historic capital of the Abbasid Caliphate after a series of provocations from its ruler, caliph al-Musta'sim. Within a few weeks, Baghdad fell and was sacked by the Mongol army—al-Musta'sim was killed alongside hundreds of thousands of his subjects. The city's fall has traditionally been seen as marking the end of the Islamic Golden Age; in reality, its ramifications are uncertain.

16.text: The Seljuks had appeared to be the most threatening, but with the Mongol capture of Baghdad in

events.name: Recapture of Constantinople (1261)
events.summary: Brownworth opens the episode with this event: Michael VIII's Caesar found the Latin garrison absent, entered through an unlocked gate with a handful of men, and opened the city. Baldwin II fled on a Venetian ship and Michael entered in triumph through the Golden Gate, carrying an icon of Mary attributed to St. Luke, and was crowned in the Hagia Sophia. The Latin Empire had expired in a pathetic whimper after only 57 years.

16.summary: Brownworth opens the episode with this event: Michael VIII's Caesar found the Latin garrison absent, entered through an unlocked gate with a handful of men, and opened the city. Baldwin II fled on a Venetian ship and Michael entered in triumph through the Golden Gate, carrying an icon of Mary attributed to St. Luke, and was crowned in the Hagia Sophia. The Latin Empire had expired in a pathetic whimper after only 57 years.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Reconquest of Constantinople was the recapture of the city of Constantinople in 1261 AD from the Latin Empire by Nicaean forces led by the general Alexios Strategopoulos. The reconquest of the city led to the re-establishment of the Byzantine Empire under the Palaiologos dynasty after an interval of 57 years, during which Constantinople had been the capital of the Latin Empire, a crusader state installed by Latin forces of the Fourth Crusade following the sack of Constantinople in 1204.

events.name: Rise of the Ottoman Empire
events.summary: Brownworth traces the Ottoman rise under Othman, who united Turkic tribes through jihad, conquered Ephesus, and captured Bursa as his deliberate capital chosen for its proximity to Constantinople. After Othman's death, the Turks called themselves Ottoman in his honor and rapidly expanded, taking Nicaea and Nicomedia in rapid succession.

16.summary: Brownworth traces the Ottoman rise under Othman, who united Turkic tribes through jihad, conquered Ephesus, and captured Bursa as his deliberate capital chosen for its proximity to Constantinople. After Othman's death, the Turks called themselves Ottoman in his honor and rapidly expanded, taking Nicaea and Nicomedia in rapid succession.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Ottoman Empire, historically also known as the Turkish Empire, was a state that spanned much of Southeast Europe, West Asia, and North Africa from the 14th century to the early 20th century, centered in modern-day Turkey. It also controlled parts of southeastern Central Europe between the early 16th and early 18th centuries.

events.name: Ottoman Consolidation of Asia Minor
events.summary: After Othman's death, the Turks — now calling themselves Ottomans — rapidly swept through the remaining Byzantine holdings in Asia: Nicaea and Nicomedia fell in rapid succession, and soon all that was left of the empire in Asia was one or two islands and cities the Turks hadn't bothered to conquer yet.

16.summary: After Othman's death, the Turks — now calling themselves Ottomans — rapidly swept through the remaining Byzantine holdings in Asia: Nicaea and Nicomedia fell in rapid succession, and soon all that was left of the empire in Asia was one or two islands and cities the Turks hadn't bothered to conquer yet.

events.wikipedia_extract: A series of military conflicts between the Ottoman Empire and various European states took place from the Late Middle Ages up through the early 20th century. The earliest conflicts began during the Byzantine–Ottoman wars, waged in Anatolia in the late 13th century before entering Europe in the mid-14th century with the Bulgarian–Ottoman wars. The mid-15th century saw the Serbian–Ottoman wars and the Albanian-Ottoman wars. Much of this period was characterized by the Ottoman expansion into the Balkans. The Ottoman Empire made further inroads into Central Europe in the 15th and 16th centuries, culminating in the peak of Ottoman territorial claims in Europe.

events.name: Hundred Years' War
events.summary: Mentioned briefly by Brownworth as one reason Western Europe failed to come to Byzantium's aid — France and England were occupied fighting the Hundred Years' War, leaving no capacity to mount a meaningful crusade.

16.summary: Mentioned briefly by Brownworth as one reason Western Europe failed to come to Byzantium's aid — France and England were occupied fighting the Hundred Years' War, leaving no capacity to mount a meaningful crusade.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Hundred Years' War was a conflict between the kingdoms of England and France and a civil war in France during the late Middle Ages. It emerged from feudal disputes over the Duchy of Aquitaine and was triggered by a claim to the French throne made by Edward III of England. The war grew into a broader military, economic, and political struggle involving factions from across Western Europe, fuelled by emerging nationalism on both sides. The periodisation of the war typically charts it as taking place over 116 years. However, it was an intermittent conflict which was frequently interrupted by external factors, such as the Black Death, and several years of truces.

16.text: France and England were fighting the Hundred Years' War, and the rest were too united

events.name: Black Death Strikes Constantinople
events.summary: Brownworth describes the arrival of the Black Death in 1347 as an awful portent of divine wrath in the same year as John VI's impoverished coronation. The population, once the largest in Europe, shrank to less than a hundred thousand.

16.summary: Brownworth describes the arrival of the Black Death in 1347 as an awful portent of divine wrath in the same year as John VI's impoverished coronation. The population, once the largest in Europe, shrank to less than a hundred thousand.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Black Death was a plague pandemic that occurred in Europe from 1346 to 1353. It was one of the most fatal pandemics in human history; as many as 50 million people died, perhaps 50% of Europe's 14th-century population. The disease is caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis and is spread by fleas and through the air. One of the most significant events in European history, the Black Death had far-reaching population, economic, and cultural effects. It was the beginning of the second plague pandemic. The plague created religious, social, and economic upheavals, with profound effects on the course of European history.

events.name: Coronation of John VI (1347)
events.summary: Used by Brownworth as a vivid symbol of Byzantine decline: the crown jewels were obviously made of glass (the real ones pawned to Venice), and the banquet was served on humble plates of pewter and clay. The Black Death struck the city that same year.

16.summary: Used by Brownworth as a vivid symbol of Byzantine decline: the crown jewels were obviously made of glass (the real ones pawned to Venice), and the banquet was served on humble plates of pewter and clay. The Black Death struck the city that same year.

events.wikipedia_extract: John VI Kantakouzenos or Cantacuzene was a Byzantine Greek emperor and nobleman, statesman, and general. He served as grand domestic under Andronikos III Palaiologos and regent for John V Palaiologos before reigning as Byzantine emperor in his own right from 1347 to 1354. Deposed by his former ward, he was forced to retire to a monastery under the name Joasaph Christodoulos and spent the remainder of his life as a monk and historian. At age 90 or 91 at his death, he was the longest-lived of the Roman emperors. His two disastrous civil wars led to the loss of much of the remaining territory in the Balkans under Byzantine control to the Serbian and Bulgarian empires, but the most severe loss during his civil war was the loss of the Gallipoli peninsula to the Ottoman Turks, allowing the Ottomans to gain territory in Europe and setting the stage for the destruction of the Byzantine Empire a century later.

events.name: Ottoman Entry into Europe via Gallipoli
events.summary: Brownworth describes the disastrous Byzantine short-term thinking that gave the Ottomans their European foothold: a Byzantine claimant for the throne offered the Sultan the town of Gallipoli in exchange for military support in a dynastic dispute. Turkish manpower streaming into Europe then enabled the conquest of Adrianople and the surrounding of Constantinople.

16.summary: Brownworth describes the disastrous Byzantine short-term thinking that gave the Ottomans their European foothold: a Byzantine claimant for the throne offered the Sultan the town of Gallipoli in exchange for military support in a dynastic dispute. Turkish manpower streaming into Europe then enabled the conquest of Adrianople and the surrounding of Constantinople.

events.wikipedia_extract: A series of military conflicts between the Ottoman Empire and various European states took place from the Late Middle Ages up through the early 20th century. The earliest conflicts began during the Byzantine–Ottoman wars, waged in Anatolia in the late 13th century before entering Europe in the mid-14th century with the Bulgarian–Ottoman wars. The mid-15th century saw the Serbian–Ottoman wars and the Albanian-Ottoman wars. Much of this period was characterized by the Ottoman expansion into the Balkans. The Ottoman Empire made further inroads into Central Europe in the 15th and 16th centuries, culminating in the peak of Ottoman territorial claims in Europe.

events.name: Ottoman Capture of Adrianople
events.summary: By 1360 the Ottomans had conquered Adrianople, the second city of the empire, and moved their capital into Europe — Brownworth marks this as the moment Constantinople was completely surrounded in an Ottoman sea, with the emperor forced to pay tribute to avoid siege.

16.summary: By 1360 the Ottomans had conquered Adrianople, the second city of the empire, and moved their capital into Europe — Brownworth marks this as the moment Constantinople was completely surrounded in an Ottoman sea, with the emperor forced to pay tribute to avoid siege.

events.wikipedia_extract: Edirne, historically known as Orestias, Orestiada, Adrianople, or Adriana is a city in Turkey, in the northwestern part of the province of Edirne in Eastern Thrace. Situated 7 km (4.3 mi) from the Greek and 20 km (12 mi) from the Bulgarian borders, Edirne was the second capital city of the Ottoman Empire from the 1360s to 1453, before Constantinople became its capital.

events.name: Battle of the Marica River
events.summary: Brownworth mentions the battle of the Marica River as the moment when a crusader army attempting to check Ottoman expansion was completely crushed, causing most of Bulgaria to fall under Ottoman sway.

16.summary: Brownworth mentions the battle of the Marica River as the moment when a crusader army attempting to check Ottoman expansion was completely crushed, causing most of Bulgaria to fall under Ottoman sway.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Maritsa, also known as the Battle of Chernomen, was fought on 26 September 1371 near the Maritsa River, close to the village of Chernomen. The conflict pitted the Ottoman forces under Lala Şahin Pasha and Hacı İlbey against a coalition of Serbian lords, led by King Vukašin Mrnjavčević and his brother Despot Jovan Uglješa who sought to halt the Ottomans' westward advance. The battle ended in a decisive Ottoman victory in which both Serbian commanders were killed, marking the decline of Serbian power in Macedonia and paving the way for Ottoman expansion into the central Balkans.

16.text: of the Marica River, the crusader army was crushed, and most of Bulgaria fell under its

events.name: Battle of Kosovo
events.summary: Described by Brownworth as the battle fought on the 'fields of blackbirds' where Serbian power was broken after their brave continued resistance to the Ottoman advance, forcing the emperor of the Romans to become a Turkish vassal and even to take part in the siege of the last Byzantine stronghold in Asia Minor.

16.summary: Described by Brownworth as the battle fought on the 'fields of blackbirds' where Serbian power was broken after their brave continued resistance to the Ottoman advance, forcing the emperor of the Romans to become a Turkish vassal and even to take part in the siege of the last Byzantine stronghold in Asia Minor.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Kosovo took place on 15 June 1389 between an army led by the Serbian Prince Lazar Hrebeljanović and an invading army of the Ottoman Empire under the command of Sultan Murad I. It was one of the largest battles of the Late Middle Ages.

16.text: battle of Kosovo, Serbian power was broken as well.

events.name: The Renaissance
events.summary: Brownworth attributes the Renaissance directly to Byzantine scholars who fled westward after Constantinople's fall, bringing classical Greek and Roman texts that had been lost to the Dark Ages in the West. Countries emerging from their medieval sensibilities fell in love with these forgotten classics, producing what they called a 'rebirth.'

17.summary: Brownworth attributes the Renaissance directly to Byzantine scholars who fled westward after Constantinople's fall, bringing classical Greek and Roman texts that had been lost to the Dark Ages in the West. Countries emerging from their medieval sensibilities fell in love with these forgotten classics, producing what they called a 'rebirth.'

events.wikipedia_extract: The Renaissance was a European period of history and cultural movement taking place at the end of the Late Middle Ages and the beginning of the early modern era. It is variously defined as lasting from the 14th century to the 17th or, more narrowly, as covering only the 15th and 16th centuries. It was characterized by the European rediscovery and revival of the literary, philosophical, and artistic achievements of classical antiquity. Associated with great change in art, architecture, politics, literature, exploration and technology, the Renaissance was first centered in the Republic of Florence, then spread to the rest of Italy and later throughout Europe.

17.text: Preserving the remnants of knowledge in the West and the Renaissance would be a pale shadow of itself
During the Middle Ages the victory of faith and after the Renaissance the dominance of reason

events.name: Battle of Ankara
events.summary: On July 28, 1402, Tamerlane shattered the Ottoman army and reportedly kept the sultan in an iron cage for the rest of his life, rescuing Manuel II's Constantinople from imminent fall. Brownworth notes the Byzantines were too weak to exploit this respite, and Ottoman power recovered within a decade.

16.summary: On July 28, 1402, Tamerlane shattered the Ottoman army and reportedly kept the sultan in an iron cage for the rest of his life, rescuing Manuel II's Constantinople from imminent fall. Brownworth notes the Byzantines were too weak to exploit this respite, and Ottoman power recovered within a decade.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Ankara or Angora was fought on 28 July 1402, at the Çubuk plain near Ankara, between the forces of Timur and the Ottoman sultan Bayezid I. The battle was a major victory for Timur, leading to the Ottoman Interregnum.

events.name: Ottoman Siege of Constantinople (1422)
events.summary: By 1422 the Ottomans returned demanding the city's surrender. Manuel II saved Constantinople through what Brownworth calls quintessentially Byzantine diplomacy — encouraging one of the sultan's sons to start a civil war back home, diverting the Ottoman threat.

16.summary: By 1422 the Ottomans returned demanding the city's surrender. Manuel II saved Constantinople through what Brownworth calls quintessentially Byzantine diplomacy — encouraging one of the sultan's sons to start a civil war back home, diverting the Ottoman threat.

events.wikipedia_extract: In 1422, the Ottoman Empire laid siege to Constantinople, the capital of the Byzantine Empire, as a result of the Byzantine Emperor Manuel II's attempts to interfere in the succession of Ottoman Sultans, after the death of Mehmed I in 1421. This policy of the Byzantines was often used successfully in weakening their neighbours.

events.name: Council of Florence and Union of Churches
events.summary: After 14 years of negotiations, John VIII gathered intimidated Eastern bishops and signed a decree of union officially joining the Eastern and Western churches. He returned to riots in Constantinople — all who had signed publicly retracted their signatures — and Brownworth frames this failed gambit as the act that fatally undermined John's credibility with his own people.

16.summary: After 14 years of negotiations, John VIII gathered intimidated Eastern bishops and signed a decree of union officially joining the Eastern and Western churches. He returned to riots in Constantinople — all who had signed publicly retracted their signatures — and Brownworth frames this failed gambit as the act that fatally undermined John's credibility with his own people.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Council of Florence was the seventeenth ecumenical council recognized by the Catholic Church, held between 1431 and 1445. It was convened in territories under the Holy Roman Empire. Italy became a venue of a Catholic ecumenical council after a gap of about two centuries. It was convoked in Basel as the Council of Basel by Pope Martin V shortly before his death in February 1431 and took place in the context of the Hussite Wars in Bohemia and the rise of the Ottoman Empire. At stake was the greater conflict between the conciliar movement and the principle of papal supremacy.

events.name: Battle of Varna
events.summary: The crusading army that John VIII had pinned everything on — outnumbered 3-to-1 by a far superior Turkish force — was completely annihilated in a few hours. John VIII, who had sacrificed his religious credibility for this outcome, was forced to present himself to the Sultan and congratulate him on his victory, and died a broken man.

16.summary: The crusading army that John VIII had pinned everything on — outnumbered 3-to-1 by a far superior Turkish force — was completely annihilated in a few hours. John VIII, who had sacrificed his religious credibility for this outcome, was forced to present himself to the Sultan and congratulate him on his victory, and died a broken man.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Battle of Varna took place on 10 November 1444 near Varna in what is today eastern Bulgaria. The Ottoman army under Sultan Murad II defeated the Crusaders commanded by King Władysław III of Poland and Hungary, John Hunyadi and Mircea II of Wallachia. It was the final battle of the unsuccessful Crusade of Varna, a last-ditch effort to prevent further Ottoman expansion into the Balkans.

events.name: Ottoman Assault on the Hexamilion
events.summary: The Sultan sent troops to punish Constantine XI for his successful restoration of Byzantine rule over the Peloponnese. Though Constantine hoped to hold the Hexamilion wall until winter, the Turks brought large cannon that demolished it in just five days. Constantine barely escaped; the Sultan allowed his troops three days of rape and plunder, taking 60,000 prisoners and selling 7,000 women and children into slavery.

16.summary: The Sultan sent troops to punish Constantine XI for his successful restoration of Byzantine rule over the Peloponnese. Though Constantine hoped to hold the Hexamilion wall until winter, the Turks brought large cannon that demolished it in just five days. Constantine barely escaped; the Sultan allowed his troops three days of rape and plunder, taking 60,000 prisoners and selling 7,000 women and children into slavery.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Hexamilion wall was a defensive wall constructed across the Isthmus of Corinth, guarding the only land route onto the Peloponnese peninsula from mainland Greece. It was constructed between AD 408 and 450, under the reign of Theodosius II.

events.name: Construction of Rumeli Hisarı (Throat Cutter)
events.summary: Mehmed II destroyed Byzantine buildings and monasteries on the European side of the Bosphorus to build the Throat Cutter fortress — completed in an astonishing four and a half months — giving him control of Bosphorus traffic and the ability to cut off Constantinople's food supply. When Constantine sent three rounds of ambassadors to protest, the third delegation was seized and executed.

When Constantine sent three rounds of ambassadors to protest, the third delegation was seized and executed.

events.wikipedia_extract: Rumelihisarı or Boğazkesen Fortress is a medieval Ottoman fortress located in Istanbul, Turkey, on a series of hills on the European banks of the Bosphorus. The fortress also lends its name to the immediate neighborhood around it in the city's Sarıyer district.

events.name: Fall of Constantinople
events.summary: The Fall of Constantinople in 1453 brought an end to the Byzantine Empire when Ottoman Turkish forces under Mehmed II breached the city's defenses, with Emperor Constantine XI Palaiologos dying in the fighting. A garrison of roughly seven thousand men had held the Theodosian walls for over a month against a force more than ten times their size before the city fell. The event ended an unbroken imperial line stretching back to Augustus, closing over a millennium of continuous Roman succession in the East.

1.summary: Brownworth marks 1453 as the end of Byzantine history, with Emperor Constantine Palaiologos dying in the streets of Constantinople fighting the Turks. This event ends the 137-emperor unbroken line stretching from Augustus.

17.summary: Brownworth opens the conclusion by referencing the fall of Constantinople and Constantine XI's heroic last stand, noting that seven thousand men held the Theodosian walls for over a month against more than ten times their number. He laments that the West failed to memorialize this as 'the final gift of the bastion of Christianity.'

events.wikipedia_extract: The Fall of Constantinople, also known as the Conquest of Constantinople or the Siege of Constantinople, was the capture of Constantinople, the capital city of the Byzantine Empire by the Ottoman Empire. The city was captured on 29 May 1453 as part of the culmination of a 53-day siege which had begun on 6 April.

17.text: Stubbornly held out for seven years after the fall of Constantinople

events.name: Fall of Constantinople
events.summary: The central event of the episode: a 53-day siege beginning April 6, 1453, culminating on May 29. Mehmed's 150,000 troops and 130 ships faced 7,000 defenders. The decisive breach came when Giustiniani was wounded and his Genoese troops abandoned the walls; Constantine threw off his imperial regalia and disappeared fighting into the breach. Brownworth frames the fall as the end not just of Byzantium but of the Middle Ages themselves.

16.summary: The central event of the episode: a 53-day siege beginning April 6, 1453, culminating on May 29. Mehmed's 150,000 troops and 130 ships faced 7,000 defenders. The decisive breach came when Giustiniani was wounded and his Genoese troops abandoned the walls; Constantine threw off his imperial regalia and disappeared fighting into the breach. Brownworth frames the fall as the end not just of Byzantium but of the Middle Ages themselves.

events.wikipedia_extract: The Fall of Constantinople, also known as the Conquest of Constantinople or the Siege of Constantinople, was the capture of Constantinople, the capital city of the Byzantine Empire by the Ottoman Empire. The city was captured on 29 May 1453 as part of the culmination of a 53-day siege which had begun on 6 April.

events.name: Ottoman Ships Transported Overland into the Imperial Harbor
events.summary: In one of the episode's most dramatic moments, Mehmed transported 70 ships overland, bypassing the Genoese colony of Galata and the harbor chain altogether, dropping his fleet silently into the Imperial Harbor. Constantine was stunned; the treacherous Genoese of Galata had not even warned him. This forced the already-stretched defenders to cover an additional 3.5 miles of sea wall.

events.name: Fall of Trebizond
events.summary: Brownworth specifies the date of 15 August 1461 as the moment Trebizond fell and with it the last Byzantine throne, noting that two Byzantine successor states held out for seven years after Constantinople's fall before this final extinction.

17.summary: Brownworth specifies the date of 15 August 1461 as the moment Trebizond fell and with it the last Byzantine throne, noting that two Byzantine successor states held out for seven years after Constantinople's fall before this final extinction.

events.wikipedia_extract: The siege of Trebizond was the successful siege of the city of Trebizond, capital of the Empire of Trebizond, by the Ottomans under Sultan Mehmed II, which ended on 15 August 1461. The siege culminated a lengthy campaign on the Ottoman side, which involved coordinated but independent manoeuvres by a large army and navy. The Trapezuntine defenders had relied on a network of alliances, which would provide them with support and a workforce when the Ottomans began their siege. Still, it failed when Emperor David Megas Komnenos most needed it.

events.name: Dias Rounds the Cape of Good Hope
events.summary: Brownworth cites Dias's rounding of the Cape of Good Hope just 35 years after Constantinople's fall as direct evidence that Ottoman closure of eastern trade routes drove European maritime exploration, inaugurating the Age of Exploration.

16.summary: Brownworth cites Dias's rounding of the Cape of Good Hope just 35 years after Constantinople's fall as direct evidence that Ottoman closure of eastern trade routes drove European maritime exploration, inaugurating the Age of Exploration.

events.wikipedia_extract: Bartolomeu Dias was a Portuguese mariner and explorer. In February 1488, he became the first European navigator to round the southern tip of Africa and to demonstrate that the most effective southward route for ships is in the open ocean, well to the west of the African coast. His discoveries were later used by Vasco da Gama to establish a sea route between Europe and Asia.

events.name: Columbus Discovers America
events.summary: Brownworth closes the episode by noting that just four years after Dias rounded the Cape, Columbus discovered America — framing both voyages as consequences of the fall of Constantinople and the Ottoman closure of eastern routes, thus placing the Byzantine endgame at the hinge of the Age of Exploration.

16.summary: Brownworth closes the episode by noting that just four years after Dias rounded the Cape, Columbus discovered America — framing both voyages as consequences of the fall of Constantinople and the Ottoman closure of eastern routes, thus placing the Byzantine endgame at the hinge of the Age of Exploration.

events.wikipedia_extract:
Between 1492 and 1504, the Italian explorer and navigator Christopher Columbus
led four Spanish transatlantic maritime expeditions in the name of the Catholic Monarchs to the Americas. These voyages led to Europeans learning about the New World. This was an early breakthrough in the period known in Europe as the Age of Exploration, which saw the colonization of the Americas, a related biological exchange, and trans-Atlantic trade. These events, the effects and consequences of which persist to the present, are often cited as the beginning of the modern era.
